A lock indicator on the housing is fine as static detail.

straight-beam-lamination.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Base art: left two-thirds is a steel plate cross-section with a 0-degree contact probe on the top surface and a thin couplant line under it; right third is an A-scan inset panel (baseline grid, initial-pulse spike pinned at left edge). Animatable reveals, timed to the beat-2 narration 'Straight beam sends a longitudinal wave straight down. Watch the back wall echo — a lamination adds a new echo and steals that back wall.' (~9.7s total): sblBeam (0–3.4s, 'sends a longitudinal wave straight down') — vertical beam column from probe face to the back wall with downward pulse arrows, while a tall back-wall echo labeled BW rises on the A-scan at the thickness position; sblWitness (3.4–5.2s, 'watch the back wall echo') — pulsing highlight ring/glow on the BW echo; sblLam (5.2–7.6s, 'a lamination adds a new echo') — thin flat horizontal lamination plane appears at mid-wall intercepting the beam, and a new echo labeled LAM rises at exactly half the BW screen distance; sblDrop (7.6–9.7s, 'and steals that back wall') — BW echo collapses to a dim stub while LAM stays tall, and the beam below the lamination fades out. Spec note overlay 'NEW ECHO + LOST BACK WALL = LAMINATION' fires at 8.6s, so the final state must read clearly with both the tall LAM echo and the dead BW stub visible.

angle-beam-path.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. NOTE: ut1-1-2 also references this asset (its beat narrates 'skip equals twice the thickness times tan theta' with note '60° ON 25 MM PLATE · SKIP = 86.6 MM') — one drawing must serve both lessons, so keep all numeric angle values OUT of the base art: label the refracted angle generically as θ and let each lesson's note overlay supply numbers. Base art: side cross-section of plate (thickness T dimensioned with ticks at the right edge), angle-beam probe on an acrylic wedge at upper left with its exit (index) point marked, subdued single-V butt weld outline at the right end of the plate as the scan target. Animatable reveals, timed to the ut1-6-1 beat-3 narration 'On a wedge, a shear wave travels the steel in a vee. Thickness times tangent gives the half skip. Mark your scan zone.' (~9.2s total): abpShear (0–2.4s, 'on a wedge, a shear wave') — refracted shear-wave beam line leaves the exit point into the steel, with the θ arc drawn from the surface normal; abpVee (2.4–4.6s, 'travels the steel in a vee') — beam extends to the back wall, bounce point flares, second leg climbs back to the top surface tracing the full V; abpHalfSkip (4.6–7.4s, 'thickness times tangent gives the half skip') — horizontal dimension arrow along the top surface from the exit point to the point directly above the bottom bounce, labeled '½ SKIP = T·TAN θ', with a thin projection line down to the bounce point; abpZone (7.4–9.2s, 'mark your scan zone') — grease-pencil-style hatched scan-zone band on the top surface from near the weld toe back past the full-skip return point. Also include abpFullSkip (unused in ut1-6-1; for ut1-1-2's beat) — longer surface dimension arrow from exit point to the second-leg surface return, labeled 'SKIP = 2T·TAN θ'. Spec note overlay 'HALF-SKIP = T × TAN θ' fires at 8.2s in ut1-6-1; keep the lower-center area clear for note text.
---
SHARED CANONICAL ASSET (also referenced by ut1-1-2 and ut1-6-1 — merge briefs; keep markers compatible with ut1-6-1's 'HALF-SKIP = T × TAN θ' note). 600x340 holographic navy/cyan side view: plate of thickness T with a single-V butt weld silhouette at the right end, angle-beam probe on a refracting wedge at the left, exit/index point marked on the surface, refracted-angle arc θ from the vertical at the index point. Reveals for ut2-4-1, timed to ~8.5 s narration ('Coverage is trigonometry. At the half skip, leg one hits the root; at the full skip, leg two returns to the cap.'): abProbe (~0.4s) emphasizes probe, wedge, index point and θ arc; abLeg1 (~2.4s) draws beam centerline leg 1 descending at θ to the bottom surface with the arrowhead landing at the weld ROOT, label LEG 1, plus a half-skip surface tick at T·tanθ; abLeg2 (~5.0s) draws reflected leg 2 rising to the top surface arriving at the weld CAP, label LEG 2; abSkip (~6.9s) draws a surface bracket from index point to the leg-2 arrival point labeled FULL SKIP = 2T·TANθ — syncs with the beat note at 7.8s.
---
CANONICAL ASSET (wedge + V-path skip legs) — will be reused by later angle-beam lessons (ut1-6-1, ut1-9-3). Cross-section of a 25 mm plate spanning the frame width; angle-beam wedge probe on the top surface at left. At the beam exit point: dashed vertical normal line with an angle arc θ measured FROM THE VERTICAL NORMAL (never from the surface) to the refracted beam. V-path: first leg down to the back wall, reflecting up as a second leg returning to the top surface — draw proportions roughly true for 60° (each leg advances ~1.73x the thickness horizontally, so the V is wide). Labels: angle chip 'θ = 60°' at the arc; thickness bracket on the right edge 'T = 25 MM'; skip-distance bracket along the top surface from exit point to the beam's return 'SKIP = 86.6 MM'; formula panel 'SKIP = 2 × T × TAN θ'. Reveals timed to ut1-1-2 beat 4 narration 'Angle beams are right triangles: skip equals twice the thickness times tan theta. Sixty degrees on 25 millimeter plate skips 86.6 millimeters.' (~8s): abpPlate (fade 0.3s) plate cross-section + wedge probe; abpNormal (fade 0.9s) dashed normal + θ arc on 'right triangles'; abpVee (draw 1.3s) V-path draws down-leg then up-leg, making the right-triangle geometry visible; abpFormula (fade 2.6s) formula panel as 'skip equals twice the thickness times tan theta' is spoken; abpAngle (ping+fade 4.6s) θ = 60° chip on 'Sixty degrees'; abpThick (fade 5.6s) T = 25 MM bracket on '25 millimeter plate'; abpSkip (draw 6.6s) skip bracket + 86.6 MM label on 'skips 86.6 millimeters'.

weld-anatomy.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of a finished single-V butt groove weld joining two plates, faint multi-pass bead boundaries visible inside the nugget. Static base: the two plate halves, V-groove weld between them, slight root and cap reinforcement protruding. Animatable reveals timed to a ~9 s narration ('Cap and toes on top, root below, fusion lines where weld meets plate, and around everything, the heat-affected zone. Examine both — weld plus zone.'): waCap (~0.5s) highlights the cap/face reinforcement crown and dot-marks both toes, labels CAP and TOE; waRoot (~2.2s) highlights the root bead on the opposite surface, label ROOT; waFusion (~3.8s) stroke-glows both weld-to-base-metal fusion lines, label FUSION LINE; waHaz (~5.6s) reveals gradient bands a few millimeters wide flanking the fusion lines through the full thickness, label HAZ — NEVER MELTED; waVolume (~7.6s) draws a dashed bracket spanning the weld width plus the HAZ band on each side, label EXAM VOLUME — syncs with the beat note 'EXAM VOLUME = WELD + HAZ' appearing at 8.2s.

film-density-window.brief: Title-beat backdrop panel, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A radiographic film strip lying on a glowing illuminator seen face-on, with four square density patches stepping darker left-to-right labeled D=1, D=2, D=3, D=4, and small transmission tags under each: 10%, 1%, 0.1%, 0.01% (each density step = another factor of ten of viewing light transmitted). A cyan bracket spans the D~2 to D=4 region tagged 'CODE WINDOW' (keep it generic ~2-4: the lesson gives 1.8-4.0 for X-ray and 2.0-4.0 for gamma, so do NOT print either specific floor), plus a small densitometer readout chip showing a needle/value of 2.5 sitting inside the window. Reveals vs narration ('Every radiograph aims at one number: film density inside the code window. Exposure arithmetic is how you hit it on the first shot.', ~8.5s title beat): fdwFilmStrip at 0.5s = illuminator glow + film strip + the four darkening patches (with 'aims at one number'); fdwTransmit at 1.8s = the 10% -> 0.01% transmission tags fade in left-to-right (with 'film density'); fdwWindow at 3.2s = code-window bracket + 'CODE WINDOW' tag + densitometer chip pulse, syncing with 'inside the code window', then holds with a subtle loop glow for the rest of the beat. Panel sits behind the EXPOSURE MATH title with panelLabel 'TARGET // DENSITY WINDOW'.

xray-exposure-chart.brief: Diagram-beat asset, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A semi-log X-ray exposure chart for steel: bottom axis 'STEEL THICKNESS (IN.)' 0 to 2.0; left axis 'EXPOSURE (mA-min)' on a logarithmic scale (ticks 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100); a family of five roughly straight kilovoltage lines fanning up-right labeled 140, 160, 180, 200, 220 kV, with lower-kV lines steeper (soft radiation attenuates faster per inch). Worked trace uses the lesson's figure-a numbers: 0.75 in. at 180 kV reads ~16 mA-min. Reveals vs narration ('One machine, one chart. Enter at the part thickness, rise to your kilovoltage line, read across to the exposure. Valid only for the printed conditions.', ~9s beat, on-screen note fires at 8.2s): xecGrid at 0.6s = axes, semi-log gridlines, axis labels (with 'One machine, one chart'); xecKvFan at 1.4s = the five kV lines draw in with labels; xecTraceUp at 3.4s = dashed cyan vertical trace entering the bottom axis at 0.75 in. and rising (with 'enter at the part thickness'); xecHitKv at 5.0s = the 180 kV line glows and a node dot lands at the intersection (with 'rise to your kilovoltage line'); xecTraceAcross at 6.2s = dashed horizontal trace runs left to the exposure axis with a value chip '~16 mA-min' (with 'read across to the exposure'); xecConditions at 7.4s = small printed-conditions stamp card slides into the lower-right reading 'FILM SYSTEM - DENSITY - SFD - SCREENS - PROCESSING' (with 'valid only for the printed conditions'), arriving just before the 8.2s caption note 'VALID ONLY FOR ITS PRINTED CONDITIONS'.

film-cross-section.brief: Title panel, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Edge-on cross-section slab of industrial X-ray film at a slight isometric angle: thin clear polyester base at center (label: POLYESTER BASE - 0.007 IN), emulsion layers coated on BOTH faces, each speckled with tiny silver bromide grains; small magnifier inset zooms one grain showing a darker fleck labeled SENSITIVITY SPECK. Reveals (beat narration is 19 words, ~6 s): fcsLayers at 0.3s — base plus both emulsion layers draw in with labels, synced to 'born inside the emulsion'; fcsGrains at ~2.3s — grain field glints across both emulsion layers, synced to 'billions of silver bromide grains'; fcsRay at ~4.3s — a faint radiation ray streaks through the slab and two or three grains pulse softly cyan (invisible seeds), synced to 'radiation write an invisible image'. After reveals, ambient loopable shimmer (title panel keeps playing).

latent-image-formation.brief: Technical diagram, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, of the Gurney-Mott sequence inside ONE silver bromide grain. Large rounded crystal drawn as an ion lattice grid; a small bright nub on its edge is the sensitivity speck. Reveals (beat narration 23 words, ~7.7 s; note overlay lands at 7.2s): lifGrain at 0.3s — crystal plus speck labeled SENSITIVITY SPECK, synced to 'Radiation frees electrons'; lifElectron at ~1.4s — a radiation bolt deposits energy, a particle marked with a minus sign drifts through the lattice and locks into the speck, which glows (negative charge), synced to 'a sensitivity speck traps one'; lifIon at ~3.6s — a silver ion marked with a plus sign drifts through the lattice to the charged speck, charge neutralizes, and one solid silver-atom dot remains at the speck, synced to 'attracts a silver ion — one silver atom'; lifCluster at ~5.5s — the electron-then-ion cycle repeats quickly, silver dots accumulate to a 4-5 atom cluster and the grain receives a cyan DEVELOPABLE tick, synced to 'Repeat: a few atoms make the grain developable'. Keep all labels qualitative (no 3-6 numeral on screen; the note overlay says A FEW).

inherent-unsharpness.brief: Technical diagram, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: why electron range smears a sharp radiation edge (inherent unsharpness Ui — distinct from the geometric-unsharpness asset). Layout: left, an emulsion strip in cross-section beneath a sharp absorber edge so half the field is shadow; right/below, a density-profile graph of that edge. Reveals (beat narration 22 words, ~7.3 s; note overlay lands at 6.9s): iusPhoton at ~0.8s — an incoming photon arrow is absorbed in the emulsion (or adjacent lead screen) and hands off to a fast electron (photon track ends, electron track begins), synced to 'electrons, not photons, expose film'; iusTrack at ~2.5s — the electron track wiggles a short range through the emulsion, lighting grains beyond the dashed line marking the true geometric edge, synced to 'they travel before stopping'; iusRamp at ~4.2s — on the graph, an ideal step edge morphs into a sloped density ramp with the smear width tagged Ui, synced to 'every edge smears — inherent unsharpness'; iusEnergy at ~5.8s — a small horizontal arrow labeled LOW ENERGY to HIGH ENERGY with the Ui smear band visibly widening along it, synced to 'grows with radiation energy'. Keep the energy reveal qualitative — no kV or mm values on screen, since narration speaks none.

field-vs-radius.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Field strength vs radius for current-carrying parts (mt2-2-1 fig-a concept). LEFT: cross-section of a solid bar with a current-direction dot at the centerline, B-vs-radius curve plotted over it. RIGHT: hollow tube shot directly (current dots in the wall), bore interior empty; later a central conductor threads the bore. Beat narration (~9.3s): 'In a solid bar, the field is zero at the center, strongest at the surface. A hollow bore sees almost nothing: run a central conductor.' Reveals: fvrBarCurve (draw, 0.5s) solid-bar curve rises from zero at centerline roughly linearly to a sharp peak exactly at the bar surface, then decays ~1/r outside — syncs with 'zero at the center'; fvrSurfacePeak (fade, 3.2s) glow marker + tag MAX AT SURFACE on the peak — syncs with 'strongest at the surface'; fvrHollowDead (fade, 5.2s) hollow tube appears with bore field flatlined, tag ID ≈ 0 — syncs with 'a hollow bore sees almost nothing'; fvrCentralFix (fade, 7.6s) central conductor bar drops through the bore and a new curve re-plots with maximum at the ID wall, tag ID STRONGEST — syncs with 'run a central conductor'. The renderer overlays note BORE FIELD ≈ ZERO → CENTRAL CONDUCTOR at 8.5s, so keep the lower band clear.

amperage-formula.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Low fill-factor coil shot with the 45,000/(L/D) rule worked as a live ledger. A horizontal part inside a 5-turn coil (turns drawn as hoops, longitudinal flux arrows along the part axis, N/S poles hinted at part ends), dimension callouts L = 16 IN and D = 2 IN; calculation chips build at right. Beat narration (~9.5s): 'Sixteen inches long, two inches across: L over D is eight. Forty-five thousand over eight: about fifty-six hundred ampere-turns. Five turns: roughly eleven hundred amperes.' Reveals: amfCoilPart (fade, 0.5s) part + 5-turn coil + L/D dimension callouts — syncs with 'sixteen inches long, two inches across'; amfRatio (fade, 2.6s) chip L/D = 16 ÷ 2 = 8 — syncs with 'L over D is eight'; amfFormula (fade, 4.3s) anchor line NI = 45,000 ÷ (L/D); amfResult (fade, 5.6s) line 45,000 ÷ 8 ≈ 5,625 AMPERE-TURNS — syncs with 'about fifty-six hundred ampere-turns'; amfMeter (fade, 7.8s) ammeter dial sweeps to readout ÷ 5 TURNS ≈ 1,125 A — syncs with 'five turns: roughly eleven hundred amperes'. Renderer note NI = 45,000 ÷ (L/D) AMPERE-TURNS lands at 8.8s — keep lower band clear.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan, split composition: rules of thumb on the left, field verification on the right. Left half: a formula card tagged STARTING POINT with the coil rule 'NI = 45,000 / (L/D)' set large, and two fine-print companion rules beneath: 'HEAD SHOT: 300-800 A PER IN DIA' and 'PRODS: 90-125 A PER IN SPACING'. Right half: a shaft segment with a small square QQI shim taped flaw-side-down on its surface, plus a Hall-effect gaussmeter dial whose scale highlights a 30-60 G band labeled COMMON TARGET. Animatable reveals vs the narration 'Question five. Formulas are starting points; proof finishes the answer. The Q Q I shim lights up, about thirty to sixty gauss on the meter.': amfFormula = formula card draws in with its STARTING POINT tag (at 'Formulas are starting points'); amfShim = the QQI's circle-and-cross artificial flaw blooms with a bright fluorescent particle indication (at 'the Q Q I shim lights up'); amfGauss = gaussmeter needle sweeps into the highlighted 30-60 G band with digital readout 'approx 45 G TANGENTIAL' (at 'thirty to sixty gauss on the meter'). End state: left card tagged START, shim and meter jointly tagged PROOF.

mt-bench-unit.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Title-panel loop (~9s): wet horizontal MT bench in side view — machined steel shaft clamped between copper contact heads (headstock/tailstock), a multi-turn coil ring around the part, particle-bath nozzle, and a prominent ammeter. Reveals: mbuBench (fade, 0.5s) bench frame + part between contact heads; mbuClamp (fade, 2.0s) contact heads close onto the part ends; mbuAmps (fade, 4.5s) ammeter needle sweeps up and the readout glows — this is the shot; mbuShot (draw, 6.5s) circular flux rings bloom around the part while the bath flows. NOTE: this canonical asset is also referenced by media-src/specs/mt1-5-3.json — dedupe with any brief already issued from that spec; one drawing serves both.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan wet horizontal MT bench unit, side elevation. Static base: long bed on legs with rails, headstock at left (contact pads, cable runs, small ammeter + shot-timer readout), movable tailstock at right, recirculating particle-bath tray with nozzle below the bed, hinted UV-A lamp above the work zone. Beat narration (23 words, ~9 s): 'The production standard: the part clamps in for a head shot, a circular field, then a coil slides over it for longitudinal magnetization.' Required animatable reveals, in order: mbuPart (~1.6 s, on 'the part clamps in') — a steel shaft appears clamped between headstock and tailstock, contact pads closing; mbuCircular (~3.5 s, on 'a head shot, a circular field') — current arrows run lengthwise through the shaft and concentric circular flux rings wrap its circumference, small mono tag 'HEAD SHOT · CIRCULAR'; mbuCoil (~6.5 s, on 'a coil slides over it for longitudinal magnetization') — a multi-turn coil slides along the rails to surround the shaft, longitudinal field arrows run along the shaft axis, small mono tag 'COIL · LONGITUDINAL'. Keep the two field renderings visually distinct (rings vs axial arrows) and leave both visible at the end: the renderer's note 'HEAD SHOT → CIRCULAR · COIL → LONGITUDINAL' lands at 8.0 s over the complete picture.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan wet horizontal bench unit (stationary MT machine), side view: headstock and tailstock contact plates clamping a dark steel shaft, coil mid-bed, ammeter face with needle, particle-bath hose and foot switch hinted. NOTE: this canonical asset was first coined by spec mt1-5-3 — if a brief already exists from that lesson, keep that design and ignore this one; mt2-2-2 only uses it as a title-side panel (label 'BENCH SHOT // SAME AMPS'), so composition must stay readable at small panel scale. If drawing fresh, animatable reveals vs mt2-2-2's ~8.1 s title narration: mbuBench at 0.5 s (machine body draws); mbuPart at 1.5 s (clamped shaft fades in); mbuShot at 2.6 s (ammeter needle kicks + current pulse glow along the part, landing on narration 'same shot'); mbuRings at 4.5 s (faint circular flux rings appear around the shaft, lingering through 'Heat and hardness decide how the steel answers').

wave-equation.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Left two-thirds: contact probe coupled to a steel block in cross-section with a longitudinal wave train crossing it; a dimension bracket marks one full wavelength. Upper right: the formula 'lambda = v / f' in large display type with small labels (v = material+mode, f = probe). Lower right: worked readout '5.92 mm/us / 2.25 MHz = 2.63 mm'. Near the wave, a half-wavelength bracket labeled 'lambda/2 ~ 1.3 mm' beside a tiny flaw dot drawn ghosted/fading to show it is below the reliable-detection floor. Animatable reveal ids vs narration ('Wavelength equals velocity divided by frequency. In steel at 2.25 megahertz, that's 5.92 over 2.25, about 2.63 millimeters. Reflectors under half a wavelength are unreliable.', ~9.3s): wqFormula = lambda = v / f formula, visible from ~0.5s during 'Wavelength equals velocity divided by frequency'; wqWave = wave train + full-wavelength bracket drawing through the steel at ~2.5-4.5s during 'In steel at 2.25 megahertz'; wqValues = the 5.92 / 2.25 = 2.63 mm readout at ~5s on 'about 2.63 millimeters'; wqFloor = lambda/2 bracket + ghosted sub-size flaw dot at ~7s on 'Reflectors under half a wavelength are unreliable' (player note chip lands at 8.2s, keep bottom-center clear for it).
---
SHARED ASSET coined by ut1-2-2 — merge with that request; keep the drawing reusable for both lessons (recommend the lambda/2 sensitivity rule stay OUT of the asset: ut1-2-2 carries it as its spec-level note). Straight-beam probe coupled to the top of a steel block cross-section; cyan sinusoid wave train traveling down into the block; bracket spanning exactly one full cycle labeled λ; formula panel 'λ = V ÷ F'; material tag on the block 'STEEL · V = 5.92 MM/µS'; probe tag 'PROBE · F = 2.25 MHZ'; worked-example chip '2.25 MHZ → λ = 2.63 MM' (this exact example appears in BOTH lessons' narration — do not change the numbers). Reveals timed to ut1-1-2 beat 2 narration 'Velocity equals frequency times wavelength. The material sets velocity, the probe sets frequency, so wavelength adjusts: steel at 2.25 megahertz gives 2.63 millimeters.' (~8.3s): weqProbe (fade 0.4s) probe + steel block; weqWave (draw 1.0s) wave train draws downward; weqLambda (ping+fade 1.6s) λ bracket on one cycle; weqFormula (fade 2.2s) 'λ = V ÷ F' panel as the relationship is spoken; weqVel (fade 2.8s) steel velocity tag on 'the material sets velocity'; weqFreq (fade 4.0s) probe frequency tag on 'the probe sets frequency'; weqExample (fade 6.6s) worked-example chip on 'gives 2.63 millimeters'. Timing also serves ut1-2-2's beat (formula spoken first, 2.63 mm example lands ~5-7s).

attenuation-grains.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Contact probe at left drives a sound beam rightward through a coarse-grained metal block drawn with visible polygonal grain boundaries; the beam visibly thins/fades with depth. Upper-right inset: mini A-scan with a hashy low-level baseline (grass) and a string of back echoes shrinking left to right. Animatable reveal ids vs narration ('Attenuation is signal lost along the path. Absorption turns vibration into heat. Scattering bounces energy off grain boundaries, spraying back as grass.', ~8.2s): agBeam = probe + main beam entering and fading with depth, from ~0.5s during 'Attenuation is signal lost along the path'; agHeat = soft heat shimmer/glow wisps along the beam path at ~3s on 'Absorption turns vibration into heat'; agScatter = grain boundaries highlight with small arrows deflecting energy off-beam in random directions at ~4.5-6s on 'Scattering bounces energy off grain boundaries'; agGrass = a few scatter arrows returning toward the probe plus the grass hash and shrinking echo string lighting up on the inset A-scan at ~6.3s on 'spraying back as grass' (player note chip lands at 7.2s, keep bottom-center clear).

survey-meter.brief:
SHARED ASSET — also referenced as the title panel of rt1-5-2 ('SURVEY // RATE RIGHT NOW'); design once, ambient scene-setting (no dense teaching labels). Handheld radiography survey meter, ion-chamber style: rounded rectangular case with top carry handle, large analog face with an arc scale ticked and unit-labeled mR/h (no specific numbers emphasized), range-selector knob, ON toggle. Composition centered, 600x340. Reveals vs rt2-6-2 beat-1 narration (~9.5s: 'You can't feel radiation. Every fact comes from an instrument — and at Level Two, choosing it is your call. Name the question first.'): svmCase fade ~0.6s (case + handle outline), svmFace draw ~1.4s (scale arc + ticks, lands on 'every fact comes from an instrument'), svmNeedle ~2.2s (needle sweeps up from zero and settles mid-scale; make this the scan/loop element so it keeps breathing through the rest of the beat), svmKnob fade ~3.0s (range selector glow), svmTag fade ~3.8s (small chip 'RATE — mR/h'). Same timings work for rt1-5-2's title beat.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan TITLE PANEL (ambient, loops, same role as pipe-scan). Scene: right foreground, handheld ion-chamber survey meter in 3/4 profile - boxy body, carry handle, large analog arc dial with 0-200 scale ticks and 'mR/h' label, range knob, small check-source disc on the housing. Left background, low to the ground: compact gamma projector silhouette (rounded shield block, guide tube snaking off-frame). Between them, 3-4 faint concentric field arcs radiate from the projector and thin as they cross toward the meter. Beat-1 narration (~8.6s): 'You can stand in a lethal field and feel nothing. Every fact about the radiation comes from an instrument. Meet your only senses.' Required reveals: svmArcs (fade ~0.6s) field arcs pulse on, then keep a slow outward-drift loop under 'stand in a lethal field and feel nothing'; svmNeedle (sweep ~1.4s) dial needle sweeps from zero to mid-scale and settles with a subtle wobble loop; svmReadout (ping ~5.0s) small HUD chip above the meter pings on with a digital mid-scale rate readout (e.g. '12 mR/h') at 'Every fact about the radiation comes from an instrument'. Keep lower edge clear for panelLabel 'SURVEY // RATE RIGHT NOW'.

instrument-range-map.brief: Operating-range map of the three survey-instrument families (renders lesson fig-a). Horizontal log dose-rate axis across the lower third, decade ticks from a few µR/h up to tens of R/h, with a small callout tick at 2 mR/h. Three horizontal range bars stacked above the axis, top to bottom in NARRATION ORDER. Beat-3 narration (~9.6s): 'Match the range. Scintillators prove areas clean but overload. G M tubes saturate in strong fields. The ion chamber stays linear — your workhorse.' Reveals: irmAxis draw ~0.5s (axis + ticks, during 'Match the range'); irmScint draw ~1.6s (top bar, far-left span ~µR/h-class up to a few mR/h, tag 'SCINTILLATOR — PROVES CLEAN'); irmScintX fade ~3.0s (warning glyph just right of the scintillator bar's end, tag 'OVERLOADS'); irmGm draw ~4.0s (middle bar, low-to-mid span — NO numeric endpoints, the lesson gives none; tag 'GM — FAST, RUGGED'); irmGmSat fade ~5.2s (hatched fade-out segment at the GM bar's right end, tag 'SATURATES'); irmIon draw ~6.6s (bottom bar, widest span from 2 mR/h to tens of R/h, brightest cyan, tag 'ION CHAMBER'); irmIonLin fade ~7.8s ('LINEAR' label along the ion bar, lands on 'stays linear — your workhorse'). Scan element irmScanG, sweep ~4.4s. Spec note chip appears at 8.6s, so keep the lower-right corner clear.

retrieval-train.brief: Side elevation of a gamma retrieval train, left to right (renders lesson fig-b): hand crank with cable spool → drive cable → exposure device (compact projector block, ghosted S-tube hint) → outlet connector → guide tube sweeping in one wide gentle bend → collimator at the far end aimed at a weld joint. Beat-4 narration (~9.7s): 'On the device side, inspect the retrieval train daily: drive cable, connectors, guide tube, collimator. A kink or mismatched connector can strand the source.' Reveals — highlight each piece in NARRATION ORDER: rtrBase draw ~0.6s (full train line art; also the scan element, sweep ~4.5s); rtrCable fade ~2.8s (glow + magnifier chip on the drive cable, micro-label 'NO KINKS OR BROKEN STRANDS'); rtrConn fade ~3.6s (connector glow, micro-label 'WEAR LIMITS — NEVER MIX MAKERS'); rtrTube fade ~4.4s (guide-tube glow, micro-label 'NO CRUSHED SECTIONS, WIDE BENDS'); rtrColl fade ~5.2s (collimator glow, micro-label 'SUPPORTED'); rtrHazard fade ~6.6s (inset detail: kinked cable section + mismatched connector pair with warning triangle and a source capsule stalled partway down the guide tube). Spec note chip appears at 8.4s lower-right — keep that corner clear.

film-density-curve.brief:
Characteristic (H&D) curve of industrial X-ray film, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Axes: vertical FILM DENSITY (0-4), horizontal LOG RELATIVE EXPOSURE. One curve: flat low floor at D~0.25, shallow toe, then a working region that steepens CONTINUOUSLY up through D~4 (industrial film has no plateau; only a faint, unlabeled roll-over hint at the extreme top-right, de-emphasized). Beat narration (~8.5s): 'Left to right: base plus fog, the toe where flaws hide, then a working region that keeps steepening. More density, more contrast.' Reveals: fdcAxes (draw ~0.5s) axes + labels + ghost curve; fdcBaseFog (fade ~1.6s, on 'base plus fog') dashed horizontal floor at D~0.25 tagged BASE + FOG; fdcToe (fade ~3.2s, on 'the toe where flaws hide') toe segment brightens with shaded low-slope zone tagged TOE - FLAWS HIDE HERE; fdcWorking (draw ~5.0s, on 'keeps steepening') steepening segment sweeps bright left-to-right with two small tangent wedges showing slope growing as density rises; fdcContrast (fade ~7.0s, on 'More density, more contrast') paired demo: the same small log-exposure step at D 1.5 vs D 3.0 projecting a small vs much larger density step onto the vertical axis. On-screen note 'MORE DENSITY = MORE CONTRAST' fires at 7.6 from the spec, so fdcContrast must land just before it.
---
H&D (characteristic) curve panel, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan house style. Static base: axis frame with X axis labeled LOG RELATIVE EXPOSURE and Y axis labeled DENSITY ticked 0-4. Animatable reveals, timed against the beat narration 'Codes window density, about one point eight to four for x ray, because film contrast, the curve's gradient, collapses low and climbs with density' (~9 s beat): (1) fdcCurve — the S-shaped curve draws left-to-right showing toe, steep straight-line region, shoulder, at 0.5-2.5 s while 'Codes window density' is spoken; (2) fdcWindow — translucent cyan horizontal band spanning density 1.8 to 4.0 with small tag 'CODE WINDOW · X-RAY 1.8-4.0', fading in 2.5-4.5 s as the numbers are narrated; (3) fdcGradient — two tangent-line slope markers: a shallow tangent on the toe below the window tagged 'FLAT' and a steep tangent on the straight-line region inside the window tagged 'STEEP', appearing 5.5-8 s as narration says the gradient 'collapses low and climbs with density'. Keep in-art labels small; the renderer's note bar adds 'BELOW THE WINDOW, CONTRAST COLLAPSES' at ~7.9 s, so do not duplicate that text in the art.
---
Beat 4 diagram (narration, 25w ~10s: 'Film contrast is the slope of the characteristic curve, and the slope climbs as density rises. That's the real reason codes set a minimum density.'). H&D characteristic curve mirroring lesson figure rt1-8-1-fig-b. Axes: X = LOG RELATIVE EXPOSURE, Y = DENSITY scaled 0-4. Industrial X-ray film curve: starts just above zero (base+fog), shallow toe, then gradient continuously steepening through the working range — do NOT flatten into a visible shoulder below density 4 (industrial film gradient keeps rising per the lesson). Reveals: fdcCurve = axes + curve trace on, 0.0-2.0s ('Film contrast is the slope of the characteristic curve'); fdcSlopeLow = at density ~1.0, a fixed-width exposure-step bracket on X projecting a SMALL density-change wedge onto Y, with shallow tangent line, ~3.0s ('the slope'); fdcSlopeHigh = same-width exposure-step bracket at density ~2.5-3.0 projecting a TALL density-change wedge, steep tangent, ~5.0s ('climbs as density rises'); fdcMinLine = dashed horizontal line near density 2.0 labeled CODE MIN with the region below subtly dimmed, ~7.5s ('codes set a minimum density'). Note strip lands at 8.6s reading 'SLOPE CLIMBS WITH DENSITY · MIN ≈ 1.8–2.0'.

film-speed-curves.brief: Two same-shape H&D curves on shared axes (FILM DENSITY vertical, LOG RELATIVE EXPOSURE horizontal), 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. The FAST film's curve sits left (reaches density 2 with less exposure), the SLOW film's curve sits right. Beat narration (~9.3s): 'Speed is position: fast films sit left, slow films right. Read the gap where they cross density two. Grain size buys speed - and costs sharpness.' Reveals: fspFast (draw ~1.2s, on 'fast films sit left') left curve + tag FAST; fspSlow (draw ~2.6s, on 'slow films right') right curve + tag SLOW; fspCut (draw ~4.2s, on 'Read the gap where they cross density two') horizontal reference line at DENSITY 2.0 crossing both curves, ticks dropping from each crossing to the exposure axis, bracket spanning the gap labeled EXPOSURE GAP = SPEED; fspGrain (fade ~7.0s, on 'Grain size buys speed') small inset pair: coarse large silver-halide grains under FAST vs fine small grains under SLOW. Spec note 'FASTER = COARSER GRAIN = LESS SHARP' fires at 8.4, after fspGrain.

film-illuminator.brief:
Title-beat panel (ambient scene, not narration-synced beyond beat start): darkened film-reading bench, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A high-intensity illuminator (light box) with a glowing cyan-white window; a girth-weld radiograph strip clipped over the lit window showing a subtle weld-seam image; a small densitometer beside it with a softly glowing readout (subtle ~2.0, no labeled callout); two or three stacked boxes of industrial film at the bench edge. Reveals (ambient): filGlow (fade ~0.6s) illuminator window lights up; filFilm (fade/slide ~1.4s) radiograph strip settles onto the lit window; filMeter (fade ~2.4s) densitometer readout + film boxes glow in. Reusable for later RT viewing/interpretation lessons.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan ambient title panel: a high-intensity film illuminator in a darkened reading room — rectangular unit in three-quarter view, large diffused viewing port, intensity control dial, vent louvers, hint of a foot-switch cable. A weld radiograph strip lies across the port with opaque mask plates framing the weld image. Reveals: ilmGlow (diffused port lights up with a soft, even cyan gradient — no hot spots, no visible lamp structure) at ~0.8s as the title lands; ilmFilm (radiograph strip with faint weld image fades in over the port) at ~1.8s; ilmMask (two opaque mask plates slide in from the sides to frame the weld zone, blocking raw port light) at ~3.0s on narration 'cashed in at the viewing station'; ilmDial (intensity dial with tick arc pulses once) at ~4.2s. Loopable idle glow afterward. NOTE: rt1-7-3 also uses this asset as its title panel ('READING ROOM // FILM BENCH') — one drawing must serve both; dedupe if a brief already exists.

coil-shot.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cutaway of a low fill-factor coil shot. BASE STATE (visible from beat start): a 5-turn magnetizing coil drawn in cross-section (upper and lower rows of turn cross-sections), current-direction arrows circulating on the turns, and faint longitudinal flux arrows running left-to-right through the EMPTY bore along a dashed centerline axis. Reveals sync to a ~10 s narration: 'Slide steel inside. Hundreds of times more permeable than air, the part pulls flux in to run along its length. No contact, no burns.' REVEALS: csPart (at ~0.8s, on 'Slide steel inside') — a steel shaft slides into the bore and rests AGAINST THE LOWER INSIDE WALL of the coil (low fill-factor position), clear air gap to the turns. csFlux (at ~3.0s, on 'pulls flux in to run along its length') — bore flux lines bend and crowd into the shaft: dense bright parallel flux lines running the shaft's full length end to end while the residual air-path lines dim; small chip 'STEEL ≫ AIR' near the flux convergence. csGap (at ~7.0s, on 'No contact, no burns') — highlight ring/ping on the air gap between shaft and coil turns with tag 'NO ELECTRICAL CONTACT', emphasizing the part carries flux, not current. Do NOT draw magnetic poles, end particle buildup, or cracks in this asset — this beat teaches induction only (poles and indications are covered by later beats without an asset).

casting-solidification.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan cutaway of a sand-mold flask holding a just-poured casting (flanged hub: one heavy boss, one thin web). Molten core glows at center; solidification advances inward from the mold walls (chill skin, columnar dendrite fringe, inward arrows); the heavy boss holds the last liquid, where a faint shrink void forms. Keep it pure foundry — NO probes, film, sources, or beams: this panel name is shared as a title visual with the UT castings lesson (ut2-3-2). Used here as a title-beat side panel behind label 'CASTING // FREEZING INWARD'. Narration (21 w, ~7.6 s): 'Castings are radiography's home turf: whatever goes wrong as the liquid freezes is locked in the volume, where the beam looks.' Reveals: csoMold at 0.3 (flask + cavity outline base state); csoMelt at 1.0 (glowing liquid core, during 'radiography's home turf'); csoFront at 3.2 (inward-freeze arrows + dendrite fringe drawing in, on 'as the liquid freezes'); csoHotSpot at 5.0 (pulsing last-to-freeze pocket in the heavy boss with faint shrink void, on 'locked in the volume'). Ambient heat-shimmer/ember particles welcome; loops calmly after final reveal.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of a riser-fed sand casting solidifying from the walls inward. Layout: sand-mold outline with an open riser on top; the cavity is an L-junction (vertical wall meeting a heavier flange) so a junction is visible; molten-metal glow inside. Used as the title-beat hologram panel behind narration 'A casting is metal that froze in place. Nothing refines it afterward, so what the mold left behind is what arrives under your probe.' (~9.5s). Required animatable reveals: cslMold (base state at 0s — mold, riser, liquid metal fill glow); cslZones (fade/draw at ~2.5s, on 'froze in place' — thin bright chill-zone skin along the mold walls, columnar grains growing perpendicular inward with subtle heat-flow arrows, coarse equiaxed grains blooming in the center); cslLast (fade + ping at ~6s, on 'what the mold left behind' — LAST TO FREEZE highlight along the thermal centerline and under the riser, with a small ragged shrinkage void pinging there). Keep reveals subtle and loop-friendly since this sits as a title panel.

sand-mold-cutaway.brief:
600x340 cross-section of a sand mold mid-pour (lesson fig rt2-5-1-fig-b analog). Flask split horizontally: COPE upper half, DRAG lower half, dashed PARTING LINE between. Left side: pouring basin feeding a vertical SPRUE down to a runner; GATES enter the cavity. Cavity is a dumbbell casting: heavy boss at right under a RISER column (reservoir of liquid with a feed arrow down into the boss), thin web at left far from the gates, and a sand CORE through the middle forming an internal passage. Molten metal rendered as animated glow filling sprue/runner/cavity. Diagram beat narration (23 w, ~8.3 s): 'Sand casting: cope over drag; metal pours down the sprue, through gates; risers feed heavy sections; cores form passages. Every feature can fail.' Reveals: smcCope at 0.9 (cope half tints + COPE label, on 'cope'); smcDrag at 1.4 (drag half tints + DRAG label + parting line, on 'over drag'); smcPour at 2.2 (molten stream animates down sprue + SPRUE label, along runner, on 'metal pours down the sprue'); smcGates at 3.7 (gate openings ping + GATES label as metal enters cavity, on 'through gates'); smcRiser at 4.5 (riser reservoir glows + downward feed arrow into the heavy boss + RISER label, on 'risers feed heavy sections'); smcCore at 5.9 (core highlights + CORE label, internal passage visible, on 'cores form passages'); smcFail at 7.0 (four small amber alert pings: riser contact, core, gate, fillet, on 'Every feature can fail'). On-screen note fires at 7.5, so smcFail must read instantly.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan side cutaway of a two-part sand casting flask, mid-pour. Used as the TITLE-beat panel (~9s on screen, panelLabel 'SAND MOLD // MID-POUR'). Geometry: cope (top half) and drag (bottom half) meeting at a thin parting line; pouring basin and vertical sprue at left; runner along the bottom; gate entering a central casting cavity; riser column standing above the cavity at right; molten metal rendered as the one warm/bright accent against the navy/cyan sand. Small mono labels: COPE, DRAG, PARTING LINE, SPRUE, RUNNER, GATE, RISER. Animatable reveals vs narration ('A weld is built layer by layer. A casting is one violent event: every flaw decided in minutes, inside a closed mold.'): smcFlask (draw, ~0.5s) flask outline, cope/drag halves, parting line — under 'A weld is built layer by layer'; smcPour (draw + gentle loop, ~1.8s) bright melt stream runs down the sprue, along the runner, through the gate — lands on 'one violent event'; smcFill (fade/grow, ~3.8s) cavity fill level and riser reservoir rise with a soft heat glow — under 'every flaw decided in minutes'; smcLabels (fade, ~6.0s) the small mono part labels fade in — under 'inside a closed mold'. Keep motion ambient and subtle; this is scene-setting, not the teaching diagram.

air-scan-rig.brief: Production air-coupled through-transmission scanning rig, side view, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A flat composite honeycomb sandwich panel (hint hex-core cells in the cut edge) rides on conveyor rollers. A C-frame yoke straddles it: transmit probe above the panel, receive probe below, each separated from the panel by a clearly visible AIR GAP. No liquid or gel anywhere in the scene. Small inset monitor showing a C-scan strip building. Static base art: panel, rollers, C-frame, monitor. Beat is ~7.5 s of narration ('Air-coupled testing throws away the couplant entirely. Nothing wet touches the part: sound crosses an air gap in, and out.'). Required reveals: asrRig (fade, ~0.6 s) highlights the C-frame and both probes as narration opens 'throws away the couplant entirely'; asrNoWet (fade, ~2.6 s) crossed-out couplant-droplet glyph beside the upper air gap for 'nothing wet touches the part'; asrBeamIn (draw, ~5.0 s) beam line from the top probe across the upper air gap into the panel for 'sound crosses an air gap in'; asrBeamOut (draw, ~6.4 s) beam continuing out the bottom surface, across the lower air gap, into the receive probe for 'and out'. Keep the lower-left corner clear for the player's panel-label chip (NO COUPLANT // COMPOSITES).

air-impedance-wall.brief: Physics cross-section of why air coupling is so lossy, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Horizontal layout: small transmit probe at left, wide AIR band, vertical solid plate mid-frame, second AIR band, small receive probe at right; label the two boundaries ENTRY and EXIT. Top-left formula chip 'Z = ρ × v'; below it two mini impedance bars, AIR (sliver-thin) vs SOLID (towering). Top-right cumulative loss meter ending past '>100 dB'. Beat is ~9.3 s of narration ('Blame acoustic impedance, density times velocity. Air's tiny, a solid's enormous: each air-to-solid interface reflects nearly everything. Two crossings can lose over one hundred decibels.'). Required reveals: aiwZchip (fade, ~0.9 s) the Z = ρ × v chip for 'acoustic impedance, density times velocity'; aiwZbars (fade, ~2.6 s) the air-vs-solid bar pair for \"Air's tiny, a solid's enormous\"; aiwBeam (draw, ~4.4 s) incident beam from the transmit probe reaching ENTRY; aiwReflect1 (draw, ~5.1 s) large reflected arrow bouncing back at ENTRY with only a thin transmitted sliver continuing, for 'each air-to-solid interface reflects nearly everything'; aiwReflect2 (draw, ~6.4 s) the same repeat at EXIT with the surviving sliver thinner still, reaching the receive probe; aiwLoss (fade, ~7.4 s) loss meter sweeping past 100 dB for 'two crossings can lose over one hundred decibels'. Keep bottom-center clear for the player's note chip (TWO AIR INTERFACES · OVER 100 DB LOST) at 8.6 s.

hvl-stack.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan shielding-math strip. Layout: gamma source capsule at far left emitting a horizontal beam arrow rightward through three identical upright lead plates spaced across the middle; the beam visibly thins and dims after each plate. Intensity readouts above the beam at each stage: '40 R/H' before plate 1, then '20 R/H', '10 R/H', '5 R/H' after plates 1-3; each plate tagged '1 HVL'. Dashed goal chip at far right: 'TARGET: 5 R/H'. Math chip along the bottom: '40 / 5 = 8 = 2x2x2'. Animatable reveals, timed against beat-3 narration ('Run one: forty roentgens per hour must drop below five. Forty over five is eight: three halvings, so three half value layers.' ~9s): hvlSource (fade ~0.8s) = source capsule + beam stub + 40 R/H readout, lands on 'forty roentgens per hour'; hvlTarget (fade ~3.0s) = dashed TARGET: 5 R/H chip, lands on 'must drop below five'; hvlMath (fade ~4.8s) = bottom math chip, lands on 'forty over five is eight'; hvlPlate1 (draw ~6.2s) = first lead plate, beam dims, '20 R/H'; hvlPlate2 (draw ~7.0s) = second plate, '10 R/H'; hvlPlate3 (draw ~7.8s) = third plate with '5 R/H' readout glowing brighter cyan, lands on 'three half value layers'. The on-screen note '40 / 5 = 8 -> THREE HALVINGS -> 3 HVL' appears at 8.0s, so all reveals must be complete by then.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan diagram: a horizontal radiation beam enters from the left and crosses a stack of four identical vertical steel plates, with a transmitted-intensity readout (bar plus fraction label) after each plate. Label one plate '0.5 IN STEEL = 1 HVL (IR-192)'. Use generic halving fractions (1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16) as the primary readouts so the asset serves multiple lessons. Reveal ids and timing vs rt2-1-1 beat 3 narration (~9.8 s): hvBeam = incident beam plus full-strength '1' bar (0-2.5 s, 'each identical layer removes the same fraction'); hvLayer1 = first plate highlights and transmitted bar drops with '1/2' readout (2.5-6 s, 'about half an inch of steel halves an iridium beam'); hvLayer2, hvLayer3, hvLayer4 = remaining plates cascade with '1/4', '1/8', '1/16' readouts (6-8.8 s, 'four layers, one sixteenth'); hvTotal = the final 1/16 readout pulses as the on-screen note lands at 8.8 s. NOTE: converge with rt1-3-3 (40 R/h dropping below 5: three halvings, stop at hvLayer3) and rt1-5-1 (3 HVL of lead = 1/8) — per-layer reveal ids let each lesson stop at its own depth, so keep readouts as separate reveal layers and fractions generic rather than hard-coded dose rates.
---
Half-value-layer attenuation diagram, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Left: Ir-192 source capsule (small dot in capsule outline) labeled IR-192 with starting rate chip '160 mR/h'. A horizontal gamma beam arrow travels right through three upright lead plates spaced evenly across the panel; after each plate the beam arrow gets visibly thinner/dimmer (exponential attenuation) and a rate chip above shows the halved value: '80 mR/h', '40 mR/h', '20 mR/h'. Each plate carries a small '1 HVL' tag. Bottom chip: '1 HVL ≈ 4.8 mm LEAD (IR-192)'. Right-end callout: '3 HVL = 1/8'. Narration over this beat (24 words, ~9 s): 'Shielding cuts the rate in half with each half value layer — for iridium one ninety-two, about five millimeters of lead. Stack three: one eighth.' Reveals: hvsSource at 0.8 = source capsule + 160 mR/h chip (on 'Shielding'); hvsBeam at 1.2 = draw beam arrow ('cuts the rate'); hvsPlate1 at 2.4 and hvsRate80 at 2.9 = first plate + 80 mR/h chip (on 'in half with each half value layer'); hvsHvlChip at 5.2 = '1 HVL ≈ 4.8 mm LEAD' bottom chip (as narration says 'about five millimeters of lead'); hvsPlate2 at 6.6 + hvsRate40 at 7.0, then hvsPlate3 at 7.4 + hvsRate20 at 7.8 (on 'Stack three'); hvsEighth at 8.4 = '3 HVL = 1/8' callout (lands on 'one eighth'). Do NOT include TVL — narration does not cover it.

dwsi-offset-shot.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan schematic. End-on cross-section of a large-bore pipe: outer ring with visible wall thickness; the girth-weld plane is a vertical dashed centerline with weld notches where it crosses the top and bottom walls. Bore interior carries a small lock chip reading NO INTERNAL ACCESS. A gamma source point sits OUTSIDE the bottom wall, offset slightly to one side of the weld plane and angled toward it; its beam cone crosses BOTH walls and lands on a film strip curved against the OUTSIDE of the top wall, centered on the weld. A thin projected-image strip floats just above the film arc. Narration for this beat (~9.5 s): 'Closed pipe above 3.5 inches: source offset outside one wall, film opposite. Read only the sharp film-side weld. Three shots minimum, 120 degrees apart.' Required animatable reveals: dwsSetup (on entry, 0-3.5 s) = pipe ring + NO INTERNAL ACCESS chip + offset/angled source with beam cone through both walls + film strip on the opposite wall; syncs with 'source offset outside one wall, film opposite'. dwsFilmSide (~3.5 s) = on the projected-image strip, a crisp weld image directly over the film labeled FILM SIDE - READ, plus an enlarged, blurred ghost image displaced to the side labeled SOURCE SIDE - NOT READ; syncs with 'Read only the sharp film-side weld'. dwsCoverage (~6.8 s) = compact inset ring at right showing three source positions spaced 120 degrees apart with three overlapping coverage arcs, tagged '>=3 SHOTS @ 120'; syncs with 'Three shots minimum, 120 degrees apart'. The beat's note overlay fires at 8.0 s - keep the lower portion of the canvas uncluttered.

elliptical-shot.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan schematic. Side elevation of a small-bore tube (horizontal, both walls drawn with thickness) with a circumferential butt weld drawn as a vertical band crossing the top and bottom walls. Source point ABOVE the tube, offset ALONG THE PIPE AXIS from the weld plane (draw an axial-offset arrow along the tube between the weld plane and the source position); beam fans through BOTH walls to a flat film plate below the tube. Projection rays show the top-wall (source-side) weld landing displaced along the film while the bottom-wall (film-side) weld lands nearly straight down. Radiograph inset panel at right. Narration for this beat (~9.5 s): '3.5 inches and under, read both walls. Offset the source along the axis; the weld opens into an ellipse. Two shots, 90 degrees apart.' Required animatable reveals: ellOffset (on entry, 0-3.5 s) = tube + weld band + axially offset source with AXIAL OFFSET arrow + beam through both walls to the film; syncs with 'Offset the source along the axis'. ellEllipse (~3.5 s) = radiograph inset draws the weld image opening into an ellipse: lower limb labeled FILM SIDE, upper limb labeled SOURCE SIDE, both readable on one film; syncs with 'the weld opens into an ellipse'. ellRotate (~6.8 s) = small end-view badge with a curved 90-degree rotation arrow between shot-1 and shot-2 source positions, tagged '2 SHOTS @ 90'; syncs with 'Two shots, 90 degrees apart'. Note overlay fires at 8.0 s - keep the lower-right area clear.

darkroom-plan.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan plan-view schematic of a film darkroom, used as a TITLE-BEAT side panel (keep linework bold and legible at panel scale). Layout: room outline with a doorless maze light-trap entrance at left (overlapping wall stubs, label LIGHT TRAP); DRY SIDE along the top wall (loading bench, light-tight film bin under it, cassette stack, small pass-through hatch in the wall); WET SIDE along the bottom wall (tank line DEV/STOP/FIX/WASH plus sink); two safelight fixtures as soft deep-red glow cones over bench and tanks; light-trapped vent louver on the right wall labeled VENT. Labels DRY SIDE and WET SIDE in mono caps. Animatable reveals timed to the ~9s title narration 'Every radiograph is made twice: exposure builds the invisible latent image, (0-4.5s) the darkroom makes it visible, (4.5-6.5s) and one light leak ruins it. (6.5-9s)': dkRoom at 0.5s (room shell + maze entrance + side labels fade in); dkDry at 2.5s (dry-side bench/bin/cassettes draw in, a cassette glyph glows cyan = unprocessed latent image arriving); dkWet at 4.5s (wet-side tank line pulses, film hanger dips toward DEV); dkLeak at 7.0s (thin white wedge of light flashing through a gap at the door seam plus small alert-triangle ping, looping subtly = the leak that ruins film).

film-processing.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan side-by-side comparison: manual tank line vs automatic processor (canonical asset, reusable for darkroom/processing lessons). LEFT ~45%: manual line of four open-top insulated tanks labeled DEV / STOP / FIX / WASH, a film hanger suspended over DEV, mono caption MANUAL. RIGHT ~55%: automatic processor cutaway with feed tray, serpentine film path through three roller-rack sections labeled DEV / FIX / WASH, then a heated DRYER section and exit slot with a finished radiograph emerging; mono caption AUTOMATIC. Keep the bottom band clear (note overlay lands at 8.0s). Reveals timed to the ~9.5s narration 'Manual tanks are cheap but every variable is yours. (0-4s) The automatic processor locks time and temperature: (4-7s) dry film in eight to twelve minutes. (7-9.5s)': fpTanks at 0.5s (manual tank line + hanger draw in); fpVariables at 1.8s (stopwatch, thermometer, and agitation glyphs hover over the manual tanks = time, temperature, agitation all belong to the operator); fpProcessor at 4.2s (processor cutaway fades in, film path traces through the rollers, a padlock glyph clamps onto a TIME + TEMP chip near the thermostat); fpBadge at 7.5s (badge '8-12 MIN DRY-TO-DRY' pings at the exit slot beside the finished radiograph).
---
Manual film processing line (600x340, holographic navy/cyan) - SHARED ASSET: referenced by rt1-9-1 (diagram beat) and rt2-2-3 (title panel); one drawing must serve both. Left-to-right darkroom wet-side line: four open tanks - DEVELOPER (with thermometer and a stainless film hanger dipping in, faint vapor), STOP, FIXER, WASH (cascade/overflow water) - then a DRYER cabinet with airflow lines at far right. Arrows step tank to tank showing fixed order. Caption strip under the stations with the lesson's times: 'DEV 5 MIN @ 68 °F', 'STOP 30-60 S', 'FIX ≥ 2x CLEARING TIME' (no fixed minutes - the lesson defines fixing by clearing time), 'WASH 20-30 MIN', 'DRY FILTERED WARM AIR'. Animatable reveals timed vs rt1-9-1 beat-3 narration ('Processing runs in a fixed sequence: develop, stop, fix, wash, dry. Development follows the time temperature chart, about five minutes at 68 degrees Fahrenheit.' ~9.5 s, note at 8.8): fpLine fade at 0.8 (tank/cabinet outlines + hanger); fpDevelop fade at 2.4, fpStop at 3.2, fpFix at 3.8, fpWash at 4.4, fpDry at 5.0 (station labels lighting in the spoken order); fpArrows draw at 5.6 (sequence arrows, 'fixed sequence'); fpTimes fade at 7.4 (the times caption strip as narration reaches 'five minutes at 68 degrees Fahrenheit'). In rt2-2-3 the asset plays as the beat-1 title panel (panelLabel 'DEVELOP→STOP→FIX→WASH→DRY' is rendered by the spec, not the asset), so the same reveal sequence works as ambient scene-setting.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan manual film-processing tank line, side elevation, for the diagram beat. Five stations left-to-right: DEVELOP tank, STOP tank, FIX tank, WASH tank (running-water ripples), DRY cabinet — linked by a thin flow arrow, with a film hanger suspended over the developer. Narration for sync (~10.3s): 'Processing runs in a fixed sequence: develop, stop, fix, wash, dry. Development follows the time temperature chart, about five minutes at 68 degrees Fahrenheit.' Reveals: fprLine at 0.5 (all five station outlines unlit + flow arrow); fprHanger at 1.4 (hanger lowers film into the developer); then each station lights cyan and its label pops in exact narration order — fprDevelop at 3.0, fprStop at 3.5, fprFix at 4.0, fprWash at 4.5, fprDry at 5.0; fprChart at 6.8 (small chip pinned to the develop tank: tiny rising time-temperature curve labeled TIME x TEMP — no numbers, because the beat note 'BY THE CHART — ABOUT 5 MIN AT 68 °F' lands at 8.8 and carries the figures; keep the lower-right area clear for that note).

safelight-coin-test.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, two zones; keep the bottom band clear (note overlay lands at 8.4s reading SEE A COIN OUTLINE? THE BENCH ISN'T SAFE). LEFT ~60%: elevation of a darkroom loading bench with a DIRECT safelight fixture hanging above it - housing with a deep-red filter panel, bulb glyph tagged 15 W, cone of dim red light down onto the bench; vertical dimension line from fixture to bench surface labeled '>= 4 FT'; on the bench, a sheet of film with three coins resting on it. RIGHT ~40%: the processed test film held up as the result - lightly fogged gray field with three pale circular coin outlines, alert-triangle, mono tag FOG = FAIL. Reveals timed to the ~9s narration 'A direct safelight: fifteen watts (0-2s) behind a filter matched to your film, (2-4s) four feet or more from the bench. (4-6s) The coin test catches fog. (6-9s)': sflLamp at 0.5s (fixture + 15 W tag fade in with the red cone); sflFilter at 2.0s (filter panel highlights with callout FILTER MATCHED TO FILM); sflDistance at 4.2s (the >= 4 FT dimension line draws downward to the bench); sflCoins at 6.0s (film sheet + coins appear on the bench); sflResult at 7.4s (processed film with visible coin outlines + FOG = FAIL pings - the failure the test exists to catch).

dac-disposition.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan A-scan style plot for dispositioning weld indications against a DAC curve (pressure-equipment workmanship acceptance). STATIC BASE: faint grid, vertical axis 'SCREEN HEIGHT %', horizontal axis 'SOUND PATH'; keep lower-right corner clear for the renderer's note chip. REVEALS (3, in narration order; beat is ~9.5s / 24 words): (1) ddCurve at ~0.5-3.5s — three echo-peak markers from side-drilled holes at T/4, T/2, 3T/4 depths with a smooth descending curve drawn through their tips, labeled '100% DAC — REFERENCE'; shows while narration says 'Pressure codes build a DAC from side-drilled holes.' (2) ddPlanar at ~4-7s — a sharp narrow spike at mid sound-path peaking clearly BELOW the curve, tagged 'CRACK / LOF / IP' with a REJECT badge; shows on 'cracks, lack of fusion, incomplete penetration reject at any amplitude' — the visual point is that planar characterization rejects even under the reference line. (3) ddLength at ~7-9.5s — a broader indication peaking ABOVE the curve with a horizontal length bracket beneath it labeled 'LENGTH > LIMIT' and a second REJECT badge; shows on 'others above reference reject on length.' Do NOT draw a recording-threshold line or percentage values other than 100% DAC — the narration does not cover them. Distinct from the existing ut1-8-3 'dac-curve' asset (which shows building the curve by peaking the holes); this one shows judging two indications against the finished curve.

particle-application.brief:
Split dry-vs-wet application scene, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. LEFT half: rough as-welded steel plate with weld ripples, squeeze bulb above puffing a light cloud of dry powder grains that tumble across the texture; subtle heat shimmer off the plate. RIGHT half: smooth machined shaft under a hose/nozzle flooding wet suspension that sheets over the whole surface; a hairline grinding crack lies across it. Thin center divider with small DRY | WET captions. Reveals timed to mt1-5-1 beat-3 narration (~9s): paDryCloud (fade, ~0.6s) bulb puffs and the powder cloud drifts over the rough weld surface, syncs with 'Dry powder tumbles over rough, hot work'; paHotTag (fade, ~3.6s) glowing temperature chip reading approx 600 F on the dry side, syncs with 'six hundred degrees Fahrenheit'; paWetBath (fade, ~5.4s) hose floods suspension over the machined shaft, syncs with 'the far finer wet bath'; paFineCrack (ping, ~7.6s) the hairline crack snaps into a thin bright indication on the wet side, syncs with 'fine, shallow cracks'. CONVERGENCE NOTE: this same asset is referenced by mt1-1-2 as an ambient title panel labeled 'DRY POWDER // WELD TOE' — the dry half must read standalone as a dry-powder-on-weld vignette, and the asset should loop gracefully when reveals are not driven.
---
Title-panel scene (600x340 holographic navy/cyan): dry-method particle application on a steel plate with a weld bead running diagonally. A hand-held powder bulb duster at upper right puffs fine dry magnetic powder; the feet of an energized yoke are cropped at the left/right frame edges so the magnetization context is honest without stealing focus from the mt-yoke asset; a thin line of powder begins to cling along the weld toe. Reveals: paPlate (static base: plate + weld bead + cropped yoke feet, visible at 0s), paPuff (duster compresses and emits a powder cloud at ~1.5s, under narration 'simple to demonstrate'), paDrift (cloud drifts down across the weld zone, ~2-5s), paCling (faint particle line condenses along the weld toe at ~6s, under 'every test walks the same backbone'). Used as ambient HUD panel behind a ~9s title VO; a gentle puff/drift loop after the first pass is fine.

continuous-method.brief: Two-lane application-timing diagram, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Across the top: a timeline with a glowing CURRENT ON window (cyan band with pulsing half-wave ticks). Lane 1 DRY (upper): small part under yoke poles; dusting and air-sweep events drawn INSIDE the ON window, with a crack indication that persists. Lane 2 WET (lower): flood-hose event drawn BEFORE the ON window, hose diverted at the window edge, then two short shot pulses (~half-second bursts) inside the window while bath drains off the part and the indication forms. Reveals timed to mt1-5-1 beat-4 narration (~9s): cmWindow (draw, ~0.7s) the CURRENT ON band draws across the timeline, syncs with 'indications form while current flows'; cmIndication (ping, ~2.2s) indication pings inside the ON window; cmDryCloud (fade, ~3.4s) light powder cloud drifts onto the energized part in the dry lane, syncs with 'dust a light cloud'; cmAirSweep (draw, ~4.9s) gentle air-stream arrow lifts loose powder while the indication stays held, syncs with 'clear excess with gentle air, all under power'; cmFlood (fade, ~6.7s) wet lane hose floods the part ahead of the window, syncs with 'flood'; cmShot (ping, ~7.9s) hose diverts and the two shot pulses fire as the bath drains, indication forms, syncs with 'divert, shoot'. The contrast to land: dry work happens under power; wet floods first, then shoots while draining.

hypo-spot-test.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan diagram of residual-thiosulfate staining and the silver densitometric spot test. Reserve a single amber/sepia accent exclusively for the sulfide stain. Layout: left ~60% is a processed radiograph (dark film rectangle showing a weld image with two or three fine IQI wires) with a magnified emulsion cross-section inset below it showing residue dots trapped in the gelatin, captioned RESIDUAL THIOSULFATE (HYPO); right ~40% is the spot-test station: a clear corner of film, a dropper above it, and a calibrated reference-stain comparison strip of five patches running faint to dark brown. IMPORTANT: patches carry NO numeric labels (the 2 micrograms per square centimeter limit is not narrated and must not appear). Animatable reveals, timed against the 23-word beat-3 narration (~9.3s total, on-screen note lands at 7.2s): (1) hstFilm — the radiograph plus emulsion inset with hypo residue dots; visible from beat start (0-2.3s) while narration says 'Leftover fixer, residual thiosulfate'. (2) hstStain — amber-brown tint sweeps across the film background, the thinnest IQI wire fades out, and a small 'Ag -> Ag2S' tag appears; reveal at ~2.3s on 'slowly converts the silver image to yellow-brown silver sulfide'. (3) hstDrop — dropper places one test drop on the clear film corner with a dwell-timer ring, then a blot motion; reveal at ~5.0s on 'A spot test catches it'. (4) hstPatches — the reference strip illuminates and the blotted test stain slides into alignment beneath one of the darker patches with a pointer chevron; reveal at ~6.4s on 'darker stain, more hypo left', completing just before the 7.2s note overlay.

a-scan-display.brief:
CANONICAL SHARED PANEL (also referenced by ut1-1-1, ut1-1-2, ut1-2-3, ut1-3-1, ut1-3-2, ut1-8-1, ut1-8-2, ut2-1-1, ut2-1-2, ut2-1-4 — merge briefs, keep it generic). 600x340 holographic navy/cyan flaw-detector A-scan: 10-division graticule, baseline sweep, tall initial pulse at left, mid-height flaw echo near mid-sweep, strong back-wall echo at right, and a horizontal distance scale under the sweep (0–100 MM) so the time axis visibly reads as a ruler. Animatable reveals (times for ut2-6-4 beat 1, ~9.0s, 24 words): asdGrid (draw, 0.5s) graticule + baseline as the screen powers up; asdPulse (fade, 1.2s) initial pulse; asdEcho (ping, 2.2s) flaw echo pops as narration says 'now say exactly where it lives'; asdBackwall (fade, 3.6s) back-wall echo for context as 'repair and code decisions' is spoken; asdRuler (fade, 7.0s) the 0–100 MM distance labels light up under the sweep exactly as narration lands 'position starts with time as distance'.

CANONICAL SHARED ASSET — also referenced by ut1-1-1, ut1-2-3, ut1-3-1, ut1-3-2; design once, generic (dedupe if a request already exists from those specs). 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: a flaw-detector A-scan screen. Instrument bezel with rounded corners, dark navy CRT area, cyan graticule grid (10x5). Reveals: asdGrid (fade, ~0.3s) graticule; asdTrace (draw, ~0.8s) baseline sweep left-to-right; asdIP (fade, ~1.5s) tall initial-pulse spike at left edge; asdEchoes (fade, ~2.8s) two mid-screen echo spikes of different heights, one tagged with a faint cyan query ring; asdBackwall (fade, ~4.0s) strong back-wall echo near right edge with small BW tag. Generic timing — in ut2-1-1 it is the title-beat side panel labeled 'A-SCAN // SIGNALS ON TRIAL' over a ~9s beat, so reveals must read as an ambient loop-friendly build, not narration-locked.

SHARED CANONICAL UT ASSET — already referenced by ut1-1-1, ut1-2-3, ut1-3-1, ut1-3-2, ut1-8-1, ut1-8-2, ut2-1-1, ut2-1-2, ut2-1-4, ut2-6-1; if a brief or drawing already exists from those requests, keep that single version and ignore this duplicate. Generic flaw-detector A-scan screen, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: dark graticule grid, initial pulse at far left, one or two mid-screen flaw echoes, back-wall echo at right, instrument bezel hinting. Ultrasonic flaw-detector screen face: thin instrument bezel with two small knob hints, dark plotting area with a 10-division grid, sharp initial-pulse spike at division 0 and a decaying train of back echoes at divisions 2/4/6/8, gain readout chip '42.0 dB' top-right, faint axis tags 'TIME →' along the baseline and 'VOLTS ↑' at the left edge. In ut2-1-4 this is the beat-1 title HUD panel (panelLabel 'TIME AND VOLTAGE ONLY') behind the narration 'a flaw detector is just a voltmeter with a screen' — it must read instantly at panel scale with idle scan-sweep/trace shimmer only; no timed reveals are required for this lesson. Provide generic reveals for diagram-beat reuse by sibling lessons: asTrace (draw) baseline + echo train, asGain (fade) gain chip, asPing (ping) on the first back echo. Pulse-echo principle with live A-scan readout (600x340, holographic navy/cyan). LEFT ~55%: vertical cross-section of a steel plate, top surface (probe side) at top, back wall at bottom. A straight-beam contact probe sits on the top surface over a thin couplant line; a small crack-like flaw floats mid-wall, dim in base art. Do NOT draw a back-wall echo signal (narration never mentions it; the plate bottom stays plain geometry). RIGHT ~45%: A-scan grid, horizontal time axis, vertical amplitude. Reveals (beat ~10s; narration: 'Around nineteen forty, Firestone's pulse-echo changed everything: send a short pulse, listen with the same crystal. Echo travel time reveals flaw depth, from one side.'): asPulse (draw, 3.6s) pulse wavefront travels from probe down into the plate on 'send a short pulse'; asPeakIp (fade, 3.8s) initial-pulse peak pops at left edge of the A-scan; asFlawPing (ping, 4.8s) pulse strikes the mid-wall flaw; asEcho (draw, 5.2s) echo-path arrow returns from flaw up to probe on 'listen with the same crystal'; asPeakFlaw (fade, 6.3s) flaw-echo peak appears mid-sweep on the A-scan; asTimeArrow (fade, 7.0s) horizontal arrow spanning the two peaks labeled TRAVEL TIME on 'echo travel time'; asDepthLbl (fade, 7.8s) depth bracket on the plate from top surface down to the flaw labeled DEPTH on 'reveals flaw depth'; asOneSide (fade, 9.4s) small bracket + ONE SIDE tag at the probe/top surface on 'from one side'. Keep bottom-center clear: the player drops the formula note chip 'ROUND-TRIP TIME x VELOCITY / 2 = DEPTH' at 9.0s.
