Canonical flaw-detector A-scan screen (600x340 holographic navy/cyan) — reusable across UT lessons. Instrument bezel framing a graticule: 0-60 mm depth scale along the bottom, faint horizontal amplitude divisions, thin cyan baseline with low grain 'grass'. Three trace signals on a 50 mm steel plate (matches lesson fig ut1-2-3-fig-b): initial pulse hard against the left edge (tallest, slightly ragged), mid-wall discontinuity echo at 25 mm (~55% screen height), back-wall echo at 50 mm (~80% height). Tiny mono captions under the peaks: 'IP', '25 MM', 'BW 50 MM'. USED AS: title-beat panel behind 'WHY ECHOES HAPPEN', beat ~9 s. Narration: 'Why does an ultrasonic echo happen? Because sound suddenly meets a material that carries it differently. That property is acoustic impedance.' REQUIRED REVEALS: asdScanG — looping sweep glint across the screen (scan id, sweep ~4.5 s); asdGrid (fade, 0.3 s) — bezel + graticule + baseline grass; asdPulse (draw, 1.0 s) — initial pulse rises, la

soundpath-trig.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of a steel plate (thickness labeled T, back wall at bottom) with a 45-degree angle-beam wedge probe on the top surface at left, its index point marked with a small tick. A reflector dot sits mid-thickness to the right. Distinct from angle-beam-path (skip coverage): this asset teaches the location right-triangle. Animatable reveals (beat 3 of ut2-6-4, ~8.5s, 22 words; spec note 'AFTER THE BOUNCE: DEPTH = 2T − S·COS θ' appears at 8.0s): sptBeam (draw, 0.8s) slanted first-leg beam from index point to the reflector, hypotenuse labeled SOUNDPATH S, refracted angle θ marked at the surface normal — narration: 'an angled path'; sptTriangle (fade, 1.8s) faint right-triangle fill joining beam, surface and vertical drop — narration: 'splits in two'; sptSurf (fade, 3.0s) horizontal bracket along the top surface from index point to above the reflector, labeled SURFACE = S × SIN θ — narration: 'surface distance is soundpath times sine theta'; sptDepth (fade, 4.9s) dashed vertical line from surface down to the reflector, labeled DEPTH = S × COS θ — narration: 'depth is soundpath times cosine'; sptExample (fade, 6.2s) small ghosted readout chip: 45° · S = 50 MM → 35 MM ACROSS · 35 MM DEEP (lesson's worked example); sptBounce (draw, 6.8s) beam continuing to the back wall and reflecting up as a second-leg ray with a ghost reflector on it — narration: 'until the beam bounces'; sptLeg2 (fade, 7.6s) dashed depth line from TOP surface to the second-leg ghost reflector with label DEPTH = 2T − S·COS θ, pairing with the spec note at 8.0s. Keep first-leg geometry visually dominant; second leg dimmer.

second-leg-locate.brief: 60-degree angle-beam location worked example, cross-section view (600x340 holographic navy/cyan). Base scene always visible: a 20 mm steel plate band in cross-section — scanning surface on top, back wall below, 'T = 20 mm' dimension at the left edge — with a single-V butt weld in the right third, V-groove bevels (fusion faces) drawn as faint glowing lines. Reveals, keyed to the 24-word narration 'Location is trigonometry: sixty degrees, twenty millimeter plate, fifty-five millimeters soundpath — second leg, twelve and a half millimeters deep. Plotted: the fusion line.' (~9.5 s beat): slgProbe (~0.8 s, with 'sixty degrees, twenty millimeter plate') — wedge + probe on the top surface left of the weld, index-point tick, refracted-angle arc labeled '60°' measured from the vertical. slgPath (~3.2 s, with 'fifty-five millimeters soundpath — second leg') — beam line animates down the first leg to the back wall (label 'leg 1 = 40 mm'), bounces into the second leg, and ends at a bright flaw dot sitting on the weld bevel; running label 'SP = 55 mm' along the path and a '2ND LEG' tag at the back-wall bounce. slgMath (~5.8 s, with 'twelve and a half millimeters deep') — holo math card upper-right: 'depth = 2T − SP·cos 60° = 40 − 27.5 = 12.5 mm' and 'surface = SP·sin 60° ≈ 47.6 mm'; matching dimension arrows appear: vertical 12.5 mm arrow from the top surface down to the flaw dot, horizontal ≈47.6 mm arrow along the surface from the index point. slgPlot (~8.3 s, with 'Plotted: the fusion line', just before the on-screen note at 8.5 s) — crosshair pin snaps onto the flaw dot and the weld bevel/fusion line flares cyan, confirming the address lands exactly on the fusion face. All numbers are the lesson's own worked example; keep them exact.

particle-chain.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan title panel. A horizontal chain of ~14 glowing particle dots linked by faint spring coils sits mid-frame on a subtle grid. A compression pulse (a zone of bunched particles) travels left-to-right along the chain on a loop. One particle near center is haloed, with a dashed rest-position tick below it and a short double-headed arrow showing its tiny oscillation around rest. Above the chain, a long cyan arrow points right (energy travel direction). Caption chip reads PARTICLES STAY / ENERGY TRAVELS. Keep edges clean — the player overlays title text and the panel-label chrome. Beat narration (~7.7 s, 20 words): 'Every ultrasonic test starts here: sound is mechanical vibration handed particle to particle. The energy travels, the particles barely move.' Scan sweep object id pcscanG, sweep ~4.4. Animatable reveals (id, kind, at, what/sync): pcLattice (draw, 0.7) particle chain + spring links as the scene opens on 'sound is mechanical vibration'; pcPulse (draw, 3.4) the traveling compression bunch moving through the chain — synced to 'handed particle to particle'; pcEnergyArrow (draw, 5.1) the long rightward energy arrow — 'the energy travels'; pcFocus (fade, 6.3) haloed particle + dashed rest tick + tiny double-headed oscillation arrow — 'the particles barely move'; pcStayLbl (fade, 7.1) caption chip PARTICLES STAY / ENERGY TRAVELS, landing ~1 s before beat end.

long-vs-shear.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan teaching diagram, two stacked horizontal bands; band titles LONGITUDINAL (top-left of top band) and SHEAR (top-left of bottom band). TOP — LONGITUDINAL: a field of particle dots in vertical columns with alternating bunched and spread spacing (two compression zones, two rarefaction zones), a cyan wave-travel arrow pointing right along the band, and small double-headed particle-motion arrows oriented PARALLEL to travel; zone labels COMPRESSION over a bunched zone and RAREFACTION over a spread zone. BOTTOM — SHEAR: the same dot field threaded by a rope-like transverse sine, travel arrow pointing right, particle-motion double-headed arrows oriented PERPENDICULAR (vertical); right end carries a SOLIDS ONLY tag with small crossed-out droplet and gas glyphs (liquids/gases cannot carry shear). Leave bottom-center open — the player drops its note chip 'SHEAR EXISTS ONLY IN SOLIDS' there at 8.4 s. Beat narration (~9.6 s, 25 words): 'Watch the particles: longitudinal squeezes them parallel to travel, compressions and rarefactions. Shear shakes them sideways, like a rope. Liquids and gases can't carry it.' Scan sweep object id lvsscanG, sweep ~4.6. Animatable reveals (id, kind, at, what/sync): lvsLongBand (draw, 1.0) top band dot field + travel arrow — 'longitudinal squeezes them'; lvsLongArrows (fade, 2.2) parallel particle-motion arrows — 'parallel to travel'; lvsCompLbl (fade, 3.6) COMPRESSION label — 'compressions'; lvsRareLbl (fade, 4.1) RAREFACTION label — 'and rarefactions'; lvsShearBand (draw, 4.8) bottom band rope sine + travel arrow — 'Shear shakes them sideways'; lvsShearArrows (fade, 5.8) perpendicular particle-motion arrows — 'like a rope'; lvsNoLiquids (fade, 7.5) crossed-out droplet/gas glyphs + SOLIDS ONLY tag — 'Liquids and gases can't carry it', just before the player note at 8.4.

radiometer-check.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, light-verification scene in a dark booth. UV-A lamp head at top left, at working distance, casting a violet beam cone onto a flat steel examination surface at bottom right. A radiometer sensor puck lies flat ON the surface with its sensor window facing UP toward the lamp, coiled cord to a handheld meter whose LCD reads '1,000 µW/cm²' with a pass tick. Upper right: a small INSET card (separate mini-frame, so it does not imply the beam is on during the ambient check) labeled 'AMBIENT — UV OFF' with a photometer chip reading '2 fc' and a pass tick. Bottom: a row of three small recheck tags, left to right: 'EVERY SHIFT / 8 HR', 'BULB CHANGE', 'POWER LOSS'. Reveals vs beat-3 narration (25 words, ~10 s: 'Verify with calibrated meters at the examination surface — radiometer for UV A, photometer for ambient white light — every shift, after bulb changes, after power interruptions'): rmcScene (lamp + beam + steel surface, on from start, covers 'at the examination surface' 0-2.5s), rmcSensor (sensor puck on surface facing lamp + cord, fade ~1.5s), rmcRadiometer (LCD '1,000 µW/cm²' + pass ping ~3.5s at 'radiometer for UV A'), rmcPhotometer (inset 'AMBIENT — UV OFF / 2 fc' + tick ~5.5s at 'photometer for ambient white light'), rmcTags (three tags appear left-to-right ~7.0-8.5s as narration lists shift / bulb / power). Spec note overlay fires at 8.8s — keep the lower-third area clear for it.

iqi-penetrameter.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan title-panel hero: the two IQI families side by side above a faint film-cassette-and-weld backdrop. LEFT GROUP: hole-type penetrameter plaque in slight perspective — thin rectangle, three drilled through-holes of stepped diameter (largest to smallest), lead ID number '25' at one end; caption chip 'HOLE-TYPE'. RIGHT GROUP: wire-type IQI — translucent plastic envelope, six straight parallel wires of visibly stepped diameter, small lead-letters block; caption chip 'WIRE-TYPE'. Both groups present from the start at ~35% opacity wireframe. Animatable reveals: iqpPlaque (fade — left group to full brightness + caption) fires at ~4.8s when narration says 'hole-type'; iqpWire (fade — right group + caption) fires at ~5.6s on 'or wire'. Do NOT include quality-level text or 2% callouts on this hero (taught in later beats). Beat narration (~8.5s): 'You can't calibrate a weld. So code radiographs carry an image quality indicator — hole-type or wire — proving the technique worked.'
---
Hole-type IQI (penetrameter) on a simulated weld radiograph, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Static base: dark radiograph strip with a lighter horizontal weld band (echo lesson figure rt2-1-3-fig-b); beside the weld sits a rectangular plaque with a lead ID number and three through-holes of diameter 1T, 2T, 4T (4T largest); a faint, unlabeled wire-type IQI may lie across the weld in the background for realism but is never referenced or highlighted. Animatable reveals, timed against the beat narration 'The I Q I proves sensitivity. Two dash two T: a plaque two percent of part thickness whose two T hole must show' (~9 s beat): (1) iqPlaque — plaque brightens with its ID number and hole labels '1T · 2T · 4T' at 0.5-3 s while 'The I Q I proves sensitivity' is spoken; (2) iqThickness — edge-on thickness dimension callout tagged 'PLAQUE = 2% OF PART THICKNESS' at 3.5-6 s on 'a plaque two percent of part thickness'; (3) iqEssential — pulsing cyan ring around the 2T hole tagged 'ESSENTIAL HOLE — 2T' at 6-8 s on 'whose two T hole must show'. The renderer note bar shows the 1.0-inch worked example at ~7.6 s, so do not bake those numbers into the art.

iqi-2t-reading.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: a radiograph on a viewer — dark film strip with a lighter horizontal weld band. Static base: hole-type plaque image lying beside the weld with three holes labeled 4T / 2T / 1T and lead ID '25'; a wire-type image with six stepped wire shadows crossing the weld; thin bracket 'AREA OF INTEREST' over the weld zone. weld-flaw-map.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Single-V groove butt weld CROSS-SECTION used as a discontinuity location map (mirrors lesson figure rt2-9-3-fig-a; deliberately distinct from weld-macro, which shows VT surface flaws/corrosion pits, and from the pending weld-anatomy in ut2-4-1, which frames UT exam volume). Static base visible from 0s: two beveled plates, bevel faces, root faces with a root gap at bottom center, faint pass boundaries inside the V (root, fill, cap), HAZ bands flanking the fusion lines, dashed vertical centerline. Beat narration (~9.5s): 'Incomplete penetration stands at the root centerline. Slag rides between passes. Lack of fusion hides along the bevel. Porosity scatters anywhere.' Reveals must fire in that order: wfIP (draw ~1.0s) tight bright vertical unfused gap between the root faces on the centerline; wfIPLbl (fade ~1.3s) 'INCOMPLETE PENETRATION / ROOT CENTERLINE'. wfSlag (fade ~3.0s) 2-3 irregular elongated dark pockets trapped along pass boundaries and the groove wall; wfSlagLbl (fade ~3.3s) 'SLAG / BETWEEN PASSES'. wfLOF (draw ~4.9s) tight planar line lying flat IN the plane of one bevel fusion face (not crossing it); wfLOFLbl (fade ~5.2s) 'LACK OF FUSION / BEVEL PLANE'. wfPore (fade ~6.6s) small scattered gas-pore circles through the weld metal; wfPoreLbl (fade ~6.9s) 'POROSITY / SCATTERED ANYWHERE'. wfAxis (fade ~7.8s, synced with the spec-level note 'MOST FLAWS RUN PARALLEL TO THE WELD AXIS' at noteAt 7.8) a subtle out-of-plane weld-axis arrow glyph at the centerline.

sharp-vs-fuzzy.brief: Diagram for the depth-reading rule: one steel cross-section slab in side view, split into left and right halves for comparison, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Flux lines flow horizontally through the slab. LEFT: surface-breaking crack interrupting flux right at the skin — leakage field concentrated and strong at the mouth, with a SHARP, dense, narrow particle line standing on the surface. RIGHT: subsurface flaw (lens-shaped void below the surface) — flux bulges weakly through the covering metal, producing a BROAD, fuzzy, faint, loosely-held particle mound on the surface. Animatable reveals vs beat-4 narration ('Once it's relevant, shape testifies to depth. Sharp, dense, tightly held: a surface break. Broad, fuzzy, weak: the flaw sits below, and deeper means fuzzier.', ~10s): svfSection at 0s — slab plus flowing flux lines (base state); svfSharp at ~3.5s (on 'Sharp, dense, tightly held') — left crack, concentrated leakage spikes, and the tight dense particle line light up; svfFuzzy at ~6.5s (on 'Broad, fuzzy, weak') — right buried flaw, diffuse wide field, and the soft fuzzy mound fade in; svfDeeper at ~8.5s (on 'deeper means fuzzier') — small depth arrow on the right showing the flaw sitting lower while the mound grows wider and fainter (ghosted second position is fine). The spec's note overlay 'SHARP = SURFACE BREAK · FUZZY = SUBSURFACE' lands at 9.0s, so keep the lower band clear and make svfDeeper legible alongside it.

ingot-zones.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Vertical cutaway of a big-end-up steel ingot standing in its cast-iron mold: mold walls flanking left/right, faint insulated hot-top cap above, labels MOLD WALL and HOT TOP. Base state: dim ingot outline + mold only. Beat narration (24w, ~9s): 'An ingot freezes from the mold wall inward: chill skin, columnar grains, then a coarse center. The last liquid, top center, collects the trouble.' Animatable reveals, in narration order: izChill (~2.8s, on 'chill skin') = thin bright stippled band of fine randomly oriented grains hugging the mold-wall interface, label CHILL ZONE. izColumnar (~4.0s, on 'columnar grains') = long thin grains growing inward perpendicular to the mold walls with small growth-direction arrow ticks, label COLUMNAR. izEquiaxed (~5.3s, on 'then a coarse center') = blobby coarse equiaxed grains filling the upper-central core, label EQUIAXED. izTrouble (~7.5s, on 'collects the trouble', holds to beat end with note at 7.6s) = pulsing cyan glow over the top-center last-to-freeze region plus a dashed vertical centerline axis, tag LAST TO FREEZE. Do NOT pre-label pipe/porosity/segregation in this asset — the next beat enumerates them.

ingot-echoes.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Left third: small as-cast bloom shown in section with coarse-grain texture, straight-beam probe contact-coupled on its LEFT side shooting a horizontal beam toward the vertical centerline (side scan hits axial flaws broadside). Right two-thirds: one large A-scan screen (grid, baseline, back-wall echo slot at far right) with a frequency chip above it. Beat narration (24w, ~9.5s): 'Coarse grain buries five megahertz in grass, so drop to one or 2.25 megahertz. Pipe, struck broadside, rings loud; porosity murmurs; segregation barely whispers.' Animatable reveals, in narration order: ieGrass (~1.2s, on 'buries five megahertz in grass') = chip reads 5 MHZ, baseline fills with dense low-amplitude grass, back-wall echo absent, micro-label NO BACK WALL. ieLowFreq (~3.8s, on 'drop to one or 2.25 megahertz') = chip swaps to 1-2.25 MHZ, grass collapses to a clean baseline and a tall back-wall echo appears. iePipe (~5.6s, on 'pipe, struck broadside, rings loud') = vertical pipe cavity lights up on the block centerline broadside to the beam; screen shows one tall sharp mid-sweep echo while the back-wall echo visibly drops, tag PIPE. iePorosity (~7.4s, on 'porosity murmurs') = small dot cluster in the block; screen swaps to a low cluster of small echoes with a sagging back wall and no single dominant signal, tag POROSITY. ieSegregation (~8.8s, on 'segregation barely whispers') = faint shaded top-center zone in the block; screen nearly flat with back wall mostly intact, tag SEGREGATION - LITTLE OR NO ECHO.

densitometer-cutaway.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan technical cutaway of a tabletop transmission densitometer, side elevation: base with film stage, hinged reading arm closed over a strip of radiographic film, digital display on the base. Static linework draws first (body, stage, arm, film strip). Keep lamp, aperture, film, and detector vertically aligned so the light path reads bottom-to-top in one glance. Animatable reveals timed against beat-3 narration 'The precision tool, the densitometer: stabilized lamp, tiny aperture, photodetector, log readout. Each shift: warm up, zero, then verify on a calibrated step tablet.' (~9.5s total): dmLamp at ~2.0s — stabilized lamp under the stage lights up with an upward light cone; dmAperture at ~2.8s — small defining aperture where the beam necks through the stage beneath the film, highlight ring + callout '2 mm'; dmDetector at ~3.6s — photodetector in the arm directly above catches the surviving light, short arriving ray; dmReadout at ~4.4s — log-circuit glyph (D = log10 I0/It) wired to the display, display flips to '2.35'; dmTablet at ~7.0s — sleeved calibrated step tablet (strip of graded density steps, small 'CERTIFIED' tag) slides onto the stage as the verify line lands. On-screen note 'VERIFY AGAINST STEP TABLET · WITHIN ±0.05' appears at 8.3s — leave the lower-third clear for it.

step-wedge-match.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan scene: a glowing film illuminator panel face-on; on its left half a production weld radiograph strip (weld seam with a marked area of interest), beside it room for a step wedge comparison film — a strip of uniform density patches stepping light to dark, each with a small printed value (1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0). The matched patch and the adjacent film tone must be plausibly identical shades so the match reads honestly. Reveals timed against beat-4 narration 'The estimating tool: the step wedge comparison film. Match a patch beside your film, read its value. Close, but subjective. The densitometer stays the referee.' (~10s total): swStrip at ~1.2s — comparison strip slides onto the illuminator beside the radiograph as it is named; swSlide at ~2.8s — strip nudges along the film edge, patches passing the area of interest; swMatch at ~4.4s — one patch (printed 2.5) locks beside the area of interest with an outline pulse and readout 'D ≈ 2.5'; swSubjective at ~6.0s — small eye glyph with a tilde over the match, signaling the call is visual and subjective; swReferee at ~7.8s — compact densitometer badge stamps into a corner labeled 'REFEREE'. On-screen note 'SUBJECTIVE MATCH · DENSITOMETER IS REFEREE' appears at 9.0s — keep the lower-third clear.

film-on-viewer.brief:
DEDUPE GUARD: this asset name was first coined by rt1-8-1 (title panel, label 'READING ROOM // VIEWER'); rt2-3-3 reuses it as a title panel labeled 'READING BENCH // DENSITY'. If a brief or design already exists from the rt1-8-1 batch, keep that single design — one SVG must serve both lessons; do not fork. Otherwise: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan — a high-intensity film illuminator on a reading bench in a darkened room, a weld radiograph strip clipped over the glowing window (weld seam and IQI silhouette faintly visible), soft spill light onto the bench. Title-panel ambient loop only, no narration-synced reveals: fvGlow — slow breathing glow of the illuminator window; fvFilm — faint shimmer drifting across the film emulsion.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan title-panel scene (idle loop + single ping, same pattern as pipe-scan): a weld radiograph clipped on a high-luminance film illuminator in a darkened reading room, masked viewing window glowing, weld image showing an IQI plaque and small lead marker numerals near the film edges. Idle behavior: soft breathing glow from the illuminator window. Reveal: fvPing at ~1.9s — highlight ping sweeping across the weld image. No narration-timed reveals required (title panel). NOTE: this panel is already referenced by rt1-8-1, rt2-3-3, and rt2-4-1 — merge/dedupe with any existing brief rather than drawing a variant.
---
DEDUPE NOTE: this asset name is already referenced by existing specs rt1-8-1, rt2-3-2, rt2-3-3 and rt2-4-1 but is not yet in media-src/assets; if an earlier brief or design exists, keep it and ignore this fallback. Fallback brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A high-intensity film illuminator in a darkened evaluation room, masked viewing window glowing bright, a radiograph strip clipped over the window showing a few subtle dark indications, soft cyan rim light, small densitometer silhouette on the bench. For rt2-9-2 it serves title-panel duty only: ambient loop (gentle glow pulse, slow sheen sweep across the film), no narration-synced reveals required. Optional generic reveal ids other lessons may drive: fovGlow (illuminator powers on), fovFilm (film placed on window), fovZoom (dashed ring on a suspect area).
---
Title panel for beat 1 (narration, 18w ~7s: 'Every radiograph faces one question: can you see the smallest detail that matters? That ability is radiographic sensitivity.'). Darkened RT reading room, straight-on view of a high-intensity film illuminator: glowing cool cyan-white viewing window with a strip of weld radiograph clipped across it — dark film, lighter weld-seam band, two small round pore indications (one obvious high-contrast, one barely visible), small densitometer silhouette lower-right. Reveals: fvGlow = illuminator window flickers on, 0.0-0.6s ('Every radiograph'); fvFilm = radiograph strip slides onto the lit window, ~1.0s ('faces one question'); fvIndications = the two pore indications fade in, obvious one first then the faint one, 2.5-4.0s ('can you see the smallest detail that matters'); fvShimmer = gentle looping scan-light sweep across the film from ~5s through beat end ('radiographic sensitivity'). Echoes the lesson's reading-room hero image; reusable across RT film-interpretation lessons.
---
MERGE NOTE: rt1-8-1 already uses this name as its title panel — design ONCE to serve both; if rt1-8-1's brief conflicts, it takes precedence. 600x340 holographic navy/cyan title panel: dark film-reading room, a pipe girth-weld radiograph clipped onto a glowing illuminator window that backlights it; weld band visible with two or three subtle unlabeled density marks (one rounded dark spot, one fine line) that invite a closer look. No verdict or classification text anywhere — for rt2-4-1 the marks must read as unexplained. Ambient title-loop reveals: fovGlow (illuminator window warms up, ~0.6 s), fovFilm (film settles onto the window, ~1.2 s), fovPing (soft ping on one unexplained dark spot, ~2.5 s).
---
AMBIENT TITLE PANEL (600x340, holographic navy/cyan), shared by rt1-8-1 and rt2-3-2 — keep it neutral (a radiograph on an illuminator; do NOT label it good or bad). Dark reading-room vignette: a high-intensity film illuminator (rounded housing, bright cyan-white diffuser window) centered, with a radiographic film strip clipped over it showing a faint girth-weld image band. Reveals (ambient intro then loop, not narration-keyed): fovHousing at 0.4 — illuminator housing and bench edge; fovGlow at 0.9 — diffuser window lights up with soft bloom; fovFilm at 1.5 — film strip with weld image and film-edge ID text drops into the clips; fovScan — slow looping vertical scanline sweeping across the lit film for the remainder of the beat (title beats run ~10 s).

source-decay.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan TWIN-PLOT decay chart for Ir-192 (canonical 'source-decay' asset). LEFT: linear axes — activity in Ci (y, gridlines at 100/50/25) vs time in days (x, ticks 0/74/148/222). RIGHT: same data on semilog axes (log activity axis, same time ticks); keep both time axes visually aligned so curve-vs-line is the single takeaway. Small tag chip 'IR-192 · T1/2 = 74 D'. Reveals timed to beat-3 narration ('Iridium one ninety-two halves about every seventy-four days: one hundred curies becomes fifty, then twenty-five. On semilog paper, decay plots as a dead straight line.' — about 9.6s total): sdcAxes (~0.4s) both empty axis frames + IR-192 tag as the half-life sentence starts; sdcPt100 (~3.6s) glowing point at 0 d / 100 Ci on the LINEAR plot exactly at 'one hundred curies'; sdcPt50 (~4.8s) point at 74 d / 50 Ci with a dashed halving step-down line at 'fifty'; sdcPt25 (~5.6s) point at 148 d / 25 Ci at 'twenty-five'; sdcCurve (~6.2s) exponential curve drawing through the linear points, falling steeply then flattening; sdcEcho (~7.4s) the same three points popping onto the SEMILOG plot at 'On semilog paper'; sdcLine (~8.0s) a straight cyan line drawing through the semilog points at 'dead straight line', extending slightly past the last point to suggest predicting future dates. The renderer's diagram note 'ON SEMILOG PAPER, DECAY IS A STRAIGHT LINE' appears at 8.6s — sdcLine must be fully drawn by then.
---
CONVERGE: source-decay is also used by rt1-2-2 and rt1-3-3 specs; reveals here are for rt2-6-1's beat 2 (~8 s, 21 words). Ir-192 decay curve: X axis days 0–150, Y axis activity in curies 0–100, holographic grid. Reveals: sdCurve (exponential curve sweeps left to right) at 'Activity decays.' (0–1.5 s); sdCert (dot at day 0 / 100 Ci with a small certificate tag reading '100 Ci') at 'Certified at 100 curies' (~2 s); sdHalf (vertical drop line at 74 days labeled '74 d = 1 half-life', dot on curve at 50 Ci) at 'one half-life ago, you hold 50 today' (~4 s); sdOutput (cyan callout chip by the 50 Ci dot: '50 Ci → ≈25 R/h @ 1 m') at 'about 25 roentgen per hour at 1 meter' (~7 s, just before the on-screen note at 7.2 s). Axis labels minimal; the 74 d figure is the Ir-192 half-life constant backing the narrated 'one half-life'.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan decay chart: smooth exponential curve of source activity vs half-lives, with a dashed halving staircase under it. Y axis ACTIVITY (CI) with ticks at 80/40/20/10; X axis HALF-LIVES ELAPSED with ticks 0,1,2,3; curve starts at 80 Ci, halves each tick, continues faintly past n=3 toward zero (no labels out there). Each staircase drop carries a small '× ½' chip; bright dots+labels at (0, 80 CI), (1, 40 CI), (2, 20 CI). Beat narration (~8.5s): 'Radiography's favorite exponent is the halving pattern: each half-life multiplies activity by one half. Two half-lives take 80 curies to 40, then 20.' Required reveals: dcyAxes (fade 0.5 — axes+ticks), dcyCurve (draw 1.0–2.2 — the decay curve, while 'halving pattern' is spoken), dcyStart (fade 1.8 — 80 CI dot at n=0), dcyHalf1 (fade 3.2 — first staircase drop with × ½ chip + 40 CI dot, syncing 'each half-life multiplies activity by one half'), dcyHalf2 (fade 5.8 — second drop + 20 CI dot, syncing 'two half-lives take 80 curies to 40, then 20'). Keep lower-right clear: the beat note 'FRACTION LEFT = (½)ⁿ' overlays at 7.3s — do NOT draw that formula inside the asset.

six-db-drop.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of the 6 dB-drop length-sizing method (matches lesson fig ut2-6-3-fig-a). BASE (always visible): steel block in cutaway filling the lower two-thirds, containing ONE horizontal planar flaw (bright cyan slab, ~220 px wide) at mid-depth — deliberately wider than the beam; a straight-beam probe on the top scan surface with a downward beam cone ~80 px wide at flaw depth, its CENTERLINE drawn as a distinct dashed ray; top-right inset: mini A-scan grid with a solid 100% line and a dashed 50% line labeled '-6 dB'. REVEALS vs narration ('The classic six decibel drop: peak the echo, move until it falls to half height, mark. Repeat the other way. That travel is flaw length.', ~10 s beat): sddPeak (~1.5 s, at 'peak the echo') — probe centered over the flaw, beam cone lit, inset echo bar touching the 100% line, small tag 'PEAK'. sddHalfA (~3.5 s, at 'move until it falls to half height, mark') — ghosted probe slid LEFT until the dashed beam centerline sits exactly over the flaw's LEFT edge; inset echo drops to the 50% line; a tick mark stamped on the scan surface at the probe index point, flagged '-6 dB'. sddHalfB (~6 s, at 'repeat the other way') — mirrored ghost probe at the RIGHT edge with a second '-6 dB' surface tick; faint motion arrows showing travel back through the peak. sddTravel (~8 s, at 'that travel is flaw length') — cyan dimension arrow spanning the two surface ticks labeled 'PROBE TRAVEL = FLAW LENGTH', plus a matching dimension bracket hugging the flaw itself to show the equality. End state keeps all three probe positions visible (center solid, edge positions ghosted) so the whole method reads at a glance; the centerline-over-edge geometry is the key idea — keep it unmistakable. Renderer note overlay fires at 9.0 s, so keep the lower-left corner clear for it.

five-question-chain.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: a suspect ultrasonic signal interrogated by the Level II 'walk the chain' habit. Layout: far left, a small A-scan thumbnail (~120x90, screen bezel + graticule) with one ambiguous mid-screen echo spike highlighted by a cyan ring — reveal fqEcho (fade, ~0.2s, as narration opens 'Five questions.'). From the thumbnail, a horizontal track runs right through FIVE rounded-hex gate nodes in a chain, each with an uppercase label below and a small glyph inside; the whole chain appears first as dim outlines — reveal fqGates (draw, ~0.6s). Then each gate lights cyan in narration order: fqMode (~1.4s, on 'What mode?') — gate 1 labeled MODE, glyph: two tiny wave squiggles (compression vs transverse). fqVelocity (~2.4s, on 'What velocity?') — gate 2 labeled VELOCITY, glyph: small speedometer with 'v = ?'. fqTime (~3.6s, on 'Where in time does it sit?') — gate 3 labeled TIME, glyph: timebase ruler with a tick marking the echo position. fqAmplitude (~5.6s, on 'What impedance step explains the amplitude?') — gate 4 labeled AMPLITUDE, glyph: echo peak beside a two-layer boundary (impedance step) with a Z1|Z2 split. fqMotion (~8.0s, on 'Does it move correctly with the probe?') — gate 5 labeled MOTION, glyph: tiny angle-beam probe with left-right arrows and a dashed echo shifting in sync. As each gate lights, the track segment to it brightens, so by ~9s the signal has visibly threaded all five gates. No verdict stamp, no pass/fail text (that lands in the next beat). Keep bottom-center clear: the player drops the note chip 'FIVE QUESTIONS · EVERY SIGNAL MUST ANSWER' there at 9.2s. Beat is ~10s total.

axial-resolution.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan split-panel comparison teaching UT axial (depth) resolution via pulse length. Two mirrored halves; each half shows a contact probe atop a block cross-section containing TWO small reflectors stacked close in depth on the same vertical beam line (e.g., a flaw sitting just ahead of the back wall), with a mini A-scan trace strip beneath the block. LEFT half labeled SHORT PULSE: probe emits a tight, clean 2-cycle wave packet down the beam line; its A-scan shows two crisp separated peaks with a clear valley, small tag TWO PEAKS. RIGHT half labeled LONG PULSE: identical geometry, but the probe emits a long ringing 7-8 cycle packet; its A-scan shows the two echoes smeared into one broad merged hump, small tag ONE HUMP. Base state (before reveals): both blocks, probes, beam lines, and the stacked reflector pairs visible; wave packets, A-scan traces, and tags hidden. Animatable reveals: axrShort = left wave packet + left twin-peak A-scan + TWO PEAKS tag draw in, fired as narration says 'a short pulse keeps two close reflectors as two peaks' (~3-5 s into beat); axrLong = right ringing packet + merged-hump A-scan + ONE HUMP tag, fired at 'a long ringing pulse smears them into one hump' (~7-9 s). Renderer's caption note appears at 9.2 s, so both reveals must be complete by then.

pulse-length-controls.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan diagram of the two levers that shorten an ultrasonic pulse: frequency and damping, plus the broadband/narrowband trade. LEFT third, zone label FREQUENCY: two wave packets with the SAME number of cycles stacked vertically — top labeled 1 MHZ (long stretched packet), bottom labeled 5 MHZ (much shorter packet) — with caption SAME CYCLES, LESS DISTANCE (optional fine print: steel longitudinal wavelengths 5.92 MM vs 1.18 MM, from lesson values). RIGHT two-thirds, zone label DAMPING: two probe cutaways. Top: HEAVY BACKING — dense-filled backing block behind the piezo element, emitted pulse a short 1-2 cycle spike, label BROADBAND. Bottom: LIGHT BACKING — sparse-filled backing, element rings on, emitted pulse a long decaying ring-down, label NARROWBAND. Animatable reveals: plcFreq = the frequency packet comparison draws in, fired as narration says 'Higher frequency' (~2-3 s); plcBacking = both probe cutaways with backing blocks and their emitted pulse shapes, fired at 'a backing block soaks up the ringing' (~4-6 s); plcTrade = verdict tags RESOLVES under the broadband probe and PENETRATES - SENSITIVE under the narrowband probe, fired at 'Broadband resolves; narrowband penetrates' (~7-8 s), just before the renderer note at 8.4 s. Do NOT depict dead zone (narration does not cover it).

dual-element-probe.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Cutaway side view of a dual-element (TR) contact probe seated on a steel section (part fills bottom ~40% as cross-section). One housing, two piezo elements side by side, EACH on its own plastic delay line; a thin cork acoustic-barrier strip runs vertically between them down to the part surface; both elements tilted a few degrees toward each other (roof angle — exaggerate slightly for legibility). Small mono-caps leaders: TRANSMIT, RECEIVE, CORK BARRIER, DELAY LINES, ROOF ANGLE. Below the entry surface the transmit cone (left, angled inward) and receive cone (right, angled inward) cross; the lens-shaped overlap is brighter, labeled PSEUDO-FOCAL ZONE; a small near-surface pit reflector sits inside the overlap just under the surface. Reveals vs beat-2 narration 'A dual probe splits the job: transmit and receive crystals flank a cork barrier, tilted so their beams cross, and near-surface reflectors reappear.' (~8.7 s): depSplit at 0.8 (both elements + cork barrier highlight pulse — synced to 'flank a cork barrier'); depTilt at 2.8 (roof-angle arcs flash, transmit and receive cones draw inward — 'tilted'); depCross at 4.8 (cones intersect, overlap lens brightens to pseudo-focal zone — 'beams cross'); depEcho at 6.8 (near-surface pit pings, echo blip travels back into the receive element — 'near-surface reflectors reappear'). Player note chip lands bottom-center at 8.2 s — keep that area clear.

tandem-path.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Cross-section of thick steel plate (~50 mm implied) with a narrow-gap weld at center; a vertical planar lack-of-fusion flaw stands at mid-wall on the weld sidewall; back wall along the bottom. On the top surface, two identical 45-degree angle-beam probes face the SAME direction — lead probe ahead, trailing probe behind — joined by a holder bar. Faint index-point ticks allowed; do NOT print the spacing formula (not narrated). Reveals vs beat-3 narration 'Pitch-catch: one sends, one receives. Tandem hunts vertical flaws singles miss. The flaw deflects the beam to the back wall; the trailing probe catches it.' (~9.3 s): tdmMiss at 0.8 (ghosted lone single probe fires at the vertical face; beam reflects away and exits with no return; ghost fades out by ~3.2 s — synced to 'vertical flaws singles miss'); tdmFire at 3.4 (lead probe's 45-degree beam draws toward the flaw face); tdmGlance at 5.0 (beam strikes the vertical face and deflects down to the back wall — 'deflects the beam to the back wall'); tdmCatch at 7.0 (final leg climbs from back wall up into the trailing probe, receive ping highlight — 'the trailing probe catches it'). Player note chip lands bottom-center at 8.8 s — keep that area clear.

reactor-core.brief: Title-beat side panel (600x340, holographic navy/cyan): cutaway of a research-reactor pool. Static base: pool walls, water with faint caustics, glowing cyan core lattice at lower center (Cherenkov-style glow). Reveals: rxcCore — core lattice with pulsing glow, visible from 0s while narration opens 'Nobody mines iridium one ninety two'; rxcCapsule — slender aluminum target capsule holding three stacked metal pellets, tagged 'Ir-191 PELLETS', lowers on a cable toward the core starting ~2s under 'every gamma source begins as stable metal'; rxcFlux — neutron particle streaks converge and swirl around the capsule, intensifying ~4s under 'radioactivity we manufacture on purpose', then settling into an ambient loop. Keep composition clean and uncluttered — it is a scene-setting title panel, not the teaching diagram.

neutron-activation.brief: Teaching diagram (600x340, holographic navy/cyan): neutron-capture sequence. Static base: faint drifting flux-particle backdrop (reactor context). Main stage left-of-center: nucleus cluster of ~30 nucleon spheres in two tones (protons/neutrons). Bottom-right inset: mini N-versus-Z chart with the curved stable band brightened and a marker dot sitting ON the band. Reveals vs narration (~9s, 23 words): naNucleus — nucleus with label 'Ir-191 · STABLE', on from 0s ('Neutron activation: in the reactor'); naNeutron — one free neutron streaks in from the left toward the nucleus, ~2.5s ('a stable nucleus captures one neutron'); naCapture — absorption flash, nucleus gains one highlighted neutron sphere, label morphs to 'Ir-192' with a small '+1 MASS' tick, ~4.5-6s ('same element, one mass number heavier') — the element symbol must stay 'Ir' throughout, only the mass number changes; naBand — the inset marker hops OFF the band to the neutron-rich side and a 'RADIOACTIVE' tag lights, ~7.5s ('pushed off the stable band'). Renderer note overlay lands at 8.6s, so all reveals complete by ~8s.

grass-vs-frequency.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan split-screen: the SAME coarse-grained casting wall tested at 5 MHz (left) vs 1 MHz (right). Each half = small probe-on-casting-wall sketch (coarse grain texture in the wall) above an A-scan strip. Mirrors lesson fig ut2-3-2-fig-b. Drives the diagram beat narration 'Coarse cast grains scatter the beam into grass, climbing roughly with frequency to the fourth power. Test castings slow: 1 megahertz strides over the grains.' (~10s; renderer note overlay fires at 7.5s, so the right side must be fully on screen before then). Required animatable reveals: gvfHi (at ~0.5s, on 'scatter the beam into grass' — left half: 5 MHZ label, A-scan baseline filled with tall grass hash and the back-wall echo buried/absent; short-wavelength ripples bouncing off individual grains in the wall sketch); gvfF4 (fade at ~3.5s, on 'frequency to the fourth power' — center callout SCATTER ∝ f⁴ with small '2× f → ~16×' tag, keep glyphs legible at hologram scale); gvfLo (at ~6s, on 'test castings slow: 1 megahertz' — right half: 1 MHZ label, A-scan with low grass, tall clean back echo, one discrete mid-wall indication); gvfStride (draw at ~8s, on 'strides over the grains' — inset on the right wall sketch: one long wavelength arc (~6 mm in steel) stepping across several grains at once).

lamination-ascan.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, split layout. LEFT: side-view plate cross-section with a straight-beam contact probe centered on the top surface, vertical cyan beam column running to the back wall; a thin planar lamination drawn at mid-thickness with a 'T/2' depth tick at the plate edge. RIGHT: A-scan grid (baseline + screen frame). Reveals (timed to 23-word narration, ~9.2 s beat): lamBeam (draw, ~0.6s) probe + beam column light up as narration says 'straight beam, broadside hit'; lamFlaw (draw + ping, ~1.8s) the mid-thickness lamination line glows cyan with its T/2 tick; lamEcho (draw, ~2.8s) a discrete echo rises on the A-scan at the half-screen position, small label 'LAMINATION' — narration 'a lamination echo stands near half thickness'; lamShadow (fade, ~4.8s) the back-wall echo collapses to a faint ghost outline and a hatched shadow region appears below the lamination in the cross-section — narration 'the back wall drops away'; lamMultiples (draw, ~6.6s) a train of evenly spaced repeat echoes at HALF the normal back-wall spacing marches across the A-scan, with a bright spacing bracket labeled 'T/2 SPACING' against a faint ghost bracket showing the normal full-T spacing — narration 'multiples crowd in at half spacing'. Keep the A-scan uncluttered: one lamination echo, ghosted back wall, 3-4 half-spaced multiples. Spec note text appears at 8.2s, so all reveals complete by ~7.5s.

bondline-stack.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cutaway of an adhesively bonded aerospace panel in cross-section (title-beat panel). Composition: left two-thirds is a metal-to-metal stack — thin aluminum face skin (callout ~1 mm) over a darker adhesive bondline over a thicker doubler sheet; right third continues the same skin over a honeycomb core segment (4-5 hex cells with adhesive fillets at the cell walls). Three flat defects, all thin, horizontal, parallel to the surface: a disbond (hairline gap at the skin-adhesive interface), a void (lens-shaped pocket inside the adhesive layer), and a skin-to-core separation (skin lifted off the fillet line over the honeycomb). Beat narration (~20 words, ~8 s): 'Disbonds, voids, core separations — bondline defects lie flat, parallel to the surface, at known depths. Straight-beam sound hits them square.' Required reveals: blsStack (t=0) — layered cutaway fades in with labels SKIN / ADHESIVE / DOUBLER / CORE, defects present but dim. blsFlaws (~1.0s) — the three defects brighten left-to-right with small tags DISBOND, VOID, CORE SEP, synced to the narration enumerating them in that order (matching on-screen title chips). blsDepthTicks (~4.0s) — dashed horizontal guides plus depth ticks from the surface to each defect, emphasizing flat / parallel / known depth, during 'lie flat, parallel to the surface, at known depths'. blsProbe (~6.3s) — straight-beam probe lands on the top surface, vertical cyan beam crosses the layers and strikes the flat defects broadside, during 'Straight-beam sound hits them square.'

bond-ring-compare.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan side-by-side comparison of pulse-echo multiple-echo patterns on a thin bonded skin (mirrors lesson figure ut2-5-2-fig-a; diagram beat). Left half headed GOOD BOND: mini cross-section of skin + adhesive + doubler with a straight-beam probe on top; below it an A-scan trace — initial pulse, then a short train of skin multiples decaying rapidly to baseline. Right half headed DISBOND: same cross-section but an air gap at the skin-adhesive interface (back of skin = metal-to-air); A-scan with a long, persistent train of sharp, evenly spaced multiples running far across the screen. Beat narration (~23 words, ~9 s): 'Pulse-echo over a good bond: multiples die fast — energy drains across the adhesive. Over a disbond, the free skin rings at skin-thickness spacing.' Required reveals: brcGoodTrace (t=0) — left A-scan draws itself with fast-decaying multiples during 'multiples die fast'. brcDrainArrow (~3.2s) — downward energy arrows leak from the skin through the adhesive into the doubler on the left cross-section, during 'energy drains across the adhesive'. brcRingTrace (~5.6s) — right A-scan draws its long ringing train during 'Over a disbond, the free skin rings'. brcSpacingBracket (~7.6s) — bracket between adjacent right-side peaks labeled SKIN THICKNESS, during 'at skin-thickness spacing' (beat note banner lands at 8.2 s; keep bracket clear of the bottom note strip).

marker-overlap.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Top: a pipe girth weld unrolled into a horizontal weld band, punch-marked ZERO reference at left, lead numeral markers 1-2-3-4 sitting on the part at stations. Below: three abutting film strips (FILM A, FILM B, FILM C) under the band. Film A's right end and Film B's left end BOTH carry the printed image of marker 2 (overlap proven); the B/C junction shares no marker numeral. Animatable reveals vs narration (24 words, ~9.5s beat): mkMarkers at ~1.2s — lead numerals + zero punch mark light up on the part as VO says 'lead location markers map film to part'; mkFilms at ~3.6s — film strips slide in beneath the part ('abutting films'); mkShared at ~5.0s — marker 2's image pulses on BOTH abutting film ends with a cyan tie-line ('must share a marker image'); mkGap at ~7.2s — red dashed bracket over the unshared B/C junction length, tag 'UNPROVEN' ('that length is unproven'). The beat's note overlay lands at 7.8s, so mkGap must be visible before it.

density-window-check.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A weld radiograph strip glowing on an illuminator with a hole-type IQI plaque alongside the weld image; a transmission densitometer head with small aperture and digital readout; vertical density scale on the right. Animatable reveals vs narration (25 words, ~10s beat): dwcMeter at ~1.0s — densitometer head appears with a small 'STEP-TABLET VERIFIED' check tag (VO: 'a verified densitometer'); dwcIqi at ~3.0s — aperture lands on the IQI plaque body between the holes, away from the ID numbers, readout shows 2.40 (VO: 'reads the I Q I body'); dwcSpots at ~5.2s — three reading pings along the weld area of interest (VO: 'every weld reading'); dwcBand at ~7.0s — on the density scale, a 2.40 reference tick plus shaded acceptance band from 2.04 (labeled -15%) to 3.12 (labeled +30%), the weld pings tick green inside it (VO: 'stays within minus fifteen, plus thirty percent of it'). Numbers must be exactly 2.40 / 2.04 / 3.12 — the lesson's worked example. Note overlay lands at 8.2s.

couplant-film.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section: split left/right comparison of a contact probe wear face seated on rough steel — left panel tagged DRY, right tagged COUPLED. Base art (drawn at beat start, no reveal): both probes seated on an exaggerated jagged surface profile; on the LEFT a hatched trapped-air gap between wear face and steel valleys labeled TRAPPED AIR, with the incident sound arrow folding straight back at the gap tagged '>99.9% REFLECTED — NO TEST' (static context carrying over the title beat's hook). Reveals (beat 2 narration, ~9.8s total: 'A couplant fixes it. A thin water film displaces the air and about twelve percent of the energy crosses — enough to run the test.'): cfFilm (fade, ~1.8s, on 'thin water film displaces the air') — translucent cyan couplant flows into the RIGHT gap, filling every valley as a visibly THIN continuous film labeled THIN WATER FILM; the hatched air vanishes on that side. cfEnergy (draw, ~4.8s, on 'about twelve percent of the energy crosses') — bold transmitted arrow continuing down into the steel tagged '≈12% IN' plus a thinner reflected arrow returning tagged '≈88% BACK'. cfEcho (fade, ~7.8s, on 'enough to run the test') — small backwall-echo arrow returning up through the film to the wear face with a glowing signal blip, signalling a live test. Do NOT print impedance values (46 / 1.5 / 0.0004 MRayl) — narration never covers them. Keep the lower third clear: the beat note 'COUPLANT DISPLACES AIR → ~12% ENTERS STEEL' lands at 9.2s.
---
Dry-vs-coupled comparison of the probe-to-steel interface, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Base scene (always visible): two identical cross-section panels side by side, each a contact probe (housing + crystal stub, face down) resting on a steel block, captions DRY (left) and COUPLED (right); bottom-center left open for the player's note chip at 8.5s. Narration timing (25 words, ~9.5s): 'Sound must physically cross into the steel' 0-2.4s; 'but a trapped air layer' 2.4-3.6s; 'reflects nearly all of it back' 3.6-5.6s; 'Couplant fills that gap' 5.9-7.4s; 'so energy crosses' 7.4-9.4s. Reveals: cfDryPulse (draw, 1.0) sound-pulse arrow leaving the LEFT probe face toward the interface; cfAirGap (fade, 2.8) magnified inset strip at the left interface showing a microscopically thin trapped layer labeled AIR between probe face and steel; cfReflect (draw, 4.0) bold arrow bouncing back up off the left interface with only a faint ghost stub passing below (nothing crosses); cfReflectTag (fade, 4.8) tag '≈100% REFLECTED'; cfFilm (fade, 6.2) glowing cyan couplant film flooding the RIGHT interface gap, small tag COUPLANT; cfTransmit (draw, 7.4) strong beam crossing the right interface and traveling down to the block's back wall; cfCrossPing (ping, 8.2) ping at the right interface where the energy crosses.

exposure-correction-chain.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A field exposure-correction chain read left to right, used in a ~9.5s diagram beat whose narration is: 'One factor at a time, then multiply. Activity halved? Eight minutes becomes sixteen. Thirty to forty inches? Squared: times one point seven eight.' Static base layer: faint technique-sheet header strip across the top reading 'SHEET: 8 MIN / 60 Ci / 30 IN SFD' (the baseline conditions). Animatable reveals, in narration order: eccBase (fade ~0.6s) leftmost glass card '8 MIN' with a small clipboard/sheet glyph — visible while narration opens 'One factor at a time, then multiply'; eccDecay (fade ~2.6s) a '× 2' operator plus a decay card showing a small falling activity curve labeled '60 -> 30 Ci' — lands on 'Activity halved?'; eccDecayOut (fade ~4.0s) running-total chip '16 MIN' beneath the decay card — lands on 'Eight minutes becomes sixteen'; eccDist (fade ~5.4s) a '× 1.78' operator plus a distance card showing a source point with two radius arcs labeled '30 IN' and '40 IN' and the formula '(40 / 30)^2 = 1.78' — lands on 'Thirty to forty inches? Squared'; eccTotal (fade ~7.4s) end-of-chain result chip '~ 28.5 MIN' with a pencil-stroke underline suggesting each factor written on the technique sheet — lands just before the beat's on-screen note 'ONE FACTOR AT A TIME, THEN MULTIPLY' at 7.8s. Do NOT include a density slot: this beat's narration covers decay and distance only (density is handled by the preceding tiles beat). Numbers come from the lesson's worked example: 8 min at 60 Ci and 30 in becomes 16 min after decay (60/30 = 2), then ~28.5 min after the distance move ((40/30)^2 = 1.78).

magnetic-domains.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, series style. A rectangular steel bar cross-section (centered, ~460x180) tiled with ~20 irregular polygonal domain cells, each holding a short bold arrow; a large applied-field arrow labeled H enters from the left when cued; S/N pole caps on the bar ends. Timed to mt1-2-2 beat 2 narration (~9.5 s): 'Steel is packed with tiny magnets: domains. Unmagnetized means scrambled, not empty. Apply a magnetizing force and they line up, giving the part poles.' Reveals: domBar (fade 0.4) bar outline + domain cell walls as the line 'Steel is packed with tiny magnets: domains' opens; domArrows (fade 0.9) per-cell arrows pointing random directions with soft per-cell glow; domNetZero (fade 3.4) dim 'NET FIELD = 0' chip near the bar ends while narration says 'Unmagnetized means scrambled, not empty'; domField (draw 5.2) applied magnetizing-force H arrow sweeps in from the left on 'Apply a magnetizing force'; domAlign (fade 6.6) all domain arrows rotate and settle pointing right in a quick cascade on 'they line up' (net-zero chip fades out); domPoles (fade 8.0) S cap glows on left end, N cap on right end, plus faint external field arcs, on 'giving the part poles'. On-screen note overlay lands at 8.3 — keep the lower-third clear. No text besides H, N, S, and the NET FIELD = 0 chip.

photon-fates.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan; title-beat hero panel. Compact industrial X-ray tube head with collimator cone at left emitting a beam rightward into a vertical steel slab shown in cross-section (subtle hatch, small STEEL label). Three photon tracks at different heights demonstrate the three fates. Beat narration (20 words, ~7.7s): 'Radiography hangs on one question: what happens when a photon meets matter? Through, absorbed, or scattered. Three mechanisms decide everything.' Reveals: pfSource (fade 0.5) tube head + collimator; pfSlab (fade 0.8) steel slab; pfBeam (draw 1.4) incident rays travel toward the slab, syncing with 'a photon meets matter'; pfThrough (draw 5.0) top track passes straight through and exits right unchanged, small label THROUGH ('Through'); pfAbsorbed (draw 5.5) middle track terminates inside the slab with a small energy-deposit flash, no exit, label ABSORBED ('absorbed'); pfScattered (draw 6.1) bottom track kinks at a point inside the slab and exits downward at a new angle, drawn dimmer after the kink, label SCATTERED ('scattered'). Keep labels small and leave the lower-right corner clear for the player's panelLabel chip.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan title panel: a collimated gamma photon stream from an emitter at left meets a steel block center; a film plane glows at right. Three photon fates drawn as distinct traced paths through/into the block. Reveal ids and timing vs rt2-1-1 beat 1 narration (~9 s): base scene (emitter, steel block, film plane) on entry during 'Welcome to Level Two' (0-1.5 s); pfThrough = one photon passing straight through to expose the film (1.5-3 s, 'you already know what photons do in steel'); pfAbsorb = one photon terminating inside the block in a small photoelectric flash with an ejected electron spark (3-4 s); pfScatter = one photon kinking at an electron and exiting at a new angle, dimmer, missing the film (4-5 s); pfLoop = ambient re-fire shimmer loop for the remainder ('now you'll predict what a technique does before you burn film', 5-9 s). NOTE: converge with rt1-3-1, which uses photon-fates as its title panel for 'through, absorbed, or scattered — three mechanisms'; the same three reveal paths serve both lessons, so build once.

photon-interactions.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan; the core teaching diagram. Three vignettes left-to-right captioned PHOTOELECTRIC, COMPTON, PAIR PRODUCTION. Beat narration (24 words, ~9.2s): 'Photoelectric: the photon vanishes, totally absorbed. Compton: it survives, scattered with less energy. Above 1.022 mega electron volts, pair production makes an electron-positron pair.' Reveals: phoPeAtom (fade 0.3) atom with nucleus + two electron shells, incoming wavy photon arrow aimed at an inner-shell electron ('Photoelectric:'); phoPeAbsorb (draw 1.0) photon track terminates at the inner-shell electron with a small flash — the photon visibly ends, nothing exits ('vanishes, totally absorbed'); phoPeEject (draw 1.7) photoelectron ejected outward from the inner shell. phoCoAtom (fade 2.7) second atom, incoming photon aimed at a loosely bound outer-shell electron ('Compton: it survives'); phoCoScatter (draw 3.7) recoil electron knocked out PLUS scattered photon exiting at a new angle, drawn with longer wavelength and dimmer stroke to read as lower energy ('scattered with less energy'). phoPpNucleus (fade 5.3) bare nucleus with field glow + incoming photon ('Above'); phoPpThreshold (fade 5.8) small chip '>= 1.022 MeV' beside the incoming photon ('1.022 mega electron volts'); phoPpPair (draw 7.0) photon track forks into e- and e+ curving in opposite directions, photon gone ('makes an electron-positron pair'). Do NOT depict positron annihilation — the narration does not cover it. Keep the bottom-center strip clear for the player's note chip at 8.2s ('ABSORBED · SCATTERED · CONVERTED TO MATTER').

bond-cscan-overlay.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan panel: plan-view through-transmission C-scan of a rectangular bonded doubler with an acceptance overlay (mirrors lesson fig ut2-5-3-fig-a). STATIC BASE: doubler outline with a datum corner and axis tick marks, faint fastener-row hint, and color-coded disbond blobs — fine sub-threshold speckle, three or four small indications (8-20 mm scale), one clearly oversize blob mid-panel, and one small blob near the doubler edge. REVEALS (ids, in narration order against: 'On the C-scan, apply all four: log recordables, check the single-size limit, total area against the density limit, and police the edge zones', beat ~9.5 s): bcoRecord at ~2.4 s ('log recordables') — cyan circles + small ID tags pop onto every indication at/above the recording threshold (6 mm illustrative); speckle stays untagged to show the threshold gate. bcoSingle at ~3.9 s ('check the single-size limit') — a dashed max-diameter ring (25 mm illustrative) snaps over the oversize blob with a caliper-style measure reading OVER; smaller ones implicitly pass. bcoDensity at ~5.7 s ('total area against the density limit') — readout chip totals recorded area as percent of bond area vs the limit, e.g. '3.8% / LIMIT 5%'. bcoEdge at ~7.5 s ('police the edge zones') — a hatched edge-zone band (13 mm illustrative) glows along the doubler perimeter and the indication inside it flags REJECT regardless of its small size. Numeric values are the lesson table's illustrative Class B numbers — render them small/subordinate, the visual logic carries the teaching. Renderer note appears at 8.6 s, so all reveals must be settled by ~8.4 s.

casting-radiograph.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A simulated casting radiograph: large dark film rectangle with rounded corners clipped over a glowing illuminator edge, faint scan-line texture, thin cyan rim light. Base art (always visible, drawn faintly so reveals can highlight): upper-left a cluster of small round smooth dark spots (gas porosity); center an irregular ragged-edged dark patch (shrinkage); lower-right a fine, straight, sharp-edged dark hairline (crack); mid-right a small distinctly LIGHT bright spot (high-density inclusion). Reveals timed to beat-2 narration (approx 9.5s total): craDarker at ~2.0s on 'prints darker' - cyan callout arrow + tag 'VOID -> DARKER' pointing at a dark rounded spot, spot brightens momentarily; craLighter at ~4.2s on 'print lighter' - callout tag 'DENSE -> LIGHTER' pointing at the light spot; craPorosity at ~6.6s on 'porosity round' - dashed ring around the round-spot cluster, label 'POROSITY - ROUND'; craShrink at ~7.6s on 'shrinkage irregular' - dashed ring around the ragged patch, label 'SHRINKAGE - IRREGULAR'; craCrack at ~8.6s on 'cracks sharp' - dashed ring tracing the hairline, label 'CRACK - SHARP'. Labels small mono uppercase; reveals appear in exactly this order.

reference-plate-grading.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Top-down view of a high-intensity illuminator lightbox. Left two-thirds: a row of five small graded reference-radiograph plates, each a film card bearing the same porosity-dot motif at increasing severity (sparser/smaller dots on plate 1 to dense/large on plate 5), numbered chips 1-5 beneath, 1 leftmost. Right third: a slightly larger 'PRODUCTION FILM' card with scattered indications, one region visibly worst. Reveals timed to beat-4 narration (approx 9.5s total): rpgFan at ~1.2s on 'graded, typically one through five' - the five plates slide/fan into the row, number chips 1-5 light sequentially left to right; rpgSpec at ~4.2s on 'beside the specified level' - plate 3 elevates and glows with tag 'SPEC LEVEL 3' (illustrative example level) while the production film docks beside it; rpgWorst at ~6.2s on 'judge the worst area' - dashed cyan rectangle pulses around the worst region of the production film, label 'WORST AREA'; rpgVerdict at ~8.2s on 'equal or better passes' - comparison glyph '<= SPEC PLATE' between film and plate 3 plus a small PASS stamp. Renderer's note overlay 'EQUAL OR BETTER -> PASS - WORSE -> FAIL' lands at 8.4s, so keep the lower-third band clear.

rt-exposure-geometry.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Canonical RT shadow-projection layout shared across RT lessons (used here as the looping title panel, labeled SOURCE // PART // FILM). Left: gamma/X-ray source head drawn with finite width and a small F size tick. Divergent beam cone rightward through a plate-with-weld part at mid-frame; film cassette line tight behind the part at right. The part's internal feature prints as a slightly enlarged shadow on the film, with a subtle penumbra fringe at the shadow edge. Dimension ticks: d (source to object) and t (object to film). Keep labels symbolic (F, d, t) — no baked-in numbers — and give it gentle ambient shimmer suitable for a title panel loop. Reveals, early-loaded for an ~8.5 s title beat against narration 'Every radiograph is a shadow picture. One source size, two distances — three numbers decide sharpness before a single photon reaches the film': regSource fade 0.5 s (source head + F tick — 'one source size'); regBeam draw 1.0 s (divergent cone — 'shadow picture'); regPart fade 1.4 s (part + internal feature); regFilm fade 1.8 s (film line + cast shadow); regDims fade 3.2 s (d and t arrows together — 'two distances'); regUg fade 5.0 s (penumbra fringe tag — 'decide sharpness'). NOTE: also referenced by rt1-2-4 (title panel) and rt1-6-2 (enlargement diagram, note IMAGE = OBJECT x SFD / SOD) — merge briefs into one drawing and keep reveal ids stable.
---
Canonical RT exposure scene (source/part/film + Ug), 600x340 holographic navy/cyan — designed as the reusable series asset; in rt1-2-4 it runs as the ambient TITLE panel (panelLabel 'SOURCE // PART // FILM'), so it must read as scene-setting with no narration sync required. Layout: compact source head top-left with a bright focal-spot dot labeled SOURCE; diverging beam cone down-right through a steel plate with a girth-weld stub and one internal void, plate labeled PART; film cassette below labeled FILM, with the void's projected dark image forming on it. Subtle penumbra ticks at the image edges with a small UG chip — keep understated here; later geometric-unsharpness lessons will lean on it. Reveals (ambient ~12 s scene loop): regSource at 0.8 (source head + focal dot); regBeam at 1.6 (cone draws); regPart at 2.6 (plate + weld); regFlaw at 3.4 (internal void pulses); regFilm at 4.4 (cassette); regImage at 5.6 (projected spot develops); regUg at 8.0 (penumbra ticks + UG chip, subtle). Title-beat narration for context only: 'Same photon, two birthplaces. X rays are made in a machine; gamma rays are born in a nucleus. That decides what you control.'
---
Projection-enlargement geometry (side view), 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Base art: film plane along the bottom. Point source (small bright focal-spot dot in a tube-housing hint) top center. A rectangular object slab floats at mid-height with a visible air gap to the film. Beat narration (~9.5s): 'Rays spread from the source, so the shadow is always enlarged — object size times the distance ratio. Film tight against the part keeps it true.' Reveals: xgSource fade 0.6 (source dot + housing — lands on 'rays spread from the source'); xgRays draw 1.2 (two diverging rays from the source past the object's edges down to the film); xgPart fade 2.2 (object slab with width tag OBJECT); xgShadow fade 3.2 (shadow band on the film clearly WIDER than the object, dashed projectors tying object edges to shadow edges + width tag IMAGE — lands on 'always enlarged'); xgSOD fade 4.8 (right-side dimension line, source to object, labeled SOD — lands on 'distance ratio'); xgSFD fade 5.5 (longer dimension line, source to film, labeled SFD); xgContact fade 7.0 (small bottom-right inset: same object resting ON the film, shadow width = object width, tag CONTACT → TRUE SIZE — lands on 'film tight against the part keeps it true'). IMPORTANT: crisp shadow edges, NO penumbra/fuzz in this asset (the geometric-unsharpness asset owns penumbra). Keep bottom-center clear for the player's note chip 'IMAGE = OBJECT × SFD ÷ SOD' at 8.0s.

geometric-unsharpness.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Similar-triangles construction of geometric unsharpness. Left: finite-width source drawn as a short vertical pellet stack (explicitly NOT a point) with a bracket labeled F. Center: part/weld block edge (object plane). Right: film plane line just behind the part with a small visible gap. Rays from the TOP and BOTTOM extremes of the source graze the same object edge and diverge to the film, opening a shaded band at the film plane: solid umbra plus hatched penumbra tagged Ug. Dimension arrows below: d (source to object) and t (object to film). Formula chip top-right: Ug = F · t / d. Keep all labels symbolic (F, t, d, Ug — no baked-in numbers) so multiple lessons reuse it; lesson-specific numbers arrive via each beat's note overlay (this lesson's note at 7.2 s: '0.150 x 2.0 / 22 = 0.014 — TYPICAL CAP 0.020'). Reveals vs rt2-8-2 narration 'Run one: source fifteen hundredths of an inch, gap two inches, standoff twenty-two — fourteen thousandths of unsharpness, inside the typical twenty-thousandths cap' (~8.5 s beat): guSource fade 0.6 s (pellet-stack source + F bracket — 'source fifteen hundredths'); guPart fade 1.2 s (object block + film line); guT fade 2.2 s (t arrow — 'gap two inches'); guD fade 3.2 s (d arrow — 'standoff twenty-two'); guRays draw 4.2 s (extreme rays opening the wedge); guPenumbra fade 5.2 s (hatched penumbra band + Ug tag — 'fourteen thousandths of unsharpness'); guFormula fade 6.2 s (Ug = F · t / d chip, holds under the cap line and the 7.2 s note). NOTE: also referenced by rt1-6-2 and rt2-1-2 — merge briefs into one drawing and keep reveal ids stable.
---
Penumbra formation at one object edge (side view), 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Base art: film plane along the bottom; object slab at mid-height with its right edge near frame center. Beat narration (~9s): 'A real source has width, so edges blur into a penumbra. Unsharpness equals source size, times object-to-film distance, divided by source-to-object distance.' Reveals: ugSource fade 0.6 (a finite-width source BAR at top — visibly not a point — lands on 'a real source has width'); ugF fade 1.2 (dimension across the bar labeled F); ugRays draw 2.0 (two rays from the LEFT end and two from the RIGHT end of the bar, all grazing the same object edge but landing at different film points); ugUmbra fade 3.0 (solid dark shadow core on the film under the object); ugPenumbra fade 3.7 (fuzzy gradient band between the disagreeing ray landings, labeled PENUMBRA, with a small width callout Ug — lands on 'blur into a penumbra'); ugT fade 6.0 (right-side dimension t from the object's SOURCE-SIDE surface down to the film — lands on 'object-to-film distance'); ugD fade 6.9 (dimension d from the source down to the object's source-side surface — lands on 'source-to-object distance'). Per lesson convention, t and d are both measured to the source side of the part. Keep bottom-center clear for the player's note chip 'PENUMBRA → Ug = F·t ÷ d' at 7.6s.

projection-magnification.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Deliberate projection-enlargement geometry (NEW asset; this is the lesson's signature visual). Left: X-ray tube tip with a visibly tiny focal-spot dot. A small part (electronics package or thin investment casting with one internal void) sits at the MIDPOINT of the span; flat digital detector panel at far right, clearly pulled away from the part with an open air gap. The projection cone continues past the part and paints a 2x ENLARGED shadow of the internal void on the detector. Equal-length dimension arrows: d (source to part) and t (part to detector), with chip M = (d + t) / d = 2X. Bottom inset strip — two side-by-side edge profiles of the same enlarged image: left tagged CONVENTIONAL SPOT with the edge smeared by a wide penumbra gradient; right tagged MICROFOCUS with a crisp edge; small tag UG = F WITH PART MIDWAY. Reveals vs rt2-8-2 narration 'Flip the rule: pull the detector away to enlarge. Part midway doubles the image, but unsharpness equals your focal spot size — enlargement demands microfocus' (~9 s beat): pjmSetup fade 0.5 s (tube, part, detector); pjmDetector fade/slide 1.2 s (detector pulled right, air gap opens — 'pull the detector away'); pjmCone draw 1.9 s (cone painting the 2x shadow — 'to enlarge'); pjmMag fade 3.0 s (d and t arrows + M = (d + t) / d = 2X chip — 'part midway doubles the image'); pjmBlurBad fade 4.8 s (conventional-spot smeared edge — 'unsharpness equals your focal spot size'); pjmBlurGood fade 6.4 s (microfocus crisp edge — 'enlargement demands microfocus'). Beat note overlay lands at 7.4 s ('PART MIDWAY: 2X, BUT Ug = FOCAL SPOT').

luminance-ladder.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Left-to-right: illuminator port icon emitting a light beam through a film strip bearing three progressively darker density patches labeled D 2.0, D 3.0, D 4.0, ending at an eye icon with chip '30 CD/M2 AT THE EYE'. Right: three vertical bars on a logarithmic ladder showing required illuminator output 3,000 / 30,000 / 300,000 CD/M2, stepped heights with small 'x10' arrows between bars, thin mono numerals. Sync to narration 'Density is logarithmic. Delivering 30 candela per square meter through density two takes about 3,000 from the illuminator; through three, 30,000; through four, 300,000.' Reveals: lumPath (beam path illuminator -> film -> eye draws) at ~0.6s on 'Density is logarithmic'; lumTarget (eye chip '30 CD/M2 AT THE EYE' fades in) at ~1.8s on 'delivering 30 candela per square meter'; lumBar2 (D 2.0 patch highlights, 3,000 bar rises) at ~4.4s on 'takes about 3,000'; lumBar3 (D 3.0 patch highlights, x10 arrow, 30,000 bar rises) at ~6.6s on 'through three, 30,000'; lumBar4 (D 4.0 patch — rendered nearly opaque against the glowing port — x10 arrow, 300,000 bar rises) at ~8.0s on 'through four, 300,000'. On-screen note arrives at 8.6s; keep lower-third area clean for it.

composite-viewing.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Two translucent radiograph films floating side by side over a soft illuminator glow, identical faint weld images in register, each with a density chip: 'D 1.4' (left) and 'D 1.5' (right). They glide together and superimpose into one visibly darker stacked film tagged 'D ≈ 2.9', with equation row '1.4 + 1.5 ≈ 2.9' beneath; transmitted-light arrows passing through thin out as the stack darkens. Sync to narration 'Composite viewing: superimpose two films and densities add — 1.4 plus 1.5 reads about 2.9. Typical rules: each film at least 1.3, the stack 2.6.' Reveals: cvwFilmA (left film + 'D 1.4' chip fades in) at ~0.8s on 'Composite viewing'; cvwFilmB (right film + 'D 1.5' chip fades in) at ~1.6s on 'two films'; cvwMerge (films glide together and superimpose, stack darkens, light arrows thin) at ~3.0s on 'densities add'; cvwSum (equation row '1.4 + 1.5 ≈ 2.9' and 'D ≈ 2.9' tag draw on) at ~4.8s on 'reads about 2.9'. Do NOT draw the 1.3/2.6 code minimums in the asset — the beat's on-screen note (EACH FILM ≥ 1.3 · SUPERIMPOSED ≥ 2.6, at 8.6s) carries them; keep the lower-third clean after ~6.5s. Weld images must stay perfectly registered when stacked.

calibration-block.brief:
IIW Type 1 (V1) calibration block in side profile, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, with an angle-beam probe + wedge coupled on the top surface — animates the four-step angle-beam standardization sequence. Base state (draws with the scan sweep, 0-1.2s): block silhouette with the 100-mm radius quadrant on the left, slot at the radius center, engraved degree scale along the lower-right edge, embedded acrylic insert disc, small 1.5-mm SDH near the top edge, probe/wedge sitting above the radius focal point. Reveals vs narration 'Angle beam follows a fixed order: exit point, then refracted angle, then range, then sensitivity. Geometry wrong means everything after is wrong.' (~9s beat): cbExit (draw, ~2.5s) beam ray from wedge to the 100-mm radius and back plus a bright tick on the wedge at the true exit point + cbExitLbl (fade, ~2.7s) tag '1 · EXIT POINT' — lands on the words 'exit point'; cbAngle (draw, ~3.6s) ray re-aimed through the acrylic insert with a highlight sweep along the degree scale and an angle arc at the exit-point tick + cbAngleLbl (fade, ~3.8s) '2 · REFRACTED ANGLE' — lands on 'refracted angle'; cbRange (draw, ~4.7s) echo arc returning from the radius with a small '100 mm' distance callout + cbRangeLbl (fade, ~4.9s) '3 · RANGE' — lands on 'range'; cbSens (ping + fade, ~5.5s) the 1.5-mm SDH glows with a small 80%-FSH reference-bar chip + cbSensLbl (fade, ~5.7s) '4 · SENSITIVITY' — lands on 'sensitivity'. Keep lower-center clear for the player's note chip at 7.9s ('EXIT POINT → ANGLE → RANGE → SENSITIVITY'). IMPORTANT: ut1-3-2 reuses this asset as a static title HUD panel (label 'IIW // KNOWN REFLECTORS'), so the un-revealed base state must read as a complete, recognizable IIW block on its own.
---
IIW Type 1 calibration block, holographic navy/cyan, 600x340. Side profile of the classic IIW shape: long rectangular body, 100 mm radius quadrant at one end (small '100 mm R' tag), engraved degree scale along the lower edge, small reference hole, and an angle-beam probe with wedge resting on the top scan surface. Used as AMBIENT TITLE-PANEL art behind narration 'Your flaw detector measures only time and voltage. Standardization converts them into distance and amplitude you can act on.' (~8s) — motion must be subtle and loopable. Reveals: cbBlock = block outline plus engravings, visible from start; cbProbe = probe and wedge fade in on the scan face at ~1s; cbPing = animated sound path arcing from the probe exit point to the 100 mm radius face and back, gently pulsing in a loop from ~2s onward. No other text labels.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. IIW Type 1 calibration block in profile: the signature 100 mm radius arc on one end, engraved degree scale (35-70 markings), engraved zero line, a small side-drilled hole, and an angle-beam probe sitting with its exit point over the zero line aimed at the radius. Used as a title-scene HUD panel (beat 1, label 'IIW BLOCK // KNOWN ECHOES'), so it needs simple looping animation rather than narration-timed reveals: calSweep = faint beam line sweeping from the probe exit point out to the 100 mm radius arc and back (loop ~4s); calPing = recurring echo ping/glow where the beam meets the radius arc (~1.8s into each loop). NOTE: spec ut1-3-2 also references 'calibration-block' as its title panel; converge on one drawing that serves both — this brief is compatible with generic title-panel use.

swinging-field.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A shaft-like production part clamped horizontally between two bench contact headstocks, with an encircling coil around its midsection — a multidirectional wet-bench unit seen slightly from above. Both circuits drawn but de-emphasized at start. Animatable reveals synced to beat-2 narration (24 words, ~8.5 s): swgDim at 0.8 — backplate/grid dims, part and both circuits sit in low emphasis while narration says 'independent magnetizing circuits share the part'. swgCirc at 3.0 — head-shot circuit energizes as narration says 'a head shot drives the circular field': contact pads glow, current arrow runs through the part, cyan circular flux rings wrap around the part's circumference, small mono label 'HEAD SHOT - CIRCULAR'. swgLong at 5.5 — coil circuit energizes as narration says 'a built-in coil the longitudinal': coil turns glow, axial field arrows run along the part length, label 'COIL - LONGITUDINAL'. swgAlt at 7.2 — as narration says 'alternating many times each second': the two field systems strobe in rapid alternation (looping swing/flicker between circular rings and axial arrows), and tiny crack ticks at several angles on the part surface each catch a particle indication as the field roughly perpendicular to them passes. Spec note text appears separately at 8.2, so keep the lower-right corner clear.

last-field-wins.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Why multidirectional magnetization is continuous-method only: a part cannot retain two field directions — whichever circuit fired last sets the residual field. Layout: a part bar with two perpendicular surface cracks (one parallel to the axis, one transverse), plus a shot-train timeline strip above it reading C - L - C - L (circular/longitudinal chips). Animatable reveals synced to beat-3 narration (23 words, ~8.3 s): lfwShots at 1.2 — the shot-train chips fire in sequence left to right while narration says 'the part keeps only the last direction fired'. lfwLast at 2.8 — the train ends; a residual-field arrow remains for the LAST direction only, while the other direction's field ghosts out with a strike-through; of the two perpendicular cracks, only the one crossed by the surviving field stays highlighted, the other goes bare with a small alert flag (residual application would miss it). lfwLive at 5.6 — as narration says 'particles must be there, mobile, while the fields alternate': bath droplets/flow lines wash over the part WHILE the shot-train strip re-strobes C/L, and now BOTH cracks build bright particle indications with a check mark. Keep it readable as one idea: bath after the shots = one direction kept; bath during the alternation = every direction caught. Spec note text appears at 7.8, so keep the lower-right corner clear.

nonmetal-parts.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan title-scene panel: bench lineup of the two non-metal parts the narration names. LEFT: cutaway of a rubber-lined steel pipe spool — bright steel shell ring with a thick darker inner elastomer layer, small mono label RUBBER LINING. RIGHT: circular glass sight-window disc shown on edge with faint specular streaks, label SIGHT GLASS. A small straight-beam contact probe hovers above the spool. Before ~4s show only a faint bench grid so the parts' arrival reads as a reveal. Reveals vs narration (23 words ≈ 9 s: 'Sooner or later someone hands you a part that isn't metal: a rubber lining, a glass window. Ultrasound can test it. Here's how.'): nmpRubber at ~4.3s — spool fades/glows in on 'a rubber lining'; nmpGlass at ~5.4s — glass disc fades in on 'a glass window'; nmpProbe at ~6.6s on 'Ultrasound can test it' — probe settles onto the spool; nmpPing — looping soft concentric pulse ripples from the probe into the lining from ~6.8s onward. Minimal mono uppercase labels only.

ringdown-bond.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan teaching diagram of the multiple-echo ring-down lining bond test, examined from the STEEL side. Lower half: horizontal cross-section band — steel shell on top (label STEEL), rubber lining beneath (label RUBBER); straight-beam probe with couplant film on the steel top surface. Two zones side by side: LEFT bonded (rubber tight to steel), RIGHT disbonded with a thin bright air gap between steel and rubber (label AIR GAP).
