Above each zone a mini A-scan trace: LEFT a multiple-echo train collapsing within a few multiples (each successive echo markedly shorter) with small arrows showing energy leaking into the rubber at every bounce (lesson: ~15% escapes per hit, never returns); RIGHT a long, persistent, slowly decaying train of tall multiples (steel-to-air reflects essentially everything). Reveals vs narration (24 words ≈ 9 s: 'The classic job: checking a rubber lining's bond from the steel side. Bonded rubber drains energy, so echoes die fast. A disbond rings on.'): rbdSetup at ~0.6s — cross-section + probe draw in while narration establishes 'from the steel side'; rbdBonded at ~4.4s on 'Bonded rubber drains energy, so echoes die fast' — left zone highlights, fast-decay A-scan sweeps in, leak arrows pulse; rbdDisbond at ~6.9s on 'A disbond rings on' — air gap flashes, right A-scan sweeps in the long ringing train. Spec note lands at 8.2s — keep the lower-right corner clear. Animate echo trains as sweeps in the style of a-scan-display.

grain-flow-map.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Split layout teaching grain-flow orientation and perpendicular scanning. Beat narration (~10 s): 'Stringers, bursts, and flakes all lie parallel to flow. So aim the beam perpendicular: shafts radially from the outside, disks axially from the face.' LEFT ~60%: longitudinal cutaway of a forged shaft (horizontal cylinder) with fibrous axial grain-flow lines visible from the start, bending around the contour. Reveals, in narration order: gfStringer (draw, ~0.6s) thin elongated dash riding a flow line at upper mid-radius, micro-label STRINGER; gfBurst (fade+ping, ~1.3s) ragged elongated tear ON the centerline, deep in section, micro-label BURST; gfFlakes (fade, ~2.0s) cluster of short flat bright dashes in the mid-radius-to-center zone, micro-label FLAKES — all three flaw types drawn parallel to the flow lines (technically required). gfPerp (fade, ~4.9s) small 90-degree right-angle tick where the coming beam crosses a flow line, cueing 'aim the beam perpendicular'. gfBeamRadial (draw, ~5.7s) straight-beam probe on the shaft OD firing a cyan beam radially down across the flow lines, label RADIAL, synced to 'shafts radially from the outside'. RIGHT ~40%: gfDisk (fade, ~7.3s) upset disk shown as a short cylinder cutaway with flow lines fanning radially outward like tree-stump grain and thin flat flaw dashes lying parallel to the end faces; gfBeamAxial (draw, ~8.0s) probe on the flat top face firing an axial beam through the thickness, label AXIAL, synced to 'disks axially from the face'. Spec note chip appears at 9.2s (handled by renderer). Keep flaw geometry honest: burst = centerline; flakes = mid-radius/center cluster; every elongated flaw axis follows the local flow direction.

forging-press.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, ambient TITLE PANEL (loops behind beat 1). Open-die press scene: massive flat upper die above a glowing hot billet resting on a flat lower die, faint heat shimmer, press frame suggested at edges. Title narration (~8 s): 'Forging kneads hot metal into shape — and whatever it cannot heal, it stretches out along the flow. That's your map.' Reveals: fpStroke (translate+ping, ~0.8s) upper die presses down a short stroke and the billet bulges laterally, synced to 'kneads hot metal into shape'; fpFlowLines (draw, ~4.8s) fibrous grain-flow lines sweep through the billet along the stretch direction, synced to 'stretches out along the flow'; fpStringer (draw, ~5.6s) one small round inclusion blob visibly elongates into a thin stringer aligned with the new flow lines, synced to 'whatever it cannot heal'; fpMapTag (fade, ~7.2s) faint micro-label GRAIN FLOW on the fibers at 'That's your map.' Subtle looping glow/shimmer after reveals is fine; panelLabel chip 'FORGE // OPEN-DIE PRESS' is rendered by the player, not the asset.

time-temp-chart.brief: Time-temperature development chart (600x340, holographic navy/cyan). X axis: developer temperature 56-78 °F; Y axis: development time 2-10 minutes. A smooth downward curve passes through three plotted points: 60 °F -> 8 min, 68 °F -> 5 min (the anchor), 72 °F -> 4 min. The 68 °F / 5 min anchor point glows with a chip reading '5 MIN @ 68 °F'. Hatched no-go bands at both chart edges: below 60 °F labeled 'UNPREDICTABLE' and above ~75 °F labeled 'FOG / SOFT EMULSION'. Small tags 'COOLER -> LONGER' near the 60 °F point and 'WARMER -> SHORTER' near the 72 °F point. Animatable reveals vs rt2-2-3 beat-2 narration ('Never develop by sight. The time temperature method: five minutes at 68 degrees Fahrenheit. Longer when the bath runs cooler, shorter when warmer.' ~9.2 s): ttAxes fade at 0.8 (axes + gridlines as narration dismisses sight development); ttCurve draw at 1.6 ('the time temperature method'); ttAnchor fade at 3.6 (anchor dot + 5 MIN @ 68 °F chip, as narration speaks the anchor numbers); ttCold fade at 6.2 (60 °F/8 min point + COOLER -> LONGER tag, on 'longer when the bath runs cooler'); ttWarm fade at 7.6 (72 °F/4 min point + WARMER -> SHORTER tag, on 'shorter when warmer'); ttZones fade at 8.2 (both hatched no-go bands, just before the spec note lands at 8.6). All numbers are from the lesson's chart values - do not invent other time/temp pairs.

sweep-linearity.brief: Horizontal (sweep) linearity verification, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. LEFT THIRD: cross-section of a 25 mm parallel-faced steel block with a straight-beam probe coupled on top; vertical multi-bounce arrows between the parallel faces showing sound shuttling at a fixed period. RIGHT TWO-THIRDS: A-scan screen with a 10-division horizontal grid (range 125 mm); five back-wall echoes standing at divisions 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; small expected-position tick marks on the baseline at those divisions; a tolerance gate bracket around one echo's mark labeled '<=2% FSW'. Narration (~8.5 s): 'Horizontal linearity first. Back-wall echoes from a parallel block must land at equal spacing - within about two percent of screen width.' Reveals: hlBlock (fade 0.6) probe + parallel block + bounce arrows, syncs 'back-wall echoes from a parallel block'; hlGrid (fade 1.6) screen grid with expected-position ticks at divisions 2/4/6/8/10; hlEchoes (draw 2.6) the five echoes rising at equal spacing, syncs 'must land at equal spacing'; hlTicks (fade 4.4) per-echo alignment markers confirming each peak on its mark; hlTolerance (fade 6.6) the 2%-of-screen-width gate bracket, syncs 'within about two percent of screen width'. Scan sweep ~4.2. Keep bottom-center clear: player note chip lands at 7.4 s.

amplitude-linearity.brief: Vertical (amplitude) linearity + dB check, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Single A-scan screen with a vertical percent-of-full-screen-height scale, gridlines at 20/40/60/80/100%. Two echoes side by side: larger at 80% FSH, smaller at 40% FSH. A gain readout chip top-right reading 'GAIN' then stepping to '-6 dB'. After the gain step, both echoes drop to 40% and 20% with dashed ghost outlines of their original heights and small down arrows; a ratio bracket between the two peaks reads '2:1' before AND after the drop. Narration (~9.8 s): 'Vertical linearity next. Lock two echoes at eighty and forty percent, then step the gain. Removing six decibels must halve both - the ratio holds.' Reveals: vlScale (fade 0.6) the FSH percent gridlines; vlEchoes (draw 1.6) the two echoes locked at 80% and 40%, syncs 'lock two echoes at eighty and forty percent'; vlRatio (fade 3.0) the 2:1 bracket between the peaks; vlGain (fade 4.6) the '-6 dB' gain-step chip, syncs 'then step the gain. Removing six decibels'; vlDrop (draw 5.8) echoes at their halved heights 40%/20% with dashed ghosts + down arrows, syncs 'must halve both'; vlHold (fade 7.6) the 2:1 bracket re-shown at the new heights, syncs 'the ratio holds'. Scan sweep ~4.4. Keep bottom-center clear: player note chip lands at 8.5 s.

right-hand-rule.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Title-beat side panel (must read at small panel scale, minimal labels): the right-hand rule on a straight conductor. A horizontal copper conductor bar spans the frame; a stylized glowing right hand grips it, thumb pointing right. Beat narration (~8.5s): 'Run current through a conductor and circular flux wraps it. (0-3s) Your right hand's curl shows which way. (3-6s) That's circular magnetization.' Reveals: rhrBar (base, visible 0s) = the conductor bar alone, subtle metallic banding. rhrCurrent (draw ~0.6s, during 'Run current through a conductor') = current arrow flowing left-to-right along the bar with small 'CURRENT' tag, conventional flow positive to negative. rhrFlux (draw ~1.8s, at 'circular flux wraps it') = 3-4 concentric elliptical flux rings around the bar with circulation arrowheads; PHYSICS: with current to the right, flux emerges toward the viewer above the bar and recedes below it, i.e. the viewer-facing edge of each ring sweeps downward — arrowheads must match. rhrHand (fade ~3.5s, at 'Your right hand's curl shows which way') = the right hand gripping the bar, thumb aligned with the current arrow, curled fingers aligned with the ring arrowheads; two micro-labels 'THUMB = CURRENT' and 'FINGERS = FLUX'.

circular-flaw-geometry.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Diagram beat: what a circular field detects, on a head-shot style solid shaft. Beat narration (~10s): 'Flux must cross a flaw to force leakage. (0-3.2s) Circular flux wraps the circumference, (3.2-5.5s) lighting up seams and quench cracks running along the axis. (5.5-9.8s)' Player note chip lands at 9.0s — keep the bottom band clear. Reveals: cfgShaft (base, 0s) = solid steel shaft in side/three-quarter view. cfgCurrent (draw ~0.8s, during first sentence) = axial current arrow entering the left end and exiting the right, 'CURRENT' tag along the axis. cfgFlux (draw ~3.4s, at 'Circular flux wraps the circumference') = evenly spaced circumferential flux hoops around the shaft with direction arrowheads, perpendicular to the current arrow. cfgCrack (draw ~5.8s, at 'seams and quench cracks running along the axis') = one tight longitudinal seam/quench crack on the surface, PARALLEL to the axis, crossing the flux hoops at 90 degrees. cfgLeak (fade ~7.0s, at 'lighting up') = small leakage-flux bristles where hoops cross the crack plus a bright particle-indication glow building along the crack line. CRITICAL GEOMETRY: the detectable crack runs along the axis (parallel to current); flux hoops must visibly cross it at right angles. Do NOT add a transverse crack — the narration does not cover the missed-flaw case.

radiation-families.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan split 'family portrait' of ionizing radiation (title-beat panel; beat narration ~9s: 'Radiation comes in two families: particles with mass, and photons of pure energy. One shapes your safety — the other makes your image.'). Center-left: pulsing unstable nucleus drawn as a glowing cluster of nucleons. Left zone = PARTICLE family, right zone = PHOTON family, soft vertical glow divider between. IMPORTANT: do NOT label particle tracks alpha/beta/neutron by name — narration does not name them in this beat; keep them anonymous particle tracks. Reveals: rfNucleus (fade 0.5) nucleus pulses as narration opens; rfSplit (fade 0.9) divider + two zone glows on 'two families'; rfAlpha (draw 1.6) short fat double-ball track leaving nucleus leftward; rfBeta (draw 2.0) longer thin fast track with small dot; rfNeutron (draw 2.4) straight neutral-ball track; rfPartLbl (fade 2.9) caption chip 'PARTICLES · MASS' under the tracks, landing on '...particles with mass'; rfPhotons (draw 4.0) two sine-wave packet rays streaking right with light-speed motion lines, landing on '...photons of pure energy'; rfPhotLbl (fade 4.6) caption chip 'PHOTONS · NO MASS, NO CHARGE'; rfSafetyTag (fade 6.4) small shield glyph on the particle side as narration says 'one shapes your safety'; rfImageTag (fade 7.4) small film-frame glyph on the photon side at 'the other makes your image'.

radiation-penetration.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan penetrating-power ladder (diagram beat; mirrors lesson figure rt1-2-3-fig-a). Four horizontal radiation lanes, TOP→BOTTOM in narration order, each lane ending at ITS OWN barrier. Left edge: shared source emitter block with four lane origins labeled α, β, n, γ/X. Lane 1 ALPHA: fat double-ball track with dense ionization tick marks, stops dead AT a paper sheet. Lane 2 BETA: thin wavy faster track, passes a ghosted paper sheet, stops INSIDE a thin aluminum plate. Lane 3 NEUTRON: neutral-ball track, ignores thin barriers, enters a water/polyethylene tank (wavy hydrogen-rich fill) and slows to a stop along a billiard-bounce path. CRITICAL: do NOT route the neutron lane into lead — must never imply lead stops neutrons. Lane 4 PHOTON: sine-wave beam enters a thick lead block, exits visibly THINNER and dimmer, and CONTINUES off-canvas as a faint dashed ray — never show the photon stopped. Reveals vs narration ('Paper stops alpha. Thin aluminum stops beta. Hydrogen-rich material slows neutrons. But photons? Lead only thins them — no thickness stops every photon.' ~8.8s): rpnBase (fade 0.3) source block + lane origins; rpnAlpha (draw 0.6) alpha track to paper with stop ping; rpnPaperLbl (fade 1.1) chip 'PAPER'; rpnBeta (draw 2.2) beta track; rpnAlLbl (fade 2.9) chip 'THIN ALUMINUM'; rpnNeutron (draw 4.0) neutron track into tank; rpnHydroLbl (fade 4.7) chip 'HYDROGEN-RICH (WATER / POLY)'; rpnPhoton (draw 6.0) beam through lead, visibly thinning; rpnLeadLbl (fade 6.6) chip 'LEAD — THINS THE BEAM'; rpnFaint (fade 7.2) emphasize the faint continuing dashed ray (pairs with the spec's on-screen note at 7.8).

xray-gamma-spectra.brief:
Energy-spectrum comparison chart, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Axes: X = PHOTON ENERGY (0 to 1.5 MEV), Y = INTENSITY. Left portion: smooth bremsstrahlung hump rising from near zero, peaking low, falling to a hard cutoff at a vertical dashed marker labeled SET KV; one small unlabeled characteristic spike riding the hump (accuracy detail, keep subtle). Right portion: discrete vertical gamma lines — a cluster between 0.2 and 0.6 MeV labeled IR-192 · AVG 0.35 MEV, and two taller lines at 1.17 and 1.33 labeled CO-60. Legend chips: MACHINE: CONTINUOUS (cyan) and NUCLEUS: FIXED LINES (white). Beat runs ~9 s; narration: 'The tube emits a continuous spectrum — every energy up to the set kilovoltage. A decaying nucleus emits fixed lines: its own menu, forever.' Reveals: xgsAxes at 0.5 (axes draw); xgsHump at 1.0 (hump draws left-to-right — 'continuous spectrum'); xgsKvCut at 2.8 (dashed cutoff + SET KV chip — 'up to the set kilovoltage'); xgsIr at 4.8 (Ir-192 line cluster + label — 'a decaying nucleus emits fixed lines'); xgsCo at 6.0 (Co-60 1.17/1.33 pair + label — 'its own menu'); xgsLegend at 7.0 (legend chips — 'forever'). Keep lower-right clear for the renderer note at 7.8 s ('SAME MENU TODAY, TOMORROW, FOREVER').
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Two mini-plots side by side, shared axes style: x-axis PHOTON ENERGY, y-axis INTENSITY. Left plot labeled X-RAY TUBE: a smooth continuous bremsstrahlung hump rising from near-zero energy, peaking, then tapering to zero exactly at a vertical cutoff line labeled 'kVp' (the hard ceiling). Deliberately NO characteristic spikes - narration does not cover them. Right plot labeled GAMMA ISOTOPE: empty continuum with three narrow discrete vertical lines in a tight cluster, tag 'FIXED LINES', plus a small lock tag near the lines reading 'NO TUNING'. Keep all labels generic - no isotope names and no keV numbers, since the narration names none. Required reveal ids, timed against the 23-word narration (~8.8 s beat): xgAxes - both plots' axes at 0 s; xgCurve - continuous hump draws left-to-right 0.5-3.5 s ('emits a continuous spread'); xgCap - kVp cutoff line snaps in 3.5-5 s ('capped at the kilovoltage you set'); xgLines - gamma lines pop in sequence 5-7 s ('Gamma isotopes emit only fixed lines'); xgLock - lock tag fades in 7-8.5 s ('No knob tunes them'). The renderer overlays the beat note 'NO KNOB CHANGES A NUCLEAR ENERGY LEVEL' at 7.8 s, so xgLock should sit clear of the lower note band.

particle-mobility.brief: Core teaching diagram for mt1-3-1 beat 2 (mirrors lesson fig-a 'Surface condition and the indication'). 600x340 split comparison, same fine surface crack in a steel surface on both halves. Left half labeled CLEAN + DRY, right half labeled OILY (translucent amber oil film over the surface, hidden until pmFilm). Narration (~8s): 'Here's why. Particles must migrate to a weak leakage field. An oil film glues them where they land. The indication dies.' Required animatable reveal ids, in firing order: pmField = small leakage-field arcs bloom at the crack mouth on BOTH halves (the field exists regardless of contamination), fires on 'weak leakage field' (~3s); pmMigrate = left side, loose particles drift with motion trails and bridge the crack into a crisp indication line, fires on 'must migrate' (~3.5-4s); pmFilm = right side, the oil film layer fades in over the surface, holding the surface particles up off the metal, fires on 'An oil film' (~4.5s); pmStuck = right side, particles sit scattered, dimmed, and frozen where they landed in the film, none reaching the crack, fires on 'glues them where they land' (~5.5s); pmDead = right side, NO INDICATION tag appears at the unreached crack (leakage arcs still present but unbridged), fires on 'the indication dies' (~6.5s), just before the beat note at 6.8s ('NO MOBILITY → NO INDICATION').

double-film-cassette.brief: Title-panel scene, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: darkroom double-loading of a film cassette, shown in cutaway from the side. Right two-thirds: an open flexible cassette in cross-section with thin LEAD SCREENS layers labeled, and two sheets of industrial X-ray film sliding in between them, front sheet tagged FAST, back sheet tagged SLOW. Left third: a small stepped steel casting silhouette (heavy boss stepping down to a thin web) waiting on the bench, plus a dim red safelight glow halo in the upper corner (only non-cyan accent, keep subtle). Beat narration (22 words, ~8s): 'One casting, two thicknesses, one density window. Thin goes black, thick stays light, until you load two films in one cassette.' Reveals: dfcShell (cassette outline + lead screens, on from 0s), dfcCasting (stepped part highlights its THICK and THIN steps at ~1.5s on 'two thicknesses'), dfcFast (FAST sheet slides into the cassette at ~6s on 'load two films'), dfcSlow (SLOW sheet slides in behind it at ~7s on 'in one cassette'), dfcGlow (faint looping safelight pulse, ambient throughout).

speed-latitude-curves.brief: Chart, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan: two film characteristic curves compared (this is NOT the existing single-curve film-density-curve asset — it must show a fast vs slow pair). X axis LOG RELATIVE EXPOSURE, Y axis DENSITY 0 to 4.5. Horizontal shaded band 1.8-4.0 labeled DENSITY WINDOW. Two S-curves: FAST curve at left (reaches density with less exposure) drawn with a moderately lower slope and a coarse-dot grain badge; SLOW curve same shape shifted right with a slightly steeper slope and a fine-dot grain badge (per film class table: coarser grain = faster + moderate contrast, finer grain = slower + higher contrast + sharper). Horizontal latitude brackets along the exposure axis marking the log-E span each curve holds inside the window; the flatter FAST curve's bracket is visibly wider. Beat narration (23 words, ~8.5s): 'Two properties drive it. Speed: coarse grain is fast, fine grain slower but sharper. Latitude: flat curves cover more thickness, steep curves discriminate.' Reveals: slcAxes (axes + window band, on from 0s), slcFast (fast curve + coarse grain badge draws at ~2s on 'coarse grain is fast'), slcSlow (slow curve + fine grain badge draws at ~3.5s on 'fine grain slower but sharper'), slcLatitude (both latitude brackets sweep in at ~5.5s on 'flat curves cover more thickness'). On-beat note overlay 'FINER GRAIN = SLOWER = SHARPER' lands at 7.8s, so keep the lower-left clear.

two-film-exposure.brief: Diagram, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, elevation view of the two-film exposure (lesson fig-b concept): gamma source capsule top center with a radiation cone down through a stepped steel part, THICK section left, THIN section right. Beneath the part, a slightly exploded cassette: front film strip labeled FAST, back strip labeled SLOW, lead screens between them. Right edge: a vertical density gauge 0-4.5 with the 1.8-4.0 window banded. Result markers: on the FAST film the area behind THICK glows in-window (marker mid-band on gauge) while its THIN area saturates black above the window; on the SLOW film the area behind THIN glows in-window while its THICK area reads pale below the window. Beat narration (24 words, ~8.8s): 'Load a fast and a slow film in one cassette with lead screens. Shoot once. Fast reads the heavy section; slow reads the thin.' Reveals: tfeSetup (part + cassette layers + labels, on from 0s while loading is narrated), tfeBeam (radiation cone fires at ~4s on 'Shoot once'), tfeFastRead (FAST film thick-section in-window marker + its blacked-out thin end at ~5s on 'Fast reads the heavy section'), tfeSlowRead (SLOW film thin-section in-window marker + its pale thick end at ~7s on 'slow reads the thin'). Note overlay 'FAST → THICK · SLOW → THIN' lands at 7.9s, keep bottom strip clear.

impedance-split.brief: Normal-incidence reflection/transmission at two boundaries (600x340 holographic navy/cyan), mirroring lesson fig ut1-2-3-fig-a and the curriculum's 'reflection and transmission coefficients at steel/water/air boundaries' diagram. Two side-by-side section panels, each with a vertical interface line. LEFT panel: STEEL (46.5 MRAYL) on the left half, WATER (1.48 MRAYL) on the right. RIGHT panel: STEEL (46.5 MRAYL) | AIR (0.0004 MRAYL). A horizontal incident beam arrow approaches each interface from the steel side. Energy split rendered by arrow weight: left panel — thick reflected arrow folding back tagged '88% REFLECTS', thin continuing arrow tagged '12% CROSSES'; right panel — full-weight reflected arrow tagged '~100% REFLECTS', faint dashed ghost beyond the boundary tagged '~0%'. Center-top formula chip: R = ((Z2−Z1)/(Z2+Z1))². USED AS: diagram beat, ~9.5 s. Narration: 'Sound meets a boundary. Difference over sum, squared: that's the reflected fraction. Steel to water: eighty eight percent reflects. Steel to air: essentially everything.' REQUIRED REVEALS: imsScanG — sweep glint (scan id, ~4.5 s); imsStage (fade, 0.4 s) — both panels with material names + Z values ('Sound meets a boundary'); imsBeam (draw, 1.2 s) — incident arrows travel in; imsFormula (fade, 2.8 s) — formula chip, landing on 'difference over sum, squared'; imsWater (draw, 5.0 s) — steel-water split with 88%/12% tags, landing on 'eighty eight percent reflects'; imsAir (draw, 7.4 s) — steel-air ~100% return + dashed ~0% ghost, landing on 'essentially everything'. Beat note fades in at 8.2 s, so keep the lower-third clear for it.

differential-absorption.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Side view: radiation source at upper left emitting a fanned beam cone down through a steel plate cross-section mid-frame. The plate has two features: a round internal void (gas pore) and a raised weld-reinforcement crown (extra thickness). Below the plate, a film strip showing the resulting density map: dark spot directly under the void, light band under the crown, mid-gray elsewhere. Transmitted-ray arrows are thickness-coded: strong through the void (less absorption), thin through the crown (more absorption). Required reveal ids: daSource (source + beam cone), daPart (plate with void and crown), daRays (thickness-coded transmitted arrows), daFilm (film density map). In rt1-6-1 this is the title-beat ambient PANEL: daSource + daPart + daFilm visible from 0 s while narration says 'Source on one side, film on the other, part between' (0-4 s); daRays pulses from ~4 s under 'the emerging beam carries a map of everything it passed through' (4-8.5 s); gentle looping beam shimmer throughout. NOTE: rt1-1-1's spec also references this asset as its diagram beat (note text 'LESS ABSORPTION -> MORE EXPOSURE -> DARKER SPOT') - keep the four reveals granular so both videos can sequence them; dedupe against any brief already submitted from the rt1-1-1 authoring pass.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan teaching cross-section for RT image formation (lesson rt1-1-1 fig-b concept). Three zones top-to-bottom, labeled in mono caps: SOURCE, STEEL + VOID, FILM. Top center: a compact radiation source point (source-type agnostic so it reads as X-ray or gamma) emitting a fan of straight rays downward. Middle: a steel slab in cross-section spanning the width, containing ONE rounded internal gas pore left-of-center. Bottom: a film strip with an exposure/density band. Rays passing through full-thickness steel visibly attenuate (dimmer below the slab); the single ray crossing the pore stays noticeably brighter beneath the void. Directly under the pore the film band shows a distinctly darker spot. Animatable reveal ids (beat narration is 23 words, ~9.2s): daBeam = the ray fan from source into/through the slab, trigger ~0.5s on 'How the image forms'; daVoid = highlight ring on the gas pore plus its brighter transmitted ray, trigger ~2.5s on 'a void absorbs less than solid steel'; daSpot = the dark spot blooming on the film band beneath the pore, trigger ~6.5s on 'printing a darker spot' — just before the beat note at 8.0s (LESS ABSORPTION → MORE EXPOSURE → DARKER SPOT), which it must visually corroborate. Keep geometry exaggeration modest: pore diameter ~15% of slab thickness, ray count 7-9 so the through-pore contrast is unmistakable.

rt-beam-orientation.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section teaching RT's volumetric-vs-planar orientation rule (beat narration ~9.0s: 'Radiography sees only thickness change along the beam. Volumetric flaws print from any angle. Planar flaws print only edge-on — laminations belong to ultrasonics.'). Static base: X-ray source point + collimator at top center, a wide horizontal steel plate slab across the middle, and a film strip running beneath the plate; keep the bottom ~50px strip clear for the note chip that lands at 8.0s. Reveals: rbBeam (draw, 0.6s) — parallel vertical ray arrows drawing down from the source through the plate to the film, while narration covers 'thickness change along the beam' (0-3.0s). rbPore (fade, 3.1s) — round gas pore inside the plate's left third; rbPoreSpot (fade, 3.8s) + rbPorePing (ping, 3.9s) — fuzzy round dark spot printing on the film directly below it, synced to 'volumetric flaws print from any angle' (3.0-5.3s). rbCrack (draw, 5.4s) — tight vertical planar crack at plate center with its plane parallel to the rays (beam riding the plane, i.e. edge-on); rbCrackLine (fade, 6.2s) — sharp dark line printing on the film below, synced to 'planar flaws print only edge-on' (5.3-7.2s). rbLam (draw, 7.2s) — thin horizontal lamination lying flat in the plate's right third, rays crossing it at ninety degrees; rbLamGhost (fade, 7.8s) — dashed empty outline on the film beneath it showing almost no image; rbUtTag (fade, 8.4s) — small 'UT' handoff tag beside the lamination, synced to 'laminations belong to ultrasonics' (7.2-9.0s). Small VOLUMETRIC / PLANAR zone captions over the left and right halves are welcome; no other labels needed.

mode-conversion.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of an acrylic wedge on steel. Horizontal interface line at ~58% height; acrylic block above tagged 'ACRYLIC · 2,730 m/s', steel below tagged 'STEEL'. Vertical dashed normal through the entry point. One incident longitudinal beam travels down through the acrylic at 20 deg from the normal and splits at the interface into TWO transmitted beams in the steel: a refracted longitudinal leg at 48 deg from the normal (tag 'L · 5,920 m/s · 48°') and a mode-converted shear leg at 24 deg (tag 'S · 3,240 m/s · 24°'). The shear leg must read visibly closer to the normal (shallower refracted angle) than the longitudinal leg. Small angle arcs at the normal for 20°, 48°, 24°. Keep bottom-center clear for the player's note chip ('TWO MODES · TWO VELOCITIES · TWO ANGLES') at 8.5s. Narration over this beat (~8.5s): 'But the boundary also splits the beam: part converts to slower shear at a shallower angle. Two beams in the part at once.' Required animatable reveals: mcIncident (draw, 0.6s — incident beam in the wedge plus 20° arc, while 'the boundary' is spoken); mcNormal (fade, 1.1s — dashed normal line); mcSplit (fade, 2.2s — bright flash node at the entry point, on 'splits the beam'); mcLong (draw, 2.8s — longitudinal leg with 48° arc and L velocity tag); mcShear (draw, 4.3s — shear leg with 24° arc and S velocity tag, landing on 'slower shear at a shallower angle'); mcTwoBeams (fade, 6.3s — twin pulse dots running down both legs simultaneously, on 'two beams in the part at once'). Include scan id mcscanG, sweep ~4.5. All values are from the lesson: 20° acrylic incidence gives sin θL = 0.342 × (5,920/2,730) → ≈48° and sin θS = 0.342 × (3,240/2,730) → ≈24°.

iqi-placement.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan side-view cross-section teaching the IQI side rule. Paired narration (~7.8 s): 'The I Q I goes source side, far from the film, enduring full geometric unsharpness. Film side only when unreachable — flagged with a lead F.' STATIC BASE: steel wall section with a V-groove weld at center, small radiation source point with collimated beam cone at top, film cassette band beneath the part labeled FILM. REVEALS: iqpSourceIqi (fade, 0.8 s) hole-type plaque IQI on a thin UNLABELED shim on the TOP surface beside the weld, tag SOURCE SIDE — lands on 'goes source side'; iqpRays (draw, 2.0 s) two rays diverging from the source past the plaque edges down to the film, showing the long IQI-to-film projection distance — 'far from the film'; iqpUgBand (fade, 3.6 s) penumbra blur band where that IQI images on the film, tag FULL Ug — 'enduring full geometric unsharpness'; iqpFilmIqi (fade, 4.9 s) ghosted second plaque on the BOTTOM film-side surface, tag FILM SIDE, with a small lock glyph marking blocked source-side access and a visibly crisper image footprint on the film — 'film side only when unreachable'; iqpLeadF (pop, 6.4 s) bold lead-character F chip adjacent to the film-side IQI — 'flagged with a lead F'. Renderer note overlay appears at 6.8 s along the bottom; keep that band clear. Do NOT label the shim (shim purpose is taught in the next beat) and do not add text beyond the tags listed.

beam-profile.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Side cross-section of a steel block with a straight-beam contact probe coupled at the LEFT edge; beam axis runs horizontally to the right as a faint center line. A thin on-axis amplitude trace is plotted along the bottom strip of the canvas. Static base: probe body, steel block outline, beam axis line. Reveals (ids required, timed to the beat-2 narration 'The element vibrates as thousands of sources, and their wavelets interfere. Inside the near field, amplitude swings wildly. Beyond N it decays predictably.' total ~9.4 s): (1) bpSources at ~0.5 s — the element face subdivides into a row of small glowing point sources, each emitting overlapping circular wavelet arcs that visibly intersect just ahead of the face (interference), synced to 'thousands of sources...wavelets interfere'. (2) bpNearZone at ~3.3 s — a roughly element-wide mottled/turbulent shaded zone from the probe face out to (not past) the N boundary, labeled NEAR FIELD; simultaneously the bottom amplitude trace draws a jagged run of maxima and minima whose tallest peak lands exactly at N; synced to 'inside the near field, amplitude swings wildly'. (3) bpNMark at ~6.0 s — vertical dashed boundary line labeled 'N' at the end of the near zone. (4) bpFarField at ~6.3 s — beyond N the beam edges diverge smoothly and symmetrically (do NOT annotate the angle or any formula; spread math is taught in the next beat), zone labeled FAR FIELD, and the amplitude trace continues from its peak at N as one clean smooth decay curve; synced to 'Beyond N it decays predictably'. The renderer's note chip lands at 8.2 s; keep the lower-left corner clear for it.

coating-standoff.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section. Steel substrate fills the lower third with a tight vertical crack open to its top surface at center-left. From the crack mouth, leakage-flux arcs bloom upward with a strong-to-faint vertical gradient (bright cyan at the mouth, nearly gone ~40px up); a small field-strength falloff curve at the right edge reinforces the rapid decay. A translucent nonmagnetic coating slab (label NONMAGNETIC COATING) lies on the steel; magnetic particle dots ride on the coating's OUTER face, visibly lifted out of the bright zone into the faint zone. Right side (or morphing state): thin-film vs thick-coating outcomes — thin keeps a crisp particle cluster over the crack, thick scatters and dims it. Beat narration (25 w, ~9 s): 'Leakage flux fades fast above the surface. A nonmagnetic coating holds particles up in the weak zone: thin films cost little, thick coatings erase indications.' Required reveals: cstField (draw, ~0.8s) leakage arcs + decay gradient while narration says flux fades fast; cstCoating (fade, ~3.2s) coating slab appears and particles land on its outer surface at a standoff, as narration says the coating holds particles in the weak zone; cstThin (fade, ~5.6s) thin-film state, crisp indication still forms, at 'thin films cost little'; cstThick (fade, ~7.2s) coating thickens and the particle cluster scatters/fades, at 'thick coatings erase indications'. Keep the bottom strip clear for the note chip at 8.8s ('THIN FILM COSTS LITTLE · THICK ERASES').

surface-vs-subsurface.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan single steel-bar cross-section spanning the width, horizontal flux lines flowing left to right through it. LEFT half: a narrow crack open to the top surface — flux lines kink and burst out of the crack mouth in a tight, concentrated leakage bloom; a sharp, dense, bright particle line with crisp edges sits exactly on the crack (small caption SHARP). RIGHT half: a rounded inclusion wholly below the surface — most flux lines visibly crowd/compress through the sound metal above it, and only a wide, faint leakage haze reaches the surface, topped by a broad, dim, soft-edged particle smudge (small caption FUZZY). Beat narration (23 w, ~8.4 s): 'A surface crack breaks the flux abruptly: sharp, dense, tightly held. A buried flaw leaks only a diluted field: broad, fuzzy, easily wiped.' Required reveals: svsFlux (draw, ~0.6s) flux lines stream through the bar as narration opens; svsSharp (ping + fade, ~2.0s) left-side leakage burst and the sharp dense indication, timed to 'sharp, dense, tightly held'; svsCrowd (draw, ~4.4s) right-side flux crowding through the sound metal above the inclusion with the diluted haze at the surface, timed to 'leaks only a diluted field'; svsFuzzy (fade, ~6.4s) the broad fuzzy weakly-held smudge with a half-wiped look, timed to 'broad, fuzzy, easily wiped'. Keep the bottom strip clear for the note chip at 8.0s ('SHARP = SURFACE · FUZZY = SUBSURFACE').

ion-chamber-exposure.brief: Exposure defined as ionization in air, taught through an ion-chamber cutaway (600x340 holographic navy/cyan, matches lesson fig rt1-3-2-fig-a). LAYOUT: left, a compact photon source (X/gamma) emitting a wavy photon beam rightward into a transparent glass-walled air cell sitting between two electrode plates (+ plate top, - plate bottom); a bracket under the cell labeled '1 kg DRY AIR'; right, an integrating meter wired to the plates with a large unit chip 'C/kg' and a small dial face marked 'mR/h'. ANIMATABLE REVEALS vs narration 'Exposure is photons ionizing air: the charge liberated per kilogram of dry air, the coulomb per kilogram. The roentgen is its exact fraction.' (23 words, ~9 s beat): icBeam at ~0.3s = source powers on, photon beam (2-3 wavy rays) crosses the air cell ('Exposure is photons ionizing air'); icIons at ~1.8s = ion-pair trail appears along the beam, small minus electrons drift up to the + plate and plus ions drift down to the - plate ('photons ionizing air' / 'charge liberated'); icCharge at ~4.0s = plates glow, current path lights to the meter, 'C/kg' unit chip plus the '1 kg DRY AIR' bracket emphasize charge-per-mass ('per kilogram of dry air, the coulomb per kilogram'); icRoentgen at ~6.8s = small 'R' badge pulses by the dial as its face reads in mR/h, signaling the field-sized unit ('The roentgen is its exact fraction'). The spec's note overlay lands at 8.0s with '1 R = 2.58 x 10^-4 C/KG - EXACT', so the asset itself must NOT bake in that conversion text. Keep all labels short and uppercase; no person, no tissue/dose imagery (exposure is air-only by definition).

cert-ladder.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Four-tier certification responsibility ladder rising left-to-right: TRAINEE (small figure tethered to a certified silhouette, tag DIRECT SUPERVISION ONLY), LEVEL I (tier card PERFORM & RECORD enclosed in a glowing boundary box labeled WRITTEN INSTRUCTION), LEVEL II (tier card INTERPRET & EVALUATE with code-book glyph), LEVEL III (top tier card APPROVE PROCEDURES / TRAIN & EXAMINE). A glowing gate/threshold line labeled INTERPRETATION sits between the Level I and Level II tiers. Whole ladder dimly visible by default (house pattern per indication-ladder); reveals light tiers. Narration this syncs to (~9.5s): 'Duties stack as you climb. A trainee works only under direct supervision. Level One stays inside the instruction — interpretation begins at Level Two.' Required reveal ids: lvTrainee (fade ~2.2s — trainee tier + supervision tether light up, on 'a trainee works only under direct supervision'), lvLevel1 (fade ~4.6s — Level I tier lights, on 'Level One'), lvBox (ping ~5.4s — WRITTEN INSTRUCTION boundary box pulses, on 'stays inside the instruction'), lvGate (fade ~6.8s — INTERPRETATION threshold line ignites), lvLevel2 (fade ~7.3s — Level II tier lights, on 'begins at Level Two'). lvLevel3 must exist as a hidden/alternate-lesson lit layer but is NEVER revealed in ut1-1-3 — the Level III tier stays dim background context here. Spec note overlay 'INTERPRETATION BEGINS AT LEVEL II' fires at 8.0s, so keep the lower-right note zone clear. Prefix 'lv' is unused by existing assets ('cl' is taken by coating-layers). This asset is method-agnostic by design — sibling certification-levels lessons in MT/PT/RT/ET/RAD courses should converge on it.

ers-limitation.brief: Equivalent-reflector-size (ERS) limitation: why a natural flaw that matches a machined reference is probably larger. 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. LAYOUT: left panel labeled IDEAL TARGET — small probe at top firing a beam onto a machined 3 mm flat-bottom-hole disc drawn smooth, air-backed, face perfectly square to the beam, with a strong specular return arrow back to the probe and a mini echo bar peaking at a dashed reference line. Right panel labeled NATURAL FLAW — three compact vignettes of the same incident beam hitting: (1) a crack tilted 10-15 degrees off square, return arrow deflected AWAY from the probe; (2) a ragged rough crack face scattering many small arrows in all directions; (3) a tightly closed crack with part of the beam passing straight through it; each vignette gets a visibly shorter echo bar than the ideal one. Bottom verdict strip: two EQUAL-height echo bars labeled 'REF 3 mm' and 'INDICATION', beside silhouettes showing the natural flaw outline clearly LARGER than the 3 mm disc. ANIMATABLE REVEALS vs my 25-word beat-3 narration ('But machined reflectors are ideal targets. Natural flaws sit tilted, rough, or tightly closed. Match a 3 millimeter hole; the real flaw is probably larger.' ~9.5 s): ersIdeal at 0.8 s = entire left ideal-target panel incl. full-height echo bar (syncs 'machined reflectors are ideal targets'); ersTilt at 2.9 s = tilted-crack vignette (syncs 'tilted'); ersRough at 3.8 s = scattering vignette (syncs 'rough'); ersTight at 4.6 s = closed-crack pass-through vignette (syncs 'tightly closed'); ersVerdict at 6.2 s = bottom strip with equal echo bars + larger-flaw silhouette (syncs 'match a 3 millimeter hole; the real flaw is probably larger'). Keep the lower area clear of critical detail where the renderer note overlay drops in at 8.8 s.

contrast-factor-tree.brief: Beat 2 diagram (narration, 21w ~8.5s: 'Sensitivity stands on two pillars: contrast and definition. Today: contrast. And contrast itself combines two contributions: subject contrast and film contrast.'). Holographic factor tree mirroring lesson figure rt1-8-1-fig-a and the curriculum's planned 'Contrast Factor Tree: Film vs. Subject'. Root node top-center: RADIOGRAPHIC SENSITIVITY. Two children: CONTRAST (caption 'density difference', left) and DEFINITION (caption 'edge sharpness', right). CONTRAST branches into two leaves: SUBJECT CONTRAST (caption 'part + beam') and FILM CONTRAST (caption 'film + processing'). Reveals: cftRoot = root node draws on, 0.0-1.0s ('Sensitivity'); cftPillars = both pillar nodes + connectors drop in, 1.5-2.5s ('two pillars: contrast and definition'); cftFocus = CONTRAST node brightens while DEFINITION dims to a ghost with a tiny 'NEXT LESSON' tag, ~3.5s ('Today: contrast'); cftSubject = subject-contrast leaf pops, ~5.5s ('subject contrast'); cftFilm = film-contrast leaf pops, ~7.0s ('and film contrast'). On-screen note strip appears at 7.4s.

ut-transducer.brief:
Cutaway of a straight-beam contact probe (canonical UT asset), vertical sandwich centered, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. From top: connector (BNC-style) and short coax lead entering a shielded case outline; heavy tungsten-loaded backing block (dense speckle fill) bonded to the element, with a small rearward echo squiggle fading INTO the backing to show absorption; thin piezoelectric element disc with electrode lines plated on both faces; wear face plate below the element; thin couplant film and a steel part section at the bottom. Side labels: BACKING, ELEMENT, WEAR FACE, PART. Narration for this beat (~9s): 'Top to bottom: a tungsten loaded backing absorbs rearward sound and damps ringing, the thin piezoelectric element, then the wear face meeting the part.' Required animatable reveals: txCase (fade, 0.5) case + connector + coax lead while narration says 'Top to bottom'; txBacking (fade, 1.5) backing block + absorbed rear-echo squiggle during 'tungsten loaded backing absorbs rearward sound and damps ringing'; txElement (fade, 5.0) electroded piezo disc during 'thin piezoelectric element'; txWearface (fade, 6.6) wear face plate during 'then the wear face'; txPart (fade, 7.4) couplant film + steel part during 'meeting the part'; txPulse (fade, 8.2) small short ring-down waveform glyph beside the backing, landing just before the player's note chip 'THE BACKING SETS THE PULSE LENGTH' at 8.3s. Leave bottom-center open for that note chip. Scan sweep ~5.0s.
---
Cutaway side view of a straight-beam contact probe resting on a steel part, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Inside a rounded housing, top to bottom: coax connector, lead wire, dark damping backing block, thin bright piezoelectric disc with electrode faces (the hero — strongest cyan glow), protective wear plate, thin couplant film, part surface. Mono-caps side labels. Used as the TITLE-beat panel. Reveals vs the 22-word (~9 s) narration 'Inside every ultrasonic probe sits one thin disc that both transmits the pulse and receives the echo — speaker and microphone in one': utrCase (fade 0.8s) housing + connector + backing block as the scene is set; utrElement (fade+ping 3.0s) the thin disc glows with label PIEZO ELEMENT on 'one thin disc'; utrTxWaves (draw 4.8s) wavefront arcs leaving the wear face into the part on 'transmits the pulse'; utrRxEcho (draw 6.3s) returning echo arcs back up to the disc on 'receives the echo'; utrBadge (fade 7.9s) small tag SPEAKER + MIC IN ONE beside the disc on the closing phrase.

probe-family.brief: Catalog/foam-tray lineup of four ultrasonic probe silhouettes in a row, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, used as the TITLE-beat panel under label 'PROBE CATALOG // ONE STACK'. Probes left to right: stubby straight-beam contact probe (cylinder with cable), angle-beam probe seated on an acrylic wedge, dual-element probe (case face split in two, twin-crystal hint), slim delay-line probe with a clear plastic standoff tip. Small mono tags under each (STRAIGHT, ANGLE, DUAL, DELAY). Narration for this beat (~7.5s): 'Open a probe catalog — hundreds of models, and every one a variation on the same sandwich. Let's take it apart.' Required animatable reveals: pfStraight (fade, 0.8), pfAngle (fade, 1.6), pfDual (fade, 2.4), pfDelay (fade, 3.2) — the four probes popping in like catalog entries during 'hundreds of models'; pfStack (fade, 4.8) an identical ghosted internal stack (backing/element/wear face) glowing inside ALL four cases at once, landing on 'variation on the same sandwich'. Ambient loop-friendly; no note chip on this beat.

earth-field.brief: Earth as a tilted bar magnet — 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Center-left: translucent globe with faint latitude lines and a thin dashed vertical spin axis; a bar magnet embedded through the core, tilted slightly off that axis, ends labeled N and S. Large closed flux loops exit one polar region, arc through space, and re-enter at the other — nested, fully closed loops with direction arrowheads, crowding near the poles. Right side: surface tag '~0.5 GAUSS' pinned at ground level, and below it a brighter cyan mini-chip 'MT FIELD // THOUSANDS x STRONGER'. No compass, no part or yoke — keep it planetary. Reveals timed vs narration 'The earth behaves like a magnet, roughly half a gauss underfoot. Magnetic particle testing builds fields thousands of times stronger, then reads where they escape.': efGlobe (fade ~0.5s) globe + dashed spin axis as the beat opens; efBar (fade ~1.0s) tilted bar magnet appears in the core on 'behaves like a magnet'; efLoops (draw ~1.4s) flux loops sweep pole to pole; efGauss (fade ~2.6s) '~0.5 GAUSS' tag pops on 'roughly half a gauss underfoot'; efStrong (fade ~5.6s) 'MT FIELD // THOUSANDS x STRONGER' chip ignites on 'thousands of times stronger'.

ring-leakage.brief: Closed ring vs slotted ring (this lesson's fig-c story) — 600x340 holographic navy/cyan, two rings side by side. LEFT: solid steel ring (donut) with 3-4 concentric flux loops circulating entirely INSIDE the ring wall, arrowheads showing direction, zero external field lines; small tag 'FLUX CONTAINED' beneath. RIGHT: identical ring with a thin radial slot cut at 12 o'clock; the internal loops bulge OUT of one cut face, arc over the gap, and re-enter the opposite face — make these leakage arcs the brightest cyan on the canvas; cut faces labeled N and S. NO particles anywhere — narration stops at leaking flux (particles belong to the existing converged flux-leakage asset used by mt1-1-1/mt1-1-3/mt1-2-3). Reveals timed vs narration 'Close a magnet into a ring: poles vanish, flux stays inside. Saw a slot, and poles snap back, leaking flux across the cut.': rlRing (draw ~0.6s) left ring + internal circulating flux on 'Close a magnet into a ring'; rlContained (fade ~2.6s) 'FLUX CONTAINED' tag on 'poles vanish, flux stays inside'; rlSlotRing (fade ~4.6s) right ring with slot on 'Saw a slot'; rlPoles (fade ~5.8s) N/S labels flash on the cut faces on 'poles snap back'; rlLeak (draw ~6.6s) leakage arcs bulge across the gap on 'leaking flux across the cut'. Do not bake in the player note text — the spec's noteAt 7.6 overlays 'POLES AT THE CUT — FLUX LEAKS ACROSS'.

gm-foldback.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan chart: GM survey-meter saturation fold-back. Layout: X axis 'TRUE DOSE RATE' arrow right, qualitative only (LOW -> EXTREME, no numbers); Y axis 'METER READING'. Dashed diagonal ideal-response reference line. Solid GM response curve follows the ideal at low rates, rolls over at a peak near mid-chart, then folds back down to hug near zero at far right. Far-right third: shaded danger band where the curve sits near zero, containing a small meter-face glyph with needle at 0 over intense-field hatching. Top-left inset: tiny pulse train that starts as discrete spikes and smears into an unbroken blur. Beat-3 narration (~9.4s): 'The G M trap: in a very intense field pulses pile up, and the meter can fold back to near zero. Quiet can mean danger.' Required reveals: gmfAxes (draw 0.6) axes + labels; gmfIdeal (draw 1.2) dashed ideal line at 'The G M trap'; gmfPulses (fade 2.8) pulse-train inset smearing solid at 'pulses pile up'; gmfCurve (draw 3.8) curve rises and rolls over; gmfFold (draw 5.2) fold-back segment dropping toward zero at 'fold back to near zero'; gmfZero (ping 6.4) 'READS ~0' tag on the flattened tail; gmfDanger (fade 7.4) danger band + zeroed meter-face glyph at 'Quiet can mean danger'. Keep lower-right clear - the beat note 'SATURATED GM: QUIET != SAFE' lands at 8.4s.

casting-shrink-centers.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan teaching diagram for the beat 3 narration 'The riser feeds that freeze. Cut a T junction off from liquid feed and the missing volume becomes a void: shrinkage at the thermal center.' (~10.4s beat; spec note 'L · T · V · X JUNCTIONS FREEZE LAST' fires at 9.2s). Layout: left two-thirds shows a casting cross-section where a horizontal rib meets a vertical wall in a T junction, with a riser on top; a bright cyan liquid-core channel runs from the riser down into the junction, while dimmer solidified skin grows inward from the mold walls. Bottom-right strip reserves space for four mini cross-section glyphs shaped like the letters L, T, V, X, each with a hot-spot dot at its junction center. Animatable reveals: cscBase (fade, ~0.4s) T-section, riser, glowing liquid feed channel — under 'The riser feeds that freeze'; cscFeedArrow (draw, ~1.2s) feed arrow flowing from riser down the channel into the junction; cscPinch (fade/draw, ~3.6s) solidification fronts close and sever the channel mid-length, dimming the upstream link — lands on 'Cut a T junction off from liquid feed'; cscVoid (fade + ping, ~6.2s) ragged shrink cavity appears at the junction's thermal center with a small mono SHRINK tag — lands on 'becomes a void: shrinkage at the thermal center'; cscLetters (fade, ~8.4s) the L/T/V/X mini-section row fades in with pulsing junction hot spots, pairing with the on-screen note at 9.2s.

mt-selection-questions.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan decision board for MT equipment selection (CP-105 9.1.1-9.1.5). Layout: five stacked rounded slot-rows, each with a small glyph, a bold mono keyword, and a short sub-question; faint mono index tags 9.1.1-9.1.5 at row left. Beat narration (24 words, ~9.5 s): 'Five questions drive the choice: what magnetizing current, where is the test, what is the material, why are you testing, and how much area.' Required animatable reveals, one row each, in order: msqCurrent (~2.0 s, on 'what magnetizing current') — bolt/waveform glyph, keyword CURRENT, sub 'AC / HWDC / FWDC?'; msqLocation (~4.0 s, on 'where is the test') — factory/map-pin glyph, keyword LOCATION, sub 'shop bench or field?'; msqMaterial (~5.8 s, on 'what is the material') — layered-steel glyph, keyword MATERIAL, sub 'alloy / heat treat / finish'; msqPurpose (~7.2 s, on 'why are you testing') — target glyph, keyword PURPOSE, sub 'criticality, sensitivity'; msqArea (~8.2 s, on 'how much area') — expand-arrows glyph, keyword AREA, sub 'local weld or full coverage?'. Optional msqGlow: subtle outline pulse around the full stack after all five are in (~9.0 s). The renderer's note 'CURRENT · LOCATION · MATERIAL · PURPOSE · AREA' lands at 8.4 s, so all rows except msqArea must be settled by then; keep row text short and legible at video scale.

hidden-circular-field.brief: Two residual-field shafts compared, teaching that a circular residual field hides from external meters while the part stays magnetized. Layout: two horizontal shafts stacked. TOP — LONGITUDINAL RESIDUAL: flux lines exit one end, arc through air, re-enter the other end; N and S pole tags at the ends; pocket field indicator near one end with needle clearly deflected. BOTTOM — CIRCULAR RESIDUAL: same shaft with a cutaway wedge revealing concentric hoop flux rings fully contained in the cross-section (rings inside a barrel), no external field lines; identical indicator held on the surface with needle dead-center reading '0 G'. Reveals timed vs narration 'Longitudinal residual fields leave poles a meter can see. / Circular residuals hide inside: the meter reads zero. / Critical procedures convert them to longitudinal first.' (~9s): hcfLongBar (fade, 0.4s) top shaft + LONGITUDINAL label; hcfLongFlux (draw, 0.8s) external flux arcs + N/S pole tags; hcfLongMeter (fade, 2.2s) deflected indicator, microtag 'METER DEFLECTS'; hcfCircBar (fade, 3.6s) bottom shaft + CIRCULAR label + cutaway; hcfCircFlux (draw, 4.0s) contained glowing hoop rings, microtag 'NO EXTERNAL POLES'; hcfCircMeter (fade, 5.2s) centered-needle indicator reading '0 G'; hcfConvert (fade, 6.6s) coil outline appears around the circular shaft with tag 'MAGNETIZE LONGITUDINALLY FIRST' as external flux arcs sprout from its ends. The 0 G meter on a still-magnetized part is the money shot.

demagnetization.brief:
Demagnetization method-vs-depth diagram. SHARED ASSET: also the beat-4 diagram of mt1-5-2 ('Match the method to the depth') — one drawing serves both lessons; dedupe with any earlier request for this name. LEFT: AC pass-through demag coil with a part withdrawing along the coil axis; alternating-polarity wave whose amplitude tapers with distance from the coil. CENTER: part cross-section with the AC field as a thin glowing skin at the surface, core still brightly magnetized. RIGHT: reversing step-down DC — staircase of polarity-flipping amplitude bars shrinking to zero, with the field penetrating the full cross-section and extinguishing the core glow. Reveals timed vs mt2-3-2 narration 'Erase it: reverse the field as it shrinks to zero. / A C hugs the surface; / deep direct current fields need reversing, step-down D C.' (~9s; pacing also fits mt1-5-2's beat-4 narration): dmgCoil (fade, 0.4s) coil + part on axis; dmgWave (draw, 0.9s) tapering alternating wave; dmgWithdraw (fade, 1.8s) withdrawal arrow, tag 'SLOW WITHDRAWAL TAPERS THE FIELD'; dmgSkin (fade, 3.4s) skin-depth shading with core still lit, tag 'AC HUGS THE SURFACE'; dmgDC (fade, 5.0s) reversing step-down staircase; dmgDeep (fade, 6.8s) full-depth penetration, core glow goes dark, tag 'REVERSING STEP-DOWN DC REACHES THE CORE'.
---
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Canonical demag-methods scene: bench demagnetizing coil (energized ring) at left, a long shaft on rollers passing through, axis arrow pointing right; cross-section inset top-right; waveform strip along the bottom. Reveals timed to narration (~10s beat, note fires at 7.6s): dmCoil — coil energizes with AC shimmer and the cross-section inset shows a thin bright skin-ring of field hugging the shaft surface, at 'the A C coil strips surface fields' (~0–3s); dmWithdraw — the shaft slides right out of the coil along the axis arrow, surface glow fading with distance, small tag '2–3 FT BEFORE SWITCH-OFF', at 'walk the part slowly out along the axis' (~3–5.5s); dmCore — the cross-section inset highlights a deep residual glow in the core that the AC skin-ring never reached, at 'deep D C fields' (~5.5–7s); dmSteps — the bottom waveform strip draws reversing step-down DC (polarity-flipping bars stepping down to zero) while the core glow extinguishes step by step, at 'need reversing, step-down direct current' (~7–9.5s).

immersion-setup.brief:
600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Side elevation of an immersion tank. Base art: tank outline with waterline; submerged rectangular steel part resting on supports near the bottom; scanning bridge rail across the top; vertical manipulator rod descending to a cylindrical immersion probe aimed straight down; clearly visible standoff gap between probe face and part top with a dimension bracket. Mono labels: SCANNING BRIDGE, MANIPULATOR, WATER PATH (on the bracket), PART. Used as the title-beat panel (panelLabel 'TANK // PROBE + WATER PATH'). Narration (~8.5s): 'In immersion testing, the probe never touches the part. Sound travels down a water path; the water is the couplant.' Required animatable reveals: imGap at ~2.5s on 'never touches the part' — highlight ring/bracket on the probe-to-part standoff gap with a NO CONTACT tag; imPulse at ~5.0s on 'Sound travels down a water path' — looping pulse wavefront arcs traveling from the probe face down the water column and into the part; imCouplant at ~7.0s on 'the water is the couplant' — water region glows cyan with tag WATER = COUPLANT.
---
Full-immersion tank, side cross-section, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. A wide rectangular tank holds translucent water with a calm surface line. A thick test block (forging) rests submerged on two short support stools, clear of the tank floor. A bridge rail spans the tank top; a vertical manipulator mast reaches down holding a downward-looking cylindrical immersion transducer whose face is underwater with a clear standoff gap above the part — probe and part must visibly NOT touch. A cyan beam column runs from the transducer face straight down through the water into the block (normal incidence, entry face level/perpendicular to the beam). Dashed raster arrows along the bridge suggest automated line-by-line scanning. Required reveals (title-beat narration, ~9.5s): imTank (fade ~0.6s) tank + water as narration opens 'In immersion testing'; imPart (fade ~1.6s) submerged block on stools while narration says 'the probe never touches the part' (~1.5-3.5s); imProbe (fade ~2.8s) bridge, mast, and transducer poised above the part with visible water gap; imBeam (draw ~5.0s) the sound column from probe face through water into the part, syncing 'a column of water carries the sound' (~4-6.5s); imRaster (draw ~7.4s) raster arrows on the bridge, syncing 'water is a consistent couplant' (~7-9.5s).
---
Title panel, 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Side view of an immersion tank: tank walls, waterline across the upper third with faint ripple rings, a carbon-steel plate resting on low supports near the bottom. A vertical manipulator arm descends from top center holding a cylindrical immersion transducer, face submerged, aimed straight down at the plate. Cyan beam cone from the probe face down through the water into the plate, soft entry glow at the front surface. A bright dimension bracket alongside the probe-to-part gap labeled WATER PATH with small up/down adjust arrows. Beat-1 narration (~8.5s): 'In immersion testing, the water between probe and part is not just coupling. It is a setting you control deliberately.' Reveals: imTank at 0.0s = tank + water + plate (under 'In immersion testing'); imProbe at ~1.2s = manipulator + transducer over the part ('the water between probe and part'); imBeam at ~3.6s = beam cone crossing water into the plate with entry glow ('is not just coupling'); imPath at ~5.8s = WATER PATH bracket with pulsing adjust arrows ('a setting you control deliberately'). Keep it a general-purpose immersion establishing scene — intended for reuse by ut1-7-1 and ut1-7-3.
---
Canonical immersion-UT establishing shot (600x340, holographic navy/cyan), side view. Open-top tank with a clear waterline; a flat plate specimen submerged on two support blocks; a scanning bridge spanning the tank carrying a vertical manipulator column that holds a cylindrical immersion transducer aimed straight down at the plate, with a visible water-path gap between probe face and part surface. A beam cone runs from the probe face through the water into the plate. Subtle X-travel arrows on the bridge convey that the probe is scanning. Used as the TITLE-BEAT PANEL while narration (~8.5s) introduces 'Immersion's finale: three ideas... plate waves... two ways to arrange the sound... contact or immersion?' — so it is a calm contextual build, not synced to specific words. Reveals: imsTank (draw, 0.6s) tank outline + waterline; imsPart (fade, 1.4s) submerged plate on blocks; imsProbe (draw, 2.2s) bridge + manipulator + transducer with the water-path gap; imsBeam (draw, 3.2s) beam cone into the part; imsScanArrows (fade, 4.5s) bridge travel arrows. Keep composition airy; right third may be partly covered by title chips in the player. NOTE: this is a shared canonical asset other immersion lessons (ut1-7-1/ut1-7-2) may also reference — keep it generic (no plate-wave tilt, no twin probes).

water-path-timing.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Split layout. Left ~35%: vertical schematic — probe at top, water column labeled WATER PATH P, part of thickness T below it with FRONT SURFACE and BACK WALL edges marked. Right ~65%: A-scan-style baseline/time axis with labeled echo spikes: IP (initial pulse) at far left, FS (front-surface echo), then BW (back-wall echo) with healthy separation in the base state. Diagram beat narration (~9s): 'The pulse bounces in the water, creating a second front surface echo. Too short a path, and it hides the back wall.' Required animatable reveals: wpBounce at ~1.5s on 'The pulse bounces in the water' — animated arrow re-bouncing probe -> front surface -> probe inside the water column on the left schematic; wpMultiple at ~3.5s on 'creating a second front surface echo' — dashed FS2 spike appears on the time axis at twice the water transit time, landing beyond BW in the base state; wpCollide at ~6.0s on 'Too short a path, and it hides the back wall' — the water column visibly shortens and FS2 slides left onto BW, which dims/gets masked beneath FS2 with a red alert flash; hold this state through the on-screen note at 8.0s ('TOO SHORT -> MULTIPLE HIDES THE BACK WALL').

three-scan-views.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. One mid-wall flaw presented in all three UT display formats (mirrors lesson fig ut2-1-2-fig-a). BASE ART (visible from beat start, narration 'Same flaw, three ways'): far left, a small isometric steel plate with a probe on top and a single lens-shaped mid-wall void at half depth, glowing cyan. Three equal display panels fill the remaining width, labeled small-caps A-SCAN / B-SCAN / C-SCAN, revealed left to right in sync with narration (24-word beat, ~9.5s): (1) tsvAScan at ~2.2s on 'A-scan: the waveform at one spot' — amplitude-vs-time trace at one probe position: initial pulse, highlighted mid-screen flaw echo, back-wall echo; thin leader line ties the flaw echo to the void in the plate. (2) tsvBScan at ~4.8s on 'Stacked along a line: the B-scan slice' — cross-section panel, depth vertical vs probe position horizontal, built visibly from 4-5 faint stacked A-scan slats merging into one slice; flaw reads as a short mid-depth streak. (3) tsvCScan at ~7.8s on 'One gated value per point: the C-scan' — plan-view X-Y map (looking down on the part) with a color-coded footprint blob at the flaw location, faint raster path lines, and a tiny gate bracket glyph feeding one value per pixel. Keep the three panels visually parallel so the same flaw clearly reads as waveform, then slice, then map. On-screen note 'ONE FLAW · WAVEFORM · SLICE · MAP' fires at 8.4s — leave footer clearance for it.

ut-data-flow.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan left-to-right block diagram of a computerized UT acquisition chain (mirrors lesson fig ut2-1-2-fig-b). BASE ART (visible from beat start, narration 'From the probe'): probe coupled to a part at far left feeding a PULSER/RECEIVER block, with a small analog RF trace flowing out of it; cyan flow arrows connect all blocks. Reveals in narration order (22-word beat, ~9s): (1) udfDigitize at ~2.4s on 'digitize each waveform' — A/D CONVERTER block: the analog trace passes through and emerges as discrete sample dots / stair-step, with fast sampling-tick marks. (2) udfEncoder at ~4.2s on 'stamp it with encoder position' — encoder wheel icon below the chain stamping an X,Y position tag onto the passing waveform packet. (3) udfGates at ~6.0s on 'gate it in software' — SOFTWARE block: gate bracket over a mini A-scan plus thumbnail B-scan slice and C-scan map being assembled from gated values. (4) udfStore at ~7.4s on 'store everything for re-analysis' — STORAGE archive cylinder at far right receiving full waveforms plus metadata, with a curved replay arrow looping back toward the software block to suggest offline re-gating. On-screen note 'STORED WAVEFORMS CAN BE RE-GATED OFFLINE' fires at 8.0s — keep lower-third clear. Blocks must stay readable at small size.

crank-and-boundary.brief:
Title panel, ambient loop (CONVERGE: rt1-5-1 already requested this name with label 'FULL CABLE // BEHIND WALL'; one drawing must serve both — this lesson labels it 'FULL CABLE // ROPE POSTED'). Scene: planned gamma shot in a fab yard. Radiographer at the crank position behind a masonry wall, full drive cable run to an exposure device, collimated beam aimed at a pipe girth weld and angled toward the ground; rope boundary with trefoil placards at the far radius; small unlabeled survey-meter glyph near the rope. Reveals: crbScene (base scene: radiographer + wall + full cable + device/collimator on weld) at beat start, under 'The levers haven't changed. You have.'; crbRope (rope stanchions and placards trace around the radius) ~2.5 s in, as narration says 'you write the plan'; crbPulse (soft repeating dose-field ripple from the source that dies at the rope, plus slow cable shimmer) looping ambient for the rest of the beat — the ripple stopping AT the rope visualizes 'every dosimeter answers to it'. No numeric text in the panel.
---
Field gamma-radiography safety scene (title panel), 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Layout: right third shows a gamma projector (exposure device) on the ground with a guide tube rising to a collimated exposure head aimed at a pipe girth weld; center has a low concrete-block wall; left has a radiographer figure crouched at a crank handle, connected to the projector by a drive cable that runs visibly at FULL length around the wall; foreground has a rope boundary arc with a trefoil radiation sign. Small mono labels: FULL CABLE near the crank, BOUNDARY near the rope. Narration over this beat (24 words, ~8.5 s): 'Radiography's core skill is exposure control: unshielded, a typical field source spends your whole annual dose limit in about six minutes at one meter.' Reveals: cabScene at 0.8 = projector + guide tube + pipe weld + ground line (scene established on 'Radiography's core skill'); cabBeam at 2.2 = faint narrow collimated beam fan exiting only toward the weld (lands on 'exposure control'); cabCable at 3.6 = draw the drive cable running left at full length (on 'unshielded, a typical field source'); cabFigure at 5.0 = radiographer at the crank behind the concrete wall (on 'annual dose limit'); cabBoundary at 6.6 = draw rope line + trefoil sign (as 'six minutes at one meter' lands). Mood: calm, procedural, distance-and-cover emphasized — the figure is far from the source with structure between.

collimated-boundary.brief: NEW asset for rt2-6-1 beat 3 (~9 s, 24 words). Plan view looking straight down on a fab yard; exposure device + source point center-left with a short crank-cable stub. Reveals in narration order: clbOpen (wide dashed circle labeled '110 m' with a small '2 mR/h' tag at its edge; faint radial rays in all directions from the source) at 'Shot open, the restricted boundary sits near 110 meters' (0–3 s); clbCol (collimator wedge snaps onto the source, omnidirectional rays collapse to one narrow ground-directed cone, off-beam rays dim, a 'rate ÷10 (typ.)' chip beside the collimator) at 'A collimator typically cuts the rate tenfold' (~4 s); clbShrink (dashed 110 m circle collapses to a solid cyan circle labeled '35 m', the 2 mR/h tag riding the shrinking edge) at 'Call it 35.' (~6 s); clbMeter (survey-meter glyph + rope-stanchion icon on the 35 m edge, needle settling on 2 mR/h) at 'The meter sets the rope.' (~8 s, paired with the note 'THE METER SETS THE ROPE — NOT THE MATH' at 8.2 s). Keep circles concentric on the source; radial scale exaggeration is fine.

panoramic-exposure.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Cross-section of a pipe/vessel: a large circle centered in frame, wall rendered as a girth-weld ring. A segmented film band wraps tight around the OUTSIDE of the circumference (label: FILM WRAP). A gamma source capsule sits at the exact geometric center (label: SOURCE ON CENTERLINE) with a continuous 360-degree radial belt of rays fanning from the center to the film band all the way around — this belt is BASE ART, visible from beat start (narration has already established 'source in the middle, film wrapped outside' in the prior beat). Beat narration (~9.5s): 'The trick is dead center: every point sees the same distance, so density runs uniform. Drift off axis — near side dark, far side light.' Required animatable reveals: (1) panRadii at ~2.5s — three equal-length radius arrows from source to film at different clock positions, each tagged SAME SFD, syncs with 'every point sees the same distance'; (2) panDensity at ~4.8s — the film band fills with a single even cyan tone, tag UNIFORM DENSITY, syncs with 'density runs uniform'; (3) panDrift at ~6.3s — a ghost copy of the source displaced off-center with a small displacement arrow, radii now visibly unequal, and the film band re-shades into a gradient: dark wedge on the near side (tag DARK) fading to light on the far side (tag LIGHT), syncs with 'Drift off axis — near side dark, far side light.' Keep centered state (solid) and drifted state (ghost/dashed) visually distinct so both readings coexist. Player overlays the caption note at 8.0s; keep the lower strip clear for it.

film-triage.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. One girth-weld radiograph strip on a light field, three marks triaged live as narration names them (beat ~8.5-9 s: 'Interpretation sorts every indication. Crimp marks live on the film: false. Spatter you verify on the part: nonrelevant. The slag line? Relevant — a discontinuity.'). BASE (visible from start, supports an ambient scan sweep): horizontal film strip with clipped corners and faint ID tag; lighter weld band running across mid-frame; the three marks already faintly present: crescent-shaped crimp mark in the upper-left film area OFF the weld, a cluster of small rounded spatter blobs at the weld toe, and an elongated fine wandering slag line along the weld image. REVEALS (prefix ftr): ftrCrimp (highlight ring on the crescent, ~1.9 s, as 'Crimp marks' is spoken) + ftrCrimpTag (fade tag 'FALSE // LIVES ON THE FILM' with tiny film-sheet glyph, ~2.6 s, on 'false'); ftrSpatter (highlight on the spatter blobs, ~3.9 s, on 'Spatter') + ftrPartInset (fade: small inset sketch of the actual pipe surface showing the same spatter physically on the part with a check mark, ~4.5 s, on 'verify on the part') + ftrSpatterTag (fade tag 'NONRELEVANT // PART FEATURE', ~5.2 s, on 'nonrelevant'); ftrSlag (draw the slag line bright, ~6.2 s, on 'slag line') + ftrSlagPing (ping on it, ~6.5 s) + ftrSlagTag (fade tag 'RELEVANT // EVALUATE', ~7.1 s, on 'Relevant — a discontinuity'). Style cue: false and nonrelevant tags in dim/neutral cyan; relevant tag hot/bright — the only one that moves forward. Renderer drops its own note chip at 8.6 s, so keep the lower-right corner clear.

mt-equipment-classes.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Three equipment bays left-to-right on a shared baseplate, each containing an IDENTICAL small glowing circuit glyph (step-down transformer + rectifier + ammeter block) drawn at three sizes to telegraph 'same circuits, different scale'. Bay 1 PORTABLE (left ~170px): hand-carried AC/DC yoke silhouette plus a small prod power pack with two prod leads; amperage tag '≈1,500 A PACK'. Bay 2 MOBILE (center): wheeled power-pack cart with a thick flexible cable running out and wrapping ~3 turns around a shaft (cable-wrap coil); tag '2,000–6,000 A'. Bay 3 STATIONARY (right): wet horizontal bench — headstock/tailstock clamping a part, coil on rails, bath hose, UV hood canopy; tag 'BENCH + BATH' (no third amperage number — narration does not speak one). Animatable reveals, fired in narration order: eqcCores — the three identical circuit glyphs pulse and link with a dashed line at beat start when narration says 'Same circuits, different scale' (~0.5s); eqcPortable — left bay + its amperage tag brighten at 'portable packs near fifteen hundred amperes' (~2.5s); eqcMobile — center bay + cable wrap brighten at 'mobile units roll two to six thousand to the work' (~4.5s); eqcStationary — right bay (clamps, coil, bath hose, hood) brightens at 'benches add fixtures and bath' (~7s). Renderer overlays the note at 8.0s; keep lower edge clear.

mt-auto-sequence.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan. Automated indexing MT line: conveyor band across the lower third carrying identical small parts through six stations left-to-right: CLAMP (contact heads closing, tiny interlock check icon), BATH (nozzle flooding the part), SHOOT (two pulse bolts labeled '2 × ~0.5 s', bath film visibly draining off while particle dots migrate to a crack), VIEW (dark UV-A hood with lamp), DEMAG (pass-through coil with decaying sine), UNLOAD (accept arrow off the line). A thin dim monitor rail with small gauge icons runs above all stations (ambient only — monitoring is narrated in the NEXT beat, so keep it neutral, never highlighted). Animatable reveals, lighting each station in narration order: asqClamp at 'clamp' (~2s); asqBath at 'flood the part' (~3s); asqShoot at 'fire two half-second shots' (~4s); asqDrain — draining film + mobile particle dots accenting the crack at 'while the draining bath still lets particles move' (~5–6.5s); asqView at 'viewing' (~7s); asqDemag at 'demag' (~7.7s); asqUnload at 'unload' (~8.2s). Renderer overlays the note at 7.6s; keep lower-left clear.

reflector-tilt.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cross-section of a steel butt-weld joint. A planar lack-of-fusion flaw is a short bright line near the weld fusion face; angle-beam probe A sits on the left top surface firing a beam ray into the flaw; a mini A-scan inset top-right shows one tall echo. Beat narration (~9.6 s): 'Tilt a planar flaw ten or fifteen degrees off the beam and its echo can collapse. That's why we scan welds from both sides.' Reveals: tltSquare (initial state, 0 s) — flaw face perpendicular to probe A's beam, bold specular return arrow retracing the beam to probe A, inset echo tall; tltTilt (~1.5 s, on 'ten or fifteen degrees') — flaw face rotates ~15 degrees with a '10–15°' angle label, reflected rays now deflect away from probe A, return arrow fades; tltCollapse (~4.5 s, on 'echo can collapse') — inset A-scan echo shrinks to near baseline leaving a dashed ghost of its former height; tltProbeB (~7.0 s, on 'scan welds from both sides') — second angle-beam probe fades in on the opposite side of the weld, its beam strikes the tilted face near-perpendicular, strong return arrow plus a small tall-echo badge beside probe B. Renderer overlays the note 'TILTED 10–15° → ECHO LOST · SCAN BOTH SIDES' at 9.2 s, so keep the lower band clear.

honeycomb-sandwich.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan cutaway of an adhesive-bonded honeycomb sandwich panel (title-beat panel, ~10s narration). BASE: bottom face sheet full width; hexagonal core cells standing on it; top face sheet peeled back from the right corner to expose the core; thin adhesive bondlines at both skin-to-core junctions with small fillets climbing the cell walls; slim bonded doubler strip on the outer skin at left. REVEALS (id = what it shows @ when vs narration 'Bonded structures trade rivets for adhesive, spreading load across the whole surface. Once cured, the joint is hidden — and nothing directly measures its strength.'): hcsRivets (fade @ ~1.0s) ghosted row of rivet points with tiny stress-riser marks, dimmed/struck through — the joint being replaced ('trade rivets for adhesive'); hcsBond (draw @ ~2.6s) cyan glow sweeping left-to-right along the full faying surface of both bondlines ('spreading load across the whole surface'); hcsClose (fade @ ~5.2s) the peeled top sheet settles closed and the core dims behind it ('the joint is hidden'); hcsTag (fade @ ~7.6s) small mono tag NO DIRECT MEASURE OF STRENGTH near the closed joint ('nothing directly measures its strength'). Include a scan sweep id hcsScan per house style.

bondline-flaws.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan wide cross-section of a metal-to-metal bonded joint (diagram beat, ~9s narration; player note chip lands bottom area at 7.8s — keep bottom-center clear). BASE: lower aluminum skin slab, adhesive bondline as a lighter exaggerated-thickness band, upper doubler slab; three zones left/center/right. REVEALS (vs narration 'Disbond: the interface separates. Void: a pocket inside the adhesive. Kissing bond: surfaces touch, sound crosses — but the grip is gone.'): bfDisbond (draw @ ~0.9s) left zone, crisp open separation gap along the adhesive-to-skin interface, mono tag DISBOND ('the interface separates'); bfVoid (fade @ ~2.9s) center zone, rounded empty pocket fully INSIDE the adhesive band touching neither interface, tag VOID ('a pocket inside the adhesive'); bfKissing (fade @ ~4.8s) right zone, interface drawn as a tight hairline dashed contact with NO gap, tag KISSING BOND ('surfaces touch'); bfWave (draw @ ~6.2s) small pulse arrow passing down through the kissing-bond contact nearly unchanged ('sound crosses'); bfEcho (fade @ ~7.6s) small reflection arrows bouncing back from the disbond gap and the void — syncs with the note AIR GAPS REFLECT · KISSING BONDS PASS SOUND. Include scan sweep id bfScan per house style.

film-loading-bench.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan glassmorphism panel; title-beat ambient loop (~9 s) behind narration: 'A radiograph can be ruined before the first photon. Unprocessed film remembers every insult and prints it, often disguised as a defect.' Scene, slight 3/4 view: darkroom loading bench with an open film cassette (front and back lead screens drawn as two thin bright plates), a closed light-tight film bin behind it, and a pair of clean bare hands lifting one film sheet by its top corners (sheet bows very slightly, never kinked). A safelight cone washes the bench from upper left — render as a muted warm-red glow accent kept subordinate to the navy/cyan palette. Animatable reveal ids: flbScene = bench + cassette + bin base linework, visible at 0 s; flbHands = hands + sheet group fading in ~0.5 s with a slow 2-3 px lift-and-settle loop for the whole beat ('unprocessed film remembers every insult'); flbSafelight = safelight cone with a gentle 3 s opacity pulse loop starting ~1 s; flbGhostMark = small radiograph inset at lower right where a faint crescent and fingerprint ghost flicker in at ~6 s (on 'prints it, often disguised as a defect') then hold at low opacity. Keep lower-left clear for the panelLabel chip 'DARKROOM // SAFELIGHT ON'.

crimp-mark-timing.brief: 600x340 holographic navy/cyan diagram teaching the crimp-mark timing rule; plays under narration (~9 s): 'Bend a sheet and you crimp it: a crescent mark. Kinked before exposure it images light; after exposure, dark. Crimps mimic real flaws.' Top third: a film sheet held at both ends and kinked in the middle, crescent-shaped crease highlighted at the kink. Bottom two-thirds: two side-by-side sequence lanes. Left lane labeled 'KINK, THEN EXPOSE': kink icon then radiation-beam icon, ending in a small radiograph swatch (mid-gray film tone) carrying a LIGHT crescent (lighter than its surround).
